Lord Justice Cotton said: 'If it appeared that the real object of the deed was that the trustees should speculate in investments, even though confined to this particular class, the case would have stood in a very different position. In my opinion there is nothing of that sort. This is not a

<br>

### --- Page 0144 ---

Cuar. II] LIMITED PARTNERSHIP AND TRUST [§ 18a

provision that they shall make a profit by selling and buying again securities of this class, whenever, in their opinion, the turn of the market makes it advisable so to do. The deed is in substance a trust deed, providing how they are to hold as trustees specified securities of a large amount with provisions enabling them in certain events to sell some of the securities, and enabling them when that is done, but only under special circumstances, to reinvest, not to speculate. In my opinion that is not a deed providing for carrying on business within the meaning of the act; it is a deed providing for the holding of trust property with such provisions only as are necessary to enable that to be conveniently done. I am of opinion, therefore, that there is no carrying on business within the meaning of the act.

The power to sell as well as to buy these securities is ordinarily so essential to the effective operation of one of these holding companies that in most instances it will be found that the declaration of trust contains a trading element. If the organization is a partnership, any increase in the value of the securities comprised in the trust fund is a profit to be divided among those entitled to profits; if a trust, it is an accretion to principal and belongs to those who own the corpus of the trust fund.

§ 18a. Limited Partnership Combined with Trust

Previous to 1922 there had been uncertainty as to the practicability of the trust with transferable shares in New York. There were no authoritative decisions on the subject and several peculiarities of New York law raised problems not encountered elsewhere. This uncertainty has now been set at rest by the opinion of the Court of Appeals in Crehan v. Megargel. This case presents a number of important aspects which require discussion.

In Kimball v. Whitney, 233 Mass. 321, 233, 123 N. E. 665, the court did not have to decide whether the Massachusetts Electric Companies was a partnership or pure trust. If it was a partnership, it was a partnership of a peculiar kind. It was not an ordinary business, commercial or trading partnership. Williams v. Milton, 215 Mass. 1, 11, 102 N. E. 355. 1234 N. Y. 67, 136 N. E. 296.

<br>

### --- Page 0145 ---

§ 18a) ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT

It was a Limited Partnership, the special capital of which was raised by a trust with transferable shares declared by the special partner. This trust, a copy of which is printed at length in the appendix, was a pure trust without partnership elements, but the plaintiff sought to hold the certificate holders as the real special partners. The court in commenting upon it, cites as authoritative the case of Williams v. Milton and in this connection says:

“The trust is an unsurmountable barrier raised between them and the partnership and separating them from an interest in its affairs, and it seems to us to be a valid arrangement and to come within the principles which have been approved both by eminent text writers and the decisions of various jurisdictions in the case of so-called commercial or business trusts as substitutes for business corporations. (Burdick on Partnerships (3d Ed.), p. 43, etc.; Thompson on Business Trusts, etc. p. 28; Rhode Island Trust Co. v. Copeland, 39 R. I. 193; Williams v. Milton, 215 Mass. 1; Home Lumber Co. v. Hopkins, (Kansas Sup. Ct.) 190 Pac. Rep. 601).”

The effort of the plaintiff in that case was to hold the defendant certificate holders to partnership liability. Although the complication to be referred to presently might make the case distinguishable on its facts from a case where a creditor of a trust of the Crocker v. Malley type sought to impose partnership liability on the shareholders, it seems plain from the above quoted language of the court that the distinction between trusts that are also partnerships and business trusts without partnership elements is law in New York.

The chief peculiarity of the instrument construed in Crehan v. Megargel, however, is the combination of limited partnership and trust with transferable shares, which it embodied. Large possibilities of revitalizing our neglected Limited Partnership Acts result from the decision. The scheme there evolved was a limited partnership with transferable shares adding to the statutory protection afforded the limited partner the further protection of the doctrines.

<br>

### --- Page 0146 ---

Cuap. IT] LIMITED PARTNERSHIP AND TRUST (§ 18a

of trusts with transferable shares. In brief, a limited partnership was formed under the provisions of the New York statute, which resembles those of almost all the other states. The special partner named in the Articles of Partnership was not, however, the real contributor of the special capital formed and may well have been an entirely unsubstantial person, proof against execution. He in turn, however, executed a declaration of trust with transferable shares of the type employed in Crocker v. Malley. Purchasers of the trust certificates entrusted him with their cash subscriptions, which he was to turn over in blocks of one hundred thousand dollars each to the firm as his own contribution of special capital. It was specified in the instruments that the certificate-holders as such had rights only against the trustee and not against the firm, yet provision had to be made for continuity of the enterprise in case of death and the ingenious clauses devised for the purpose were deemed by the court not to involve so direct a relationship between the shareholders and the firm as to countervail the other clauses which made the trustee the sole intermediary. The plaintiff was a firm creditor, who, relying upon certain violations of the limited partnership act, sought to collect from the certificate-holders on the theory that they were the real contributors of the special capital, and therefore the real special partners, and because of the failure to comply with the statutory requisites to complete immunity of special partners, had become liable as general partners.

The first statute permitting limited partnerships in either England or the United States was adopted one hundred years ago in New York. It has since been copied in the Codes of almost all the States and in England. A characteristic of these statutes has been a public record of the name of the special partner and the amount of his contribution of capital, an affidavit of the general partner that the special capital had been actually and in good faith paid in cash, the exemption of the special partner from personal liability for debts of the firm so long as the certificate and affidavit.

<br>

### --- Page 0147 ---

§ 18a ] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [ Chap. II

were duly filed and the statute otherwise complied with, but his liability as a general partner, if compliance was evaded or neglected. Previous to Crehan v. Megargel it had been generally supposed that these features of the statute made impossible a limited partnership with transferable shares. Since modern business conditions made transferable shares essential in most large enterprises, the field of the limited partnership was narrowed and its possibilities neglected.

The original Limited Partnership Act of New York was taken almost bodily from the Commercial Code of France. That Code recognized two varieties of this form of business organization, the société en commandité and the société en commandité par actions. The former is the one which New York adopted. The latter was a partnership with transferable shares representing the special capital which apparently is identical with that devised by Megargel & Company with the exception of the intervention of the trustee. Between 1817 and 1830 there was a rapid increase in France in the number of limited partnerships with transferable shares. It was also a period of feverish speculation. Abuse of that form of business organization, free from the public restraints of incorporation, are declared by an early commentator to have become a public scandal. The public was unable to distinguish such a partnership from a corporation and it was proposed to forbid them by statute. An extended discussion of the history of the act by a contemporary in a decision of the Surrogate’s court had declared that the legislators deliberately chose to exclude the latter form of organization, when they framed the Limited Partnership Act.

Des Sociétés Commerciales, M. Delangle (Paris, 1843), Vol. 2, Art. 38, Par. 490.
Ames v. Downing, 1 Bradf. 321, 329.

<br>

### --- Page 0148 ---

Chap. II] LIMITED PARTNERSHIP AND TRUST (§ 18a

ley, in response to a resolution of the Assembly, gave an opinion that a Limited Partnership with transferable shares would be unconstitutional.®

The present decision,* however, brushes away the doubts which were suggested by the history of the statute as well as those which arose from previous declarations of public policy by individual judges, which are not, however, embodied in authoritative opinions of the Court of Appeals. A limited partnership with transferable shares is now held permissible where it contains the double insulation against

5 Assembly Documents, Vol. 4, 1837.

*Crehan v. Megargel, 234 N. Y. 67, 136 N. E. 296. It is curious that, after all these years, a second organisation, very similar to that in the New York case, should have come into court in Chicago at about the same time. The opinion suggests that under the form of Limited Partnership Act which was before the New York Court it would have held the trust certificate holders liable but this case arose under the Uniform Limited Partnership Act, which imposes less severe penalties and protects those who act in good faith and surrender their profits on discovering that the organization fails to conform to the statute. In re Marcuse & Co., 281 Fed. 928 (C. C. A.—Ill.) See article by Calvert Magruder on Labor Copartnership in Industry, 35 Harv. Law Rev. 920.

7 Hibbs v. Brown, 190 N. Y. 167, 192 involved the validity of a stipulation in bonds eliminating personal liability of shareholders in the Adams Express Company, a joint stock association. In his opinion, Edward T. Bartlett, J. said:

“It is the obvious policy of the state to maintain this distinction, to wit: If men desire to embark in great business ventures, practically exempt from governmental control, they must do so subject to the joint and several liability to pay the debts thereby incurred. If they wish to be freed from that liability, they can secure the same result by forming a corporation and submitting to governmental visitation and control and to various statutory restraints by virtue of which the rights of creditors are protected. Public policy clearly requires that such vast transactions shall be carried on either under corporate restraint or the rigorous common law rule of joint and several liability for debts.”

Cullen, C. J., concurring, said:

‘any provision in the contract of a joint stock association relieving the shareholders from liability is contrary to public policy and void, since it would enable the shareholders of such association to obtain all the immunities of stockholders in a corporation without incorporating and subjecting themselves to the restrictions and regulations imposed by law on corporations.”

Werner, J. said

“The maker of the bond is an association having a business name, which it used in making them, having shares of capital stock, indefinite succession, and a number of other characteristics which the public associates exclusively with corporations. These considerations lend great force to the suggestion that as a matter of public policy the exemption clause referred to should be treated as nugatory.” See also Ruger, C. J. in Hackett v. Stanley, 115 N. Y. 625, 629. See McCamey v. Hollister Oil Co., 241 S. W. 689 (Tex. Civ. App.)

<br>

### --- Page 0149 ---

§ 18a] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT (Chap. I

individual liability of the contributors of capital provided by the Limited Partnership Act and the trust with transferable shares.

The next important effect of this decision is the application to trusts with transferable shares of the doctrine of “collateral attack” upon incorporation. This subject is discussed more fully in another section of this book.? For the purposes of this section it is sufficient to say that in New York, at any rate, a creditor of a business organized along the lines above described cannot sue the purchasers of the trust certificates because of any failure of the trust to comply with the New York statutes forbidding restraints upon alienation. Indeed, the language of the Court of Appeals, as well as that of the Appellate Division,® implies that no creditor of any trust with transferable shares may dispute its validity.

§ 19. Perpetuities and Restraints upon Alienation

It has been earnestly contended that the trust deeds by which these organizations are created violate the rule against perpetuities and the rule forbidding restraints upon alienation unless limited to twenty-one years after a life or lives in being at the creation of the trust. In a leading case the court said:

“Is the trust void as creating a perpetuity, or imposing an illegal restraint upon alienation? The right of a shareholder to convey his own shares, or interests, under the trust is in no way restricted, but it is contended that illegality is found in the circumstance that no sale of the corpus of the trust, and no termination of the trust, will necessarily occur within the period of a life or lives in being at the time of the creation of the trust and twenty-one years, and that the provision that the certificate-holders shall not have partition of the land, and can compel its sale only by a three-fourths vote, works an illegal restraint upon alienation.

‘The trustees take the legal title to allow the association

8 § 43a,

*Crehan v. Bailey, 192 N. Y. S. 290, 199.

<br>

### --- Page 0150 ---

Cuar. II] PERPETUITIES AND RESTRAINTS [§19

through its directors to manage the land and to enjoy its rents and income, and to sell the land free of trusts at the will of the association, with a further provision for the termination of the trust by vote of the association at any time after July 1, 1895. Leases for more than five years, and sales, can be made only in accordance with an affirmative vote of three-fourths of the shares, and a like vote is necessary to terminate the trust. The substance of the situation is that the shareholders for the time being have the whole equitable estate in the corpus of the trust, and can at all times sell and transfer their equitable estates at their own pleasure; and the trustees hold the legal title in fee simple in trust to do with the land whatever may be required by the owners of the equitable estate, which owners have full capacity, at all times and at their own option, to require a sale of the land discharged of the trust, or the immediate termination of the trust after a period of five years and a few days, the owners of the equitable estate being a voluntary association, the beneficial interests in which are represented by shares, and the association acting by vote of the shareholders. That the directions of the association to the trustees are to be given by three-fourths votes, rather than by majority votes, is immaterial, since it cannot be said that one is more improbable than the other: either is a reasonable way of declaring the will of the association, and there is no provision that a vote to sell or to end the trust must be passed within any stated period, or at all.

“Such a trust for the convenience of an unincorporated association in renting and selling the land, under which the land is held for no other purpose, and where the income is not accumulated but is distributed as it accrues, and where the land is to be sold free of trusts at the will of the association, and where the whole equitable interest in the trust is at every moment vested absolutely in those who at that moment are shareholders, and never can become vested in any other persons save by act of the absolute owners or by operation of law upon their property, and not by force of any limitation contained in the deed of trust, the equitable

<br>

### --- Page 0151 ---

§19] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [Chap. II

interests so vested being also constantly vendible by their several owners without let or hindrance, as well as subject to their debts and passing like other property upon death by virtue not of the deed of trust but of the general laws governing the disposition of the property of decedents, withdraws no property from commerce, and is not within the reason of what is called the rule against perpetuities. The trust involves no future limitations, no restraint upon alienation, and no accumulation either of income or of principal. The provisions by which the trust fund may be at some time held for the benefit of persons not shareholders at its inception, and who may become such at a period more remote than that allowed by the rule, are not future limitations made by the trust deed in the sense in which the word 'limitation' is used in speaking of the operation of the rule. If there shall ever be a shareholder other than those in whom the whole equitable estate was absolutely vested at the inception of the trust, that shareholder will not take his interest by virtue of a limitation in the trust deed, but because of his succession by virtue of the general principles of law to the property of the original shareholder. The new shareholder, with reference to the rule, is in the same situation as a person who, after the expiration of all lives which were in being when a fee or an estate tail was created, and of a further period of twenty-one years, takes the fee by the operation of the law which makes property vendible by or descendible from the owner, and not by virtue of a limitation in the instrument which created the fee. The entire ownership is never for a moment uncertain, nor unvested, and at every moment each owner can freely dispose of his property, and at each moment it can be transferred to his creditor by the ordinary process of the law, and at each moment the trust can be terminated at the will of the owners of the equitable interest."

Although this decision was by only a majority of the

1 Howe v. Morse, 174 Mass. 491, 55 N. E. 213. (The report of this case contains the full text of a real estate trust deed, though not of the most approved type.)

<br>

### --- Page 0152 ---

Chap. II] PERPETUITIES AND RESTRAINTS [§19

court, it has been frequently cited since in Massachusetts without criticism and may be assumed to be the law in that State. In the only other decision on the point in which a business enterprise was concerned, the Illinois court said: "Where there are persons in being at the creation of an estate capable of conveying an immediate and absolute estate in fee in possession there is no suspension of the power of alienation and no question of perpetuities can arise."

It has been contended that these trusts, unless limited in duration to the period permitted by the Rule against Perpetuities, are illegal as restraints upon alienation, because of the provision usually inserted that the shareholders shall have no right to demand partition or other division of their respective shares before the termination of the trust in the manner provided therein, which, without an express limitation of duration to a life or lives in being and twenty-one years thereafter, might exceed that period. There are several English cases relating to non-profit associations where the trust was held invalid because of restrictions on the right to wind up the association.

A gift by will for the benefit of the Penzance Library was held void because the library was established for the use of subscribers only and one of the rules provided that it should not be broken up as long as ten subscribers remained.* A bequest to the Tunstall Atheneum and Mechanics Institution was held void for perpetuity, the institution being a voluntary association and one of the rules providing that it should not be dissolved without the consent of nine-tenths of the members present at a meeting.

* Hart v. Seymour, 147 Ill. 598, 35 N. E. 246 (real estate development association). Acc. Gray, Perpetuities 3d ed., § 509. See § 37, note 1.

*Carne v. Long, 2 D. F. & J. 7; In re Dutton, 4 Ex. D. 54; see Carrier v. Price (1891), 3 Ch. 159, 169.

<br>

### --- Page 0153 ---

19] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT (Chap. II

the members for the time being. These cases, there being no public charity, were simply private trusts and the gifts were bad because the members for the time being did not have power to alienate the estate." This court further said: "We express no opinion upon the contention that the interests of the shareholders are real estate and that an agreement not to make partition may be open to objection under the law as to perpetuities and restraints upon alienation. These are matters which upon any theory cannot make the whole trust illegal." That is, the court did not decide whether or not the provision depriving the shareholder of the right to partition might be declared void. As the case before them was a bill to enforce the right to dissolution regardless of this stipulation, it is hard to see why the court did not also decide that question in entering its decree.

Until the question is decided in other jurisdictions, however, it is safer to limit the duration of the trust to the period permitted by the Rule against Perpetuities, and this seems to be the usual practice. In our kaleidoscopic business life the enterprise will probably be reorganized well within the time limited. The limitation usually adopted is twenty years after the death of the survivor of certain persons named, who are usually the children of trustees or prominent shareholders. Since there might be inconvenience in tracing the lives of these individuals not officially connected with the trust if a question as to the proper time for termination of the trust should ever arise, a more satisfactory form would seem to be that employed in the Appendix of Forms, infra, limiting the trust on the death of the last survivor of the trustees named "and such other persons now living as shall hereafter become trustees of these presents before any person or persons not now living shall have become the only trustee or trustees thereof or the offices of trustees thereof shall have become entirely vacant whichever of the said periods shall first expire and at the expiration of the time so limited the said trusts shall terminate." It is important in using this form to recall that it must be so phrased that no interval could exist when all the present

<br>

### --- Page 0154 ---

Chap. III PERPETUITIES AND RESTRAINTS [§19

trustees are dead and none of their successors then in office were living at the date of the creation of the trust though subsequent trustees might be men who were living when the trust was created. Such a limitation has been held void for uncertainty.8

The New York law relating to perpetuities and restraints upon alienation is very different from that of most of the States. Its provisions, however, have been imitated in several of the so-called code States. Section 11 of the Personal Property Law makes illegal any "suspension of the absolute ownership" of personal property for a period not measured by two lives in being. There have been numerous decisions which have stated without qualification that any trust for a term of years is illegal and void.* There are corresponding provisions in the Real Property Law prohibiting "the suspension of the power of alienation." Most of the cases in which decisions have been rendered, however, were cases relating to the validity of wills. The doubt as to whether these doctrines applied to business trusts has been a serious obstacle to the practical use of the trust with transferable shares in New York. In most States any danger of conflict with such doctrines is avoided by making the maximum period of duration of the trust one measured by the lives of a number of young and healthy children. However ridiculous this sort of stipulation may seem to a business man, it has been the only safe course in case of doubt. New York, however, distinctly limits the number of lives that may be used to two. The use of only two names would ordinarily make the danger of interruption of continuity of the enterprise too great, for continuity is an essential of any modern large-scale business. The Court of Appeals in the case of Crehan v. Megargel* had an opportunity to clear away the doubts that had existed on

8 Ex parte Exmouth, 23 Ch. D. 158, 163.

* Everitt v. Everitt, 29 N. Y. 39, 71; Smith v. Edwards, 88 N. Y. 92, 102, 109; Underwood v. Curtis, 127 N. Y. 523, 537; Kalish v. Kalish, 166 N. Y. 368, 373; Matter of Hitchcock, 222 N. Y. 57, 71; Re Snyder’s Estate, 21 N. Y.S., 430, 435; Woolley v. Hutchins, 114 Misc., 11.

§ 42.
234 N. Y. 67, 136 N. E. 296.

<br>

### --- Page 0155 ---

§19) ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT (Chap. II)

this point. It is unfortunate that a clear cut decision was
not forthcoming instead of resort to the doubtful doctrine of collateral attack.

The opinion of the Appellate Division in this case was
explicit, though not necessary to its decision. Justice
Greenbaum said "The respondent also contends that the
trust indenture is invalid because it offends the rule against
perpetuities. We are of opinion that the rule has no application to such a transaction as this." The Court of
Appeals merely said: "We believe that there may be various
answers to this contention, but we shall only consider
three of them." In view of the language of the court below
this obviously implies approval of the contention of the
defendants that the New York rule against suspension of
alienation in effect applies only to what are known as spendthrift trusts created by a testator or someone other than
the trustee or the beneficiaries and does not apply to trusts
the creators of which are also the beneficiaries. In other
words the statute held to permit creditors to reach assets
which a person puts in trust for himself is held to make the
interest of the beneficiary assignable, and under other Code
provisions it is only non-assignable beneficial interests (i. e.
spendthrift trusts) which "suspend the absolute ownership" and so encounter the prohibition of the New York
rule. It is perhaps fair to say that one who buys or subscribes for a certificate in a trust with transferable shares is the creator of this trust within the meaning of the decision above cited, at least so far as his share is concerned
and of course he is not seeking to place his property beyond
the reach of his creditors.

It is believed that this decision will go far to convince
the profession that a business trust for a term of years is
valid in New York. If doubt remains, however, the ingenious provision for continuity of the enterprise on the

* See § 43a.

10 Crehan v. Bailey, 192 N. Y. 8. 290, 299.

11 Schenck v. Barnes, 156 N. Y. 316; Newton. Hunt, 134 App. Div. Pag af. 201 N. Y. 599; Hoskin v. Long Island Co., 139 App. Div. 258, affd. 588; Hammerstein v. Equitable Trust Co., 156 App. Div. 644.

<br>

### --- Page 0156 ---

Chap. II] MEMBERSHIP IN ASSOCIATIONS [§ 20

death of the original trustee set forth in the indenture of trust in Crehan v. Megargel, which was emphatically approved by the Court of Appeals as not amounting to a substantial continuation of the trust beyond the life of the first trustee, while in the present form adapted only to the rather peculiar organization then in question, will doubtless suggest to draftsmen methods of preserving for all practical purposes the essential continuity of the enterprise which should be equally acceptable to the Court of Appeals of New York.

§ 20. Membership in Informal Associations for Profit

One who signs the constitution of an informal association for profit becomes a member from the date of signature.1 A subscription to stock and payment of an assessment was held sufficient evidence of membership in a New York joint stock association to hold the defendant as a partner, though he did not sign the articles of association, never attended any meetings and had no knowledge of the business of the association and the facts that the plaintiff when the debt accrued did not know the defendant was a partner or that the association never issued certificates of stock were not material.2 New members were added to a joint stock association formed to publish a newspaper by the trustees signing an agreement to hold in trust for them as well as for the others.3 In general, membership in an association is a question of fact for the jury.4 A wife of a shareholder, who herself held stock, was

1 Though his name was not voted on till later. Beaman v. Whitney, 20 Me. 413, 420. But signing a book and paying a dollar for membership in a cooperative store did not make defendants partners where there were several blank pages between the constitution and list of signatures without evidence of intent to subscribe to articles of partnership. Moore v. May, 117 Wis. 192, 94 N. W. 45.

Patrons of a cheese factory operated by a voluntary association do not necessarily become members of the association and therefore partners. Coolidge v. Taylor, 85 Vt. 39, 80 Atl. 1038, 1045.

2 B. & A. R.R. v. Pearson, 128 Mass. 445, 449.

2 Holt v. Blake, 47 Me. 62.

“Subscribers to shares in an express company who had paid assessments were held partners and also liable for its debts. Frost v. Walker, 60 Me. 468,

<br>

### --- Page 0157 ---

§ 20. ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT (Chap. II)

held not liable for debts of the association because a wife cannot be a partner with her husband. Where no rule or by-law of an unincorporated association requires its stock to be transferred on its books, legal title to the share passes on transfer and delivery of the certificate by the person to whom it was issued. It has been held that a holder of trust certificates which had been lost and the rights under which had not been enforced for sixty years would be barred by the statute of limitations and laches as against a successor corporation, but since the certificates in this case were finally held to be certificates for shares in a joint stock company which had subsequently been incorporated by act of Congress the court held that the corporation could not by vote providing for exchange of certificates cause a forfeiture of rights of shareholders.

§ 21. Property Rights in Associations for Profit

The fact that the shareholder in an association organized under a deed of trust is a cestui que trust as well as a member of an association has caused doubt as to the nature of the shareholder’s interest in the assets of the association. Uncertainty as to the line of distinction between pure trusts and partnerships has added to the confusion.

In the law of partnership a distinction has developed between the title to firm personalty and the title to firm realty. It is now well settled that the legal title to firm personalty is in the firm as an aggregate, that is, an imaginary entity distinct from the individual partners, and that the only right of the individual partners with respect to the firm personalty is a right to an accounting and a share in profits; contra, it has been said that subscribing is merely a declaration of intention to become a partner. Appeal of Hedge, 63 Pa. St. 273, 277; see also Galveston City Co. v. Scott, 42 Tex. 535, 553.

Shareholders of a joint stock company are liable as partners. If the company has engaged in the business for which it was formed, each subscriber for shares with the intent to share profits and each contributor to the funds is liable as a partner. So is one who takes part in meetings or acts as manager. Hunnewell v. Willow Springs Co., 53 Mo. App. 245, 248. 

Norwood v. Francis, 25 App. D.C. 463, 476.

Condit v. Galveston City Co., 186 S.W. 395, 404 (Tex. Civ. App.). 

Yeaman v. Galveston City Co., 173 S.W. 489 (Tex. Civ. App.).

<br>

### --- Page 0158 ---

Cuap. IT] ’ PROPERTY RIGHTS [§ 21

the net assets after payment of debts. This right is enforceable only in equity because only in that court can an accounting be effectually compelled.

When realty is conveyed to all the partners by their individual names, it is equally well settled that the legal title vests in these individual partners. If they own equal shares in the firm capital, no conflict between their partnership rights and their rights as tenants in common of real property can arise. Upon the liquidation of the firm its realty must be applied so far as necessary to payment of firm debts. The distinction in the common-law rules of descent between real and personal property has, however, proved troublesome when applied to the assets of a commercial business. In order to provide for the application of firm realty to the payment of firm debts upon liquidation on the death of a partner, the English courts resorted to the fiction of equitable conversion and pretended that the realty was personalty for all purposes of descent. The courts in the United States, as a rule, have not followed the English cases to the full extent, but have held that, so far as needed to pay firm debts, the real estate should be treated as personalty; but that so much of the land, or its proceeds on sale, as was not needed for that purpose, should go to the heirs and not to the executor.

When legal title to the firm realty is not vested in the partners as individuals in the same proportions in which they will share in the capital of the firm on liquidation, it has been held that the holders of the legal title are trustees.

1 Pratt v. McGuinness, 173 Mass. 170, 172, 53 N. E. 380. 
2 Son v. Hunt, 35 Minn. 357, 29 N. W. 2; Holmes v. Jarrett, 7 Heisk (Tenn.) 
3 Darby v. Darby, 3 Drew. 495. 
4 Shearer v. Shearer, 98 Mass. 107, 115.

In Pennsylvania the interest of a shareholder in land is treated as personalty. Joint stock company to deal in real estate. Land is personal property belonging to the company collectively. Shareholder has only a contingent interest in profits and his attaching creditor can get no more. Kramer v. Arthurs, 7 Pa. St. 165, 172.

Proceeds of mining lands declared as a dividend was held income as between life tenant and remainderman. Shareholder’s interest in property said to be only a right to an accounting. Oliver’s Estate, 136 Pa. St. 43, 59, 20 Atl. 527.

<br>

### --- Page 0159 ---

§21) ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT

Whether they hold as trustees for the firm as an entity or as trustees for the individual partners has never been distinctly decided. The Supreme Court of the United States, in disposing of a levy made at law on a judgment against an individual partner, used language consistent only with a decision that the trust is for the firm and not the individual partners. This firm was in fact an association and the title to the realty was held by trustees under an express trust. After stating that the remedy of the purchaser at the execution sale was to go into equity for an account, Judge Nelson said, "The fact that the property in this case consists of real estate does not change the principles of law governing the ultimate rights and interests concerned. The real property belonging to the partnership is treated in equity as part of the partnership fund and is disposed of and distributed the same as the personal estate. In this case the legal title is in the trustees who are bound to account to the stockholders, the cestui que trusts, according to their respective shares after all debts of the association have been discharged." * If the trust is for the firm, then the beneficial interest in the real estate would pass with the rest of the personalty. It seems probable, however, that the courts have not intended to make a distinction between the descent of firm realty where the legal title happens to be in the proper proportions and firm realty which by intent or accident has not been conveyed so as to vest legal title in the proper ultimate proportions. It is probable, therefore, that, if the question were directly presented, it would be held that the trust is for the benefit of the individual partners. This view is strengthened by the fact that this trust is enforceable only upon liquidation, when the rights of partners in other respects are severed.

In the case of a pure trust it is well settled that the entire legal title is vested exclusively in the trustee and that the rights of the beneficiaries are enforceable only in equity.

* Clagett v. Kilbourne, 1 Black (U. S.) 346, 349, 17 L. ed. 494. See Owens v. Collins, 23 Ala. 837, 842; Merchant v. Smith-Powers Co., 91 Ore. 442, 178 Pac. 939.

<br>

### --- Page 0160 ---

Cuar. II] PROPERTY RIGHTS [§ 21

It has seldom been of importance in litigation to determine the exact nature of the interest of the cestui que trust in the trust res, but learned scholars have found this a fertile topic for speculation.* The controversy is whether the beneficiary is the owner of an equitable estate or title in the trust res or whether he owns merely a chose in action against the trustee. It is impossible to reconcile with either view all of the decided cases. The historical reasons which caused the development of the distinction in partnership between the partner’s right in firm personalty and his right in firm realty did not affect the beneficiary in a pure trust, because the nature of the trust requires the vesting of the entire legal title in the trustee with respect to both classes of property. If the beneficiary, therefore, has an equitable property right, he may have it in both personalty and realty. The clear tendency of the modern cases with respect to transmission of the beneficiary’s interest and taxation of it is to hold that the beneficiary has an ownership in the trust res analogous to legal ownership and that this applies both to personalty and realty.’

Until the organization of modern trusts with transferable

The two sides of the controversy are ably restated in articles by Professor Austin W. Scott, 17 Columbia Law Rev. 269 and Professor Harlan F. Stone, 17 Columbia Law Review 467. The net result indicates that it is impossible to square all the decided cases with either theory. The classic theory sustained by Stone seems most in harmony with the historical attitude of courts of equity, but the innumerable dicta of modern decisions seem to have definitely committed most of our courts to the conception that the beneficiary has an estate analogous to the corresponding legal estate. This means that courts will have to be astute in applying the doctrine to trusts with transferable shares owning real estate to avoid undesirable consequences. This has so far been done successfully by Loring, J., by means of a highly artificial development of the law of equitable conversion. The other way would be much simpler. This important class of cases merits consideration in the search for a consistent theory of trusts.

Freedman’s Co. v. Earle, 110 U. S. 710, 28 L. ed. 301. See Haskell v. Haskell, 234 Mass. 442, 125 N. E. 601. In a petition for leave to foreclose a mortgage under the Soldiers’ and Sailors’ Civil Relief Act it appeared that the mortgagors were trustees under a declaration of trust for numerous shareholders, some of whom were in military service. The trustees claimed the shareholders were owners within the meaning of the act. The court assumed for the purposes of this case that this was correct without deciding whether their interests were legal or equitable. John Hancock Co. v. Lester, 234 Mass. 559, 562, 125 N. E. 594.

<br>

### --- Page 0161 ---

§ 21] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [ Chap. II

shares, many of which are necessarily also associations, no occasion for the coordination of the law of trusts and of partnership has arisen and each has developed in its own way. The courts have definitely tried to eliminate the principles of trusts from their explanations of their decisions in cases on partnership, preferring to rest them upon an original basis representing the custom of merchants. For example, the House of Lords held that the right of the partner is not the same as that of a beneficiary in a trust, although it is enforceable only in equity and through the process of an accounting. The procedure employed by the beneficiary to protect his rights is the same. The adjunct to the partnership rule that a partner owns nothing until the debts are paid is really in practice nothing more than the application of the same sensible principles that a court of equity would apply in ascertaining the net value of the beneficiary’s share in a trust estate. It is equally unfortunate from the standpoint of simplification of the law that a partner’s interest in firm realty should not have been treated in the same way as his interest in personalty, as merely a right of action for an accounting, the unyielding laws of real property being satisfied by the creation of a trust in the individual partners in whom legal title has been vested for the benefit not of the individual partners, but of the firm. If these incongruities were eliminated, the ascertainment of the nature of the interest of a certificate holder in a trust with transferable shares would be very simple whether that trust were also a partnership or not.

The Uniform Partnership Act is somewhat obscure in its disposition of these problems. It eliminates in substance the distinction between the title to real and the title to personal property by creating a new form of estate known as tenancy in partnership, which, while declaring the partners co-owners as individuals, has so qualified their ownership that in substance their only right is a claim against the firm.®

® Knox v. Gye, 5 H. L. Cas. 656.

* Uniform Partnership Act. Sec. 25.

<br>

### --- Page 0162 ---

Chap. II] PROPERTY RIGHTS [§21

property of a partnership, the act more than once speaks of the 'equitable interest of the partnership' in the property, one of which references occurs in the clause dealing with property expressly held in trust for the partnership. It would seem that in substance the commissioners have done what is here suggested. In the process of practical enforcement of property rights, however, there is today, even in the absence of a statute, no real distinction between partner and beneficiary. The problem arises not in the effective conduct of the business of the organization, but in extraneous circumstances such as the application of probate and property rules upon the death of a certificate holder. Even here the distinctions are so few and so slight that it is a pity that they cannot wholly be eliminated.

In modern law, a partner's interest in personalty, being a chose in action, passes on his death to his executor. His interest in realty in America descends to his heir, subject to the application of so much of the realty or its proceeds as is necessary to the payment of firm debts. In England even the interest in realty vests in the executor by means of the application of the doctrine of equitable conversion. The interest of the beneficiary of a trust of personalty passes to the executor. His interest in realty passes to the heir. A widow cannot claim dower in partnership realty to the exclusion of firm creditors nor can she claim dower in the interest in realty of a beneficiary.

In a case relating to a real estate trust formed in Massachusetts to deal in land in Iowa, the widow of a shareholder claimed dower in land conveyed by the trustee. The court said: 'The enterprise was a partnership the object of which was to buy and sell real estate; and the

Freeman v. Freeman, 136 Mass. 260.

11 Wilcox v. Wilcox, 13 Allen 252, 253.

12 Darby v. Darby, 3 Drew. 495.

See Freedman's Co. v. Earle, 110 U. S. 710, 713; 28 L. ed. 301; King's Attorney v. Sands, Freem. Ch. Cas. 129.

Reardon v. Reardon, 192 Mass. 448, 451, 78 N. E. 430.

Dawson v. Parsons, 31 N. Y. 8, 78, 80; Dyer v. Clark, 5 Met. 562; Shearer v. Shearer, 98 Mass. 107.

Read v. Whitney, 7 Gray 533, 536.

<br>

### --- Page 0163 ---

ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT

interest of the individual members of the partnership was the proceeds of the sale of the land. The written contract expressly provides that the trustee shall be invested with the legal title of the land purchased and the same shall be sold and conveyed by him and a distribution of receipts from the land sales shall from time to time be made to the members of the association in proportion to their respective interests. It was not contemplated that there should at any time be any partition or division of the lands among the members of the partnership, but the contract plainly provides that the lands shall be sold by the trustee and the proceeds divided among the several partners. Being partnership land, it must be treated as personal assets, not only so far as the rights of creditors of the partnership are involved, but, so far as necessary, for the purpose of carrying out the provisions of the partnership contract. It is plain to be seen that if the claim of plaintiff be well founded, a husband could not become a member of a partnership of this character without associating his wife with him as a member of the firm. It is not claimed that the contract of partnership is void. Contracts of partnership for buying and selling real estate as a business are as valid and binding upon the parties as any other legal contracts. The parties to this contract expressly provided that the title to the land should be held by a trustee and that he should sell and convey a clear and absolute title. The contract itself refutes the idea that persons who paid their money in aid of the enterprise became seized of any estate in the land. Their relation to the enterprise was very much like the relation of stockholders in a corporation to the property of the corporation. Hence the widow’s claim was dismissed.

The assignee of a shareholder will take his share subject

Mallory v. Russell, 71 Ia. 63, 66, 32 N. W. 102. See Butterfield v. Beardsley, 28 Mich. 412, 425 (proceeds of oil lands); Horner v. Meyers, 29 Ohio Weekly Law Bulletin 403 (petition for partition of land refused); Dillworth v. Ackley, 1 Walk. 180 (Pa.) (bank); Brobst v. Marty, 162 Wis. 296, 156 N. W. 195. (Sale of a cheese factory ordered since it could not be partitioned.) A levy on shares in a real estate association gave no lien on the real estate. The interest of the shareholder was personalty. Pittsburg Wagon Works Estate, 204 Pa. St. 432, 434, 54 Atl. 311.

<br>

### --- Page 0164 ---

Chap. II. PROPERTY RIGHTS [§ 21

to the equity of his co-partners to have the property applied in payment of firm debts. A pledgee of a certificate for shares gets rights superior to a creditor who attaches the undivided interest of the shareholder in the property of the association. A pledgee has been described as an equitable owner of the shares which stood in the name of the pledgor on the books of the association. It has been held that a New York court has jurisdiction by substituted service to foreclose a pledge of shares in a joint stock association which shares are located in New York though all the property of the association is in Arkansas and that the purchaser at the sale is entitled in the United States court for Arkansas to a decree ordering transfer of the certificates and issue of new ones. The articles of agreement contained stipulations as to the shares investing them with most of the attributes of shares in corporations. The court said that these agreements between the partners distinguished the case from ordinary partnerships and estopped the shareholder from contending that the certificates did not represent the interests of the holders in the partnership property or that a transfer by judicial sale did not convey those interests; that these certificates had every attribute that renders certificates for shares in corporations thus transferable and their presence in any state like the presence of certificates for shares in corporations justified a judicial sale there.21 Under a statute authorizing an executor to deliver property in specie instead of selling it, shares in a New York joint stock association may be delivered.22 As between life tenant and remainderman

48 Sale of share in a mine. Duryea v. Burt, 28 Cal. 569, 577, 586.
1 Citizens’ Bank v. Bank of Commerce, 80 Kan. 205, 101 Pac. 1005.
A joint stock association was formed to develop timber lands. The trustee three-quarters of the shares. He mortgaged his interest to a bank and then conveyed it to his brother. The documents suggest that the parties were trying to convey real estate and not shares, but the certificates were also delivered to the mortgagee. Held: Though this is a partnership the shareholder could mortgage his interest whatever that might be. The mortgage is valid and the conveyance to the brother gave simply an equity of redemption. The rights of creditors do not intervene here. Stringham v. Durkee, 8 Wis. 1, 123,

Linnell v. Leon, 206 Mass. 71, 91 N. E. 895.
21 Beal v. Carpenter, 235 Fed. 273, 276 (C. C. A—Ark.).
33 Lane v. Albertson, 79 N. Y. S. 947, 953, 78 App. Div. 607.

§21] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [ Chap. II

derman, shares in a New York joint stock association were treated as shares in a corporation for the purpose of apportioning dividends.?* There was a similar case relating to a Massachusetts association.?4 A deed of land describing the grantee in its association name vests title in those who are ascertained to have been members of the association at the time,®® at least if the firm name does not contain the name of any individual partner.* If the firm name does include the name of any partner some courts would hold that title vests in that partner in trust for the firm.” An association can acquire by prescription a right of way over adjoining land of one of its members.* The effect on the legal title to the property of an association of a change of trustee in accordance with the provisions of the declaration of trust but without a formal conveyance is considered in Section 47, infra.

The chose in action of a partner is a right against the firm as an entity, though enforced against the individual partners. The chose in action of a beneficiary is a right against the trustee. Both are rights to bring a bill in equity for an accounting. The partner’s right is to his share in the net assets after payment of debts. Where the trust res is employed in business, the trustee is entitled to charge the

® Bishop v. Bishop, 81 Conn. 509, 529, 71 Atl. 583. Acc. D’Ouoge v. Leeds, 176 Mass. 558, 563, 57 N. E. 1025.

4 “And while the association is not a creature of our statutes governing the formation and powers of corporations, but is organized and exists at common law, there is no ground for any distinction in the application of the principle announced in our decisions, that in whatever manner described or apportioned an addition to capital stock generally is treated as capital belonging to the remainderman and not to the life tenant. We are accordingly of opinion that there having been no fund which could be designated either as profits, undivided earnings or accumulated surplus sufficient to meet the disbursement necessary to discharge the arrearage, the issue of new shares must be deemed an addition to the outstanding capital of the association.” Gardiner v. Gardiner, 212 Mass. 508, 99 N. E. 171. See Bisbee v. MacKay, 215 Mass. 21, 102 N. E. 327.

*5 Kentucky Block Co. v. Sewell, 249 Fed. 840, 844 (C. C. A.—Ky.).

% Pomeroy v. Latting, 2 Allen 221, 224 (a mortgage to “‘The Copake Iron Works” was foreclosed by a writ of entry brought by the individuals who composed a firm of that name). Byam v. Bickford, 140 Mass. 31, 32, 2 N. E. 887 (a non-profit association).

27 Beaman v. Whitney, 20 Me. 413, 420.

### --- Page 0166 ---

Chap. II) PROPERTY RIGHTS (§ 21

payment of debts before he accounts for the net capital. It seems therefore even now to matter little whether the certificate-holder’s interest is looked at from the point of view of partnership or of trust. In either case the difficulties arise only with respect to the transmission of his interest by descent or inter vivos and the difficulties are the same in both cases, because even where the certificate holder is a member of an association, as well as a beneficiary, he does not hold the legal title to the real estate. It is of course important to the very existence of these organizations that the certificates should be freely transferable in the manner provided in the declaration of trust or other instrument creating the organization. It is well settled, however, that parties cannot by agreement make that to be personal property which is really real property or vice versa.” It is equally well settled that courts of equity will sometimes for purposes of descent effectuate the intention of the creator of a trust by applying the doctrine of equitable conversion. This doctrine is now relied upon to solve the difficulties above mentioned in the cases relating to partnership associations and trusts with transferable shares. It becomes necessary, therefore, to consider those cases for a moment.

The doctrine of equitable conversion describes a practice of courts of chancery with respect to the ascertainment and enforcement of rights in property. A will or agreement dealing with real or personal property sometimes provides for the sale of the one kind of property and investment of the proceeds in the other kind of property before the ultimate disposition provided for in that instrument. If this conversion has not actually taken place at the time as of which such disposition is to be enforced, the question will arise as to whether rights incident to ownership of that property are to be treated as personal property or real property for purposes of enforcement or distribution. The general rule laid down is that if there is a declared intent imperatively directing a sale, the court will treat that sale

Gibbs v. Estey, 15 Gray 587; Barnes v. Hosmer, 196 Mass. 323, 325, 82

<br>

### --- Page 0167 ---

21] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT Chap. II

as having taken place at the death of the testator or execution of any other instrument in question. The doctrine is based on the declared intent, and the direction to sell "must be imperative in the sense of being positive and unmistakable."® It must be mandatory and not discretionary.** "In construing instruments of this kind, courts of equity will regard the substance and not the mere form of the agreement and give to it the precise effect which the parties intended."*? The declared intent controls.®*

An imperative direction to sell may be implied from a study of the entire instrument. Thus, in a deed to trustees of a syndicate the idea of resale ran all through it, but that was consistent with the proposition that the trust was created merely as a cheaper and more convenient method of preserving and conveying the land and therefore there was no conversion.34 An agreement for a land syndicate, however, which declared the interest of the subscribers to be personal property was held to effect a conversion.36

It is necessary that the fiduciary directed to sell should have a certain amount of time in which to do it, but in the absence of some specific provision in the document covering that point there is always danger of dispute as to when it is the duty of the fiduciary to carry out the intent and effectuate the sale. To avoid such disputes it is common to insert in such instruments some provision giving the fiduciary discretion as to the time of sale. The decision whether to sell or not must not be discretionary, but where there is an imperative direction to sell ultimately, the cases have gone very far in declaring an equitable conversion though the fiduciary has absolute discretion as to the time of sale or the right to postpone the sale indefinitely so long as he completes it within the ultimate time limited. In all these cases a conversion can be declared as of the time when the

* Wheless v. Wheless, 92 Tenn. 293, 295, 21 S. W. 595.
*1 Collins v. Combs, 160 Ky. 325, 328, 169 S. W. 721.
32 Lynn v. Gephart, 27 Md. 567, 563 (will).
33 Bennett v. Bennett, 282 Ill. 266, 118 N. E. 391.
34 Wheless v. Wheless, 92 Tenn. 293, 300, 21 S. W. 505.
38 Dicus v. Sherer, 277 Ill. 168, 115 N. E. 161.

<br>

### --- Page 0168 ---

Chap. IV] PROPERTY RIGHTS [§ 21

sale is made or is directed to be made. The important problem is what form of words will declare an intention that will result in a conversion as of the date of execution of the instrument or the taking effect of a will. The following cases in which it was held that the conversion took place from the beginning show the attitude of the courts on this subject.

A discretion as to the time of sale does not prevent an immediate equitable conversion.*

"The rule is well settled that if the will requires the real estate to be converted into money at all events, notwithstanding the executors may have a discretion as to time, it must be considered as converted into money from the death of the testator."* A trust deed containing discretionary power to sell until the death of X, and on that happening, an imperative direction to sell was held to produce a conversion from the beginning.**

A will contained the following clause: 'I empower my trustees to postpone any sale, calling in and conversion of the whole or any part or parts of my real and personal estate, hereinbefore given in trust for sale, calling in and conversion, during so long as my trustees in their uncontrolled discretion shall deem proper and in particular to retain any shares, stock and securities of M. & Co. Ltd. or any other investments held by me at my death during any period without being liable for any loss arising thereby.' This was interpreted as not a power to postpone indefinitely, but to postpone only for a reasonable time.

A marriage settlement contained the following provision: 'The said trustees or trustee may postpone the sale, hereinbefore directed, of so much of the premises hereinbefore expressed to be hereby granted and assigned as consists of real estate for so long a period as they or he

* Compton v. McMahon, 19 Mo. App. 494, 500; Stagg v. Jackson, 1 N. Y. 206, 212; Hoover v. Landis, 76 Pa. 354; Tazewell v. Smith, 1 Rand. (Va.) 313, 320.

* Wurtz Executors v. Page, 19 N. J. Eq. 365, 376. See Albright v. Van Voorhis, 104 Atl. 27 (N. J.); Contra, Christler's Executor v. Meddis, 6 B. Mon. (Ky.) 36, 37.

8 Fonseca v. Jones, 21 Map. 168, 184.

Re Marshall, (1914) 1 Ch. 192, 202.

<br>

### --- Page 0169 ---

§ 21) ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [Chap. II

shall think fit and shall not incur any liability by reason of such postponement. And also that while such real estate shall continue unsold the same shall be held by the said (trustee) upon such trusts as shall as nearly as the nature of the property will admit correspond with the trusts hereinbefore declared concerning the said trust fund.’’ The Master of the Rolls said, ‘‘the general principle applicable to this case is not open to doubt. We must look at the whole instrument, not at one clause only, and see whether there is that which amounts to an imperative direction to sell, although there may be power to postpone in the widest terms and although it may require the consent of somebody else.” The court then found from the entire instrument an imperative direction to sell. It contained also ‘‘a common form to authorize postponement of the sale, declaring that the trustees might postpone the sale of so much of the premises as consisted of real estate for so long a period as they should think fit.” It was admitted that this postponement clause did not prevent the trust for sale from being absolute. The instrument also provided that while the real estate should continue unsold it should be held by the trustees ‘upon such trusts as should, as nearly as the nature of the property would admit, correspond with the trusts thereinbefore declared concerning the trust fund.’’ The court said this did not prevent an immediate conversion.”

A will provided: ‘‘That my trustees may in their uncontrollable discretion and without being responsible for any loss and diminution of value which may result therefrom postpone the sale and conversion of any part of my residuary, real and personal property and particularly any of my real property which in the opinion of my trustees has any present or prospective value as building land, and the income of any property until sale or conversion shall be held and applied upon the same trusts as are declared concerning the income of my trust estate.” This was held to work an immediate conversion.

“Gresham Life Assurance Society v. Crowther, (1915), 1 Ch. 214, 219, 223.

“1 Re Johnson, (1915) 1 Ch. 434, 440, 442.

<br>

### --- Page 0170 ---

Chap. II] PROPERTY RIGHTS (§ 21

Where the sale is to take place only with the consent of the beneficiaries the question is more difficult. In a land syndicate case there was held to be no immediate conversion. The contrary result was reached in a case where the trustees must not only sell on the request of the beneficiaries, but must obtain the assent of the beneficiaries. The court said that these provisions were made only to assure cooperation and that at the proper time the trustee and beneficiary could be compelled to cooperate.

In the case of Dana v. The Treasurer, Judge Loring emphasized the importance of finding from the instrument an intent that the real and personal property pending the sale should be treated as a common fund and held that when that was combined with an imperative provision for ultimate sale, it effected an immediate equitable conversion. There was a similar decision in an earlier case in New York. It has also been decided in New York that shares in a New York joint stock association owned by a deceased resident of New York are personal property and taxed at full value, though part of the assets was real estate which if it had descended to the heirs of the deceased would have been exempt. A provision that the trustees may transfer the property to a corporation, if instructed by the shareholders to organize one, but authorizing them to sell only in default of action relative thereto by the shareholders was held insufficient to effect an immediate conversion. The court also noted that the declaration of trust provided that the shares "shall be personal property as to title, whatever the legal effect of such a clause may be."

42 Fox v. Fox, 250 Ill. 384, 392, 95 N. E. 498; Re Goswells’ Trusts, (1915) 2 Ch. 106, 111, 113; Metropolitan Trust Co. v. Krans, 174 N. Y. S. 541, 546; Wheless v. Wheless, 92 Tenn. 293, 300, 21 S. W. 595. See Dyett v. Central Trust Co., 19 N. Y. S. 19, Affd. 140 N. Y. 54, 35 N. E. 341.

Crane v. Bolles, 49 N. J. Eq. 373, 379, 24 Atl. 237; O’Grady v. Wilmot, (1916) 11 A. C. 231 (H. of L.).

Dana v. Treasurer, 227 Mass. 562, 116 N. E. 941. Acc. Re Reel’s Estate, 116 Atl. 107 (Pa.).

Stagg v. Jackson, 1 N. Y. 206, 212.

Re Jones, 172 N. Y. 575, 65 N. E. 570. See Re Williams Estate, 138 N. Y. S. 649, 153 App. Div. 804.

### --- Page 0171 ---

§21] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT (Chap. II

In taxation cases the courts of Massachusetts have repeatedly declared that the interest of the shareholder is a tangible property right existing in Massachusetts without considering whether the organization was really a trust or an association. In the case of Kinney v. Treasurer the organization was an association, yet on the death of a nonresident pledgee of a certificate, Massachusetts taxed the succession on the ground that title to property located in Massachusetts passed by the death. This trust was an informal one and the certificate was really a deposit book. It was pledged in New Hampshire where the pledgee lived. The shareholder lived in Massachusetts where the trustee lived and did business. The real estate was in Massachusetts. The terms of the trust instrument are not given and the court did not consider the distinction between trust and partnership, but a note to the report states that the share was in a "Voluntary Association." The court first held that the interest of the decedent, as mortgagee of certain real estate in Massachusetts should be taxed, saying that the language of the tax statute indicated "An intention on the part of legislature to tax all property that it has the power to tax. The statute is as broad as the jurisdiction of the Commonwealth" and then, speaking of the shares in the real estate trust, the court said: "This property was a valuable interest in real estate in Cambridge, the legal title to which was held by trustees." "Although the testator held only an equitable interest in this real estate instead of a legal interest we perceive no difference in principle between the passing of this interest in succession and the passing of his interest under a mortgage held in like manner as security for a debt. We are of opinion that all this property is subject to the tax upon the succession prescribed by our law."

In Peabody v. Treasurer the question was whether shares in certain real estate trusts owned by a nonresident decedent were subject to a succession tax. The trustees of the property

207 Mass. 368, 93 N. E. 586.

215 Mass. 129, 102 N. E. 435. See § 35, n. 14.

<br>

### --- Page 0172 ---

Cuap. II] PROPERTY RIGHTS [§ 21

erty were within the jurisdiction. The trustees were subject to a limited direction on the part of the shareholders. The court did not decide, however, whether it was a trust or a partnership and said that it made no difference. It cites several earlier cases which had implied that the shareholder had an equitable estate in the trust res.

“It is not necessary to analyze with greater nicety the precise character of the property interest of the shareholder under each of the trusts. It is true of all of them that their rights are equitable interests in tangible property within this Commonwealth. While the legal title is in the trustees, their ownership is fiduciary, and the certificate-holders are the ultimate proprietors of the property, which is held and managed for their benefit and which must be divided among them at the termination of the trust. Their rights constitute not choses in action but a substantial property right. In this respect the case is indistinguishable in principle from shareholders in a domestic corporation.”

In another case it was decided that the words of a statute, “this estate found here” (which is to be administered according to Massachusetts law) includes not only stock in Massachusetts corporations but “shares in the Western Real Estate Trust.” “The trustees are resident in this Commonwealth and their home business office is here, where only can the certificates be transferred upon surrender and new certificates issued. The certificate-holder is at least the owner of an undivided equitable interest in the property held by the trustees. There is on principle in this respect no distinction between such certificate and a certificate for shares of stock in a domestic corporation.”

An action was brought in the United States Court for the District of Massachusetts to recover a legacy tax collected by the United States under the War Tax of 1898 on certain

Kennedy v. Hodges, 215 Mass. 112, 114, 102 N. E. 432. See Greves v. Shaw, 173 Mass. 205, 53 N. E. 372, and Bellows Falls Power Co. v. Commonwealth, 222 Mass. 51, 109 N. E. 891. By Statute 1912 c. 678 and St. 1916 c. 268 s. 1 the tax was confined to “real estate within the commonwealth or any interest therein. See Priestly v. Treas., 230 Mass. 452, 454, 120 N. E. 100. See § 47 a, n. 1.

<br>

### --- Page 0173 ---

shares in fifteen different real estate trusts. The issue was whether the interests of the shareholders in those trusts were real or personal. To the extent that they were personal property they were taxable; if real estate they were not taxable. The plaintiff contended that to the extent that the property owned by the several trusts was real estate, the shares were equitable interests in real estate and the tax was illegally assessed. The trust deeds were of the common type but differed in many details, especially in the extent of power vested in the shareholders to control or direct by votes at meetings or by writings signed by certain proportions of them, the business to be done by the trustees, the amendment of the trust agreement, and the termination of the trust. All the agreements except one fixed a time when the trust should terminate, and in all of them a prescribed number of shareholders could terminate the trust by vote or writing at any time. The provisions as to a sale of the property on termination varied. In some the trustees had discretionary power to sell at any time without the consent of the shareholders; in others the trustees had that right with the consent of the shareholders; in others there was no provision for sale except on special authorization by the shareholders. One of the trusts had cash on hand at the time of the death of the shareholder, but was obligated in a larger amount to buy certain land. On the theory that equity will regard as done what ought to be done, the court ruled that it should treat that case as real estate unless it had to be treated as personalty under the stipulations of the trust agreement to be considered later. In another trust which had cash on hand, there was a provision for a reserve fund but the court said that the reserve fund should come out of the gross income and would not affect the equitable conversion of the cash on hand into real estate for the purchase of which it had been accumulated. The court held that the provision in most of the trust agreements for a final termination and distribution, whether imperative or discretionary, would not affect an equitable conversion of the property at the time of the death of the shareholder, because at that time the

<br>

### --- Page 0174 ---

Chap. II) PROPERTY RIGHTS [§ 21

period for conversion had not arrived. None of the trust agreements contained an imperative direction to the trustees to sell the trust property during the life of the trust and the court therefore held that the real estate was not equitably converted into personal property upon the conclusion of the trust agreements or upon the death of the shareholder. It was contended that the provision that the shareholder should have no legal or equitable interest in the trust property indicated that the right of the shareholders was nothing but a chose in action, but the court pointed out that it had been held in Massachusetts that the shareholders’ interest is not a mere chose in action but an equitable interest in the corpus of the trust, and the provision above referred to was not sufficient to change this rule, because in each of the trusts where it occurred the shareholders reserved either (a) the power to control the trustees in the sale and disposition of the property, to instruct, remove, replace trustees, amend and terminate the declaration of trust, and to order a conveyance to themselves; or (b) a right to authorize a sale or mortgage, to rebuild, to remove trustees, and to terminate the trust when the property shall be conveyed to the shareholders; or (c) to authorize sales, in which case the trustees were required to pay over the net proceeds, the right to mortgage and rebuild, remove trustees, terminate the trust and having terminated it require a conveyance of the property to the shareholders. "Under such circumstances it cannot reasonably be said that they have no right, title or interest in the property, and that their interest as shareholders is a mere chose in action." In a number of other trusts it was provided that the shares should be personal property. It was contended that this implied an imperative direction of the trustees to convert the real estate. The court said: "It is plain that the mere declaration of the creator of the trust that real estate, or an equitable interest in real estate, shall be considered as personalty, will not give it that nature. You cannot impress upon real estate the character of descendibility according to the rules applicable to personal estate without directing the real estate to be

<br>

### --- Page 0175 ---

§21] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT (Chap. II

sold." In these particular trusts, taking all their provisions into consideration, the court had no doubt that the power of sale intended to be vested in the trustees, if any, was discretionary. In two of the trusts the trust was to continue for twenty years unless sooner terminated, and the trustees were to improve, lease or sell the real estate subject to the control or direction of two-thirds of the shareholders, but with the power to act according to their discretion unless they had received instructions. The court said that this indicated that so far as they had any powers vested in them they were discretionary. 'The provision for assignability of shares without complying with the formalities necessary for a conveyance of real estate does not make them personal property. They represent equitable interests in the corpus of the trust and their character is determined by the nature of the corpus and if the corpus is real estate it would seem that the transferability would depend upon the law governing the transfer of interests in real estate in the place where the real estate was situated in the absence of legislative authority making special provision for their transfer.' The court therefore held that to the extent that the corpus of the property was real estate or cash directed or agreed to be invested therein the tax was illegal.*1

In the case of Dana v. The Treasurer ** the Supreme Court of Massachusetts again declared that an equitable interest in land outside the State is not subject to a succession duty here. The court then said that it found no escape from the rule that a shareholder in Massachusetts owns real estate in New Hampshire "'so far as the assets of the trust consist of real estate, if the rule of ordinary partnerships applies.' The opinion later shows that the court meant that the Massachusetts certificate-holder owns only pro rata. But because the declaration provided for conversion into cash at the end of the trust and also indicated an intent that the realty and personalty should be one single fund for purposes of administration in the meantime, the court said that there

% Bartlett v. Gill, 221 Fed. 476 (D. C.—Mass.).

227 Mass. 562; 116 N. E. 941.

<br>

### --- Page 0176 ---

Chap. II] PROPERTY RIGHTS §22

was an equitable conversion from the beginning according to the English rule. It showed that the early Massachusetts cases on descent of partnership realty refused to follow the English cases because the courts said that the English cases cited as authorities were really based on agreement. Judge Loring therefore held that the Massachusetts cases recognized the effectiveness of an agreement in producing an equitable conversion even in partnership cases. The declaration of trust in that case as in most of these cases was sufficiently specific to show the intent of the parties and the court held that it evidenced an intent that the entire property of the organization should be treated as a single fund whether real or personal and that it required that ultimately the realty should be sold and distributed as cash. Hence the court found that the shareholder’s interest in the Amoskeag Manufacturing Company was personal property although it was a share in a partnership association and although its assets included a large proportion of real estate in New Hampshire.

The court then considered the matter of a share in a pure trust without any association feature which owned only foreign real estate. There were no certificates representing the shares. Since there was no combined fund of realty and personalty, the court held that a provision for the sale of realty at the end of the trust could not be treated as a present equitable conversion, conversion could be decreed only as of the date when the sale was to be made, namely at the termination of the trust. Hence the share was an equitable interest in land outside the State and was not taxable.

The distinctions made in the Dana case were affirmed in the case of Priestley v. Treasurer, 230 Mass. 452, 454, 120 N. E. 100. See Withington v. Fidelity Co., 237 Mass. 73, 77, 129 N. E. 418.

<br>

### --- Page 0177 ---

§ 21) ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT

The subject was left by the case of Bartlett v. Gill and makes plain that an endorsement on a certificate representing shares in either a trust or an association is sufficient to pass title to whatever interest the certificate represents without the necessity of including the formalities of a conveyance of real estate at law.

The rest of the Dana case does not relate to the nature of the interest of the beneficiary or partner but to the right of the State of Massachusetts to levy a succession tax on a share on the ground that the enforcement of the shareholder’s right can only be through the courts of Massachusetts. The court found as a fact in this case that it could also be enforced in Wisconsin, where the property was. It is in this connection that the court speaks of “undivided quarter in a beneficial fee.” This shows the extent to which the Massachusetts courts have gone in their dicta on the nature of the interest of the beneficiary in a trust.

It seems therefore to be settled in Massachusetts,

1. That a shareholder’s interest in a trust of real estate is realty and an equitable property right and not a chose in action alone.

2. That its transfer does not require a deed.

3. That a direction to sell at the end of the trust alone does not effect a present conversion, but that such a direction in a trust of both real and personal property may show an intent to create a common fund of both realty and personalty from the beginning and in that case the doctrine of equitable conversion can be applied and the shares treated as personalty.

4. It does not matter that the beneficiary is also a partner in an association because the American limitations on the equitable conversion of partnership land do not apply where the intent to convert is declared in the instrument creating the partnership.

The court has not yet passed on the applicability to trusts with transferable shares of the cases which found that an equitable conversion, resulting from direction to sell realty,

<br>

### --- Page 0178 ---

Cuapr. II PROPERTY RIGHTS : [§ 21

was not impaired by a provision that the sale may in the
discretion of the trustee be indefinitely postponed so long as it must at least occur before the end of the trust. It would
seem, however, that the Dana case implied approval of
that doctrine also.

Since freedom of transfer is sufficiently established by the
application of these doctrines, the important problem arising
in this section has been solved for practical purposes, and
from the standpoint of the organization, it does not matter
how the share descends or how it is taxed and no present
problem seems to require further analysis of the nature of
