$32] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT (Caap. II

right against the trustee directly will be in equity. Even before time of payment the creditor may prevent the fund from being misapplied. This does not amount, however, to an implied contract to continue to carry on the business. It will be practically impossible to sue at law on such contracts. The remedy will be in equity, unless some other remedy is provided by statute.

A policy of mutual insurance issued by an unincorporated association provided for conclusiveness of an action against the manager of the fund who, however, was not a member. By express terms of the policies he was made attorney in fact of all the members for that purpose. The court held he was also trustee of a common fund and that a bill in equity would lie against him by a policy holder to reach the fund. The fact that the trust deed provides that the trustees shall be free from personal liability does not prevent them from contracting personally with a creditor of the association if they see fit.

§ 83. Trustees of Associations for Profit

That there is no trust relation under a declaration of trust which creates an association and not a pure trust is the dictum in a case which says the “so-called trustee” is merely an agent, and since he did not own shares not even a partner. This seems an unnecessary fiction. There is no reason why the relation of trustee and beneficiary should not exist between the shareholders and an express trustee.

L., c. 182, § 6 provides for an action at law for breach of contract or tort against an association created under a written instrument or declaration of trust having transferable shares and allows an attachment of the trust property. Service of process on one trustee is sufficient. It does not, however, exclude actions against the trustee. The statute appears inapplicable to pure trusts.

even though the shareholders are themselves also partners and most cases have assumed this to be so.

The relations between the trustees of associations for profit and the shareholders are determined by the rules ordinarily applicable to the relation of trustee and beneficiary. A shareholder may file a bill in equity against the trustee to compel performance of the trust,? or for a receiver of the trust fund and removal of the trustees.’ An assignee of all the shareholders and of the interest of one of the trustees cannot bring a writ of entry to recover possession of a building the association has erected.‘ A trustee cannot make a secret profit at the expense of the shareholders.’ Conference of all the trustees is not necessary

2 Mann v. Butler, 2 Barb. Ch. 362, 367; Chapin v. First Universalist Society, 8 Gray 580, 583.

Shareholders in an unincorporated association to speculate in land, title to which was taken in name of defendant, bring bill for dissolution of the association, winding up and establishment of trust. Held: Statute of Limitations did not begin to run till after an open repudiation of the trust. Barker v. White, 58 N. Y. 204, 213.

A bill by beneficiaries of an association for an accounting against the trustee and manager was not multifarious. He occupied a fiduciary relation towards the complainants in both capacities and his breaches of trust relate to the same subject matter. Moody v. Flagg, 125 Fed. 819 (C. C.—Mass.).

2 In such a suit the trustees sufficiently represent the other shareholders. Bingham v. Graham, 220 S. W. 105. (Tex. Civ. App.) And although the declaration gives certificate-holders no power of removal. Burnett v. Smith, 240 S. W. 1007 (Tex. Civ. App.).

“Chapin v. First Universalist Society, 8 Gray 580, 583.

Land was conveyed to trustees for development to pay off a mortgage and then turn over to the directors of an association. The trust deed and articles of association were different instruments, but the latter was incorporated by reference in the former. Held: Directors of the association had no power to dedicate part of the land to the public without an act by the trustees. Ward v. Davis, 3 Sandf. 502, 514.

‘ Minority shareholders in an association organized under a deed of trust may bring a bill for an accounting against the trustee who is alleged to have secretly caused to be transferred to himself shares which had been forfeited to the association, thus gaining a majority and to have transferred the trust funds to a corporation which he controls. Booth v. Dodge, 69 N. Y. 8. 673, 60 App. Div. 23.

A cooperative store association made an arrangement for a contribution of capital by the son of its general manager for which a certificate for shares was issued. Later the manager mortgaged all the goods of the store to the son to secure this, claiming it had been a loan. Held: It was a contribution of capital and not a loan and there was no debt as consideration for the mortgage. Hence the manager must account for the goods in a suit by other shareholders (p. 183)

to action. Being merely ministerial officers one might delegate his authority to the others. Ordinarily the powers of the trustees of a joint stock company are to be jointly exercised, but the others may ratify with knowledge the act of one.' A majority of the trustees, where the declaration of trust provided for action by a majority, can ratify expressly or impliedly a contract under seal originally signed by one trustee without formal authorization.2 Even when it is expressly provided that a majority may decide, all must have notice or an opportunity to be heard.® Notice to one trustee is notice to all and is not affected by the resignation of the original trustees and substitution of new ones." They are not liable for honest mistakes of judgment,!! but may be held responsible for negligence."* It

622). Declared to be a partnership (p. 618). Snyder v. Lindsey, 157 N. Y. 616, 52 N. E. 592.

An unincorporated company with transferable shares to work a gold mine was organized by a declaration of trust by the owner. The trustee was asked by a shareholder to sell his share. He bought it himself with the knowledge and consent of the shareholder and paid assessment on it. Later a rich strike was made. Held: If sale was in good faith it was valid and trustee cannot be compelled to account for subsequent increase in value. Swan v. Davenport, 119 Ia. 46, 96 N. W. 65. Must account for secret profit made in sale of own shares when they could have obtained same bargain for the other shareholders. Ferguson v. Crawford, 236 S. W. 837 (Ark.). See § 44, note 3.

has been decided that when trustees were held on an accounting responsible for losses due to their negligence, they should bear it equally and not in proportion to their shareholdings. Managers who are not shareholders are liable for the debts of the association only in case of exceeding authority or fraud. Trustees have no implied power to add to the capital of the association by borrowing money, and for an obligation incurred personally for such a purpose they have no right of indemnity against the other shareholders.*

When the declaration of trust authorized the trustees to appoint a treasurer and to define his duties and the trustees adopted by-laws prescribing as one of the duties of the treasurer the signing and endorsing of notes in the name of the company, the court held that this warranted a finding that the company was bound by his acts and its members liable on the notes. It further appeared, however, that the officers and some of the shareholders knew that he was signing such notes and even casual attention to the affairs of the organization would have disclosed how its business was being carried on. From this the court found implied ratification.

33 Coit v. Tracy, 9 Conn. 15, 21.

A defect in a chain of title was claimed to exist because it passed through certain trustees of land for a development association and the consent of the latter to the conveyance did not appear. Held: The trustees will be presumed to have been also members of the association and so beneficially interested. Hence the New York Statute vesting title in the beneficiaries did not apply. Hence the trustees had authority to convey good title. King v. Townshend, 141 N. Y. 358, 364, 36 N. E. 513.

46 Greenup v. Barbee’s Exec., 1 Bibb. 320 (Ky.).

A bill for an accounting was brought by shareholders against the trustees to charge them for losses in managing a hotel alleged to be outside the scope of the purposes of the association. Quimby v. Tapley, 202 Mass. 601, 89 N. E. 167.

Burmester v. Norris, 6 Ex. 796; Ricketts v. Bennett, 4 C. B. 688; Hawtayne v. Bourne, 7 M. & W. 595; Hawken v. Bourne, 8 M. & W. 703. A power to sell in usual course of business was held to give the trustees power to sell all the assets to a new company. Lucky, etc., Ass’n v. Cox, 230 S. W. 858 (Tex. Civ. App.). See Cox v. Lucky Pat Oil & Gas Ass’n, 241 S. W. 105.

Re Worcester Company, 3 De G. M. & G. 180; Ex parte Chippendale, 4 De G. M. & G. 43; Australian Co. v. Mounsey, 4 K. & J. 733. For cases on the right of indemnity of trustees, see § 43, notes 12 et seq., § 44, note 23, and § 64, notes 2 and 3.

<br>

### --- Page 0237 ---

ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT

“It may be that many of these defendants were misled by the appearance and language of the certificate of ‘non-assessable’ ‘shares of stock’ issued by the officers of the company, that they assumed that the company was a corporation, and did not fully realize that they were associating themselves as members of a partnership. But as the auditor finds, ‘none of these defendants except the officers paid any attention whatever to the conduct of the business of the organization.’ They voluntarily adopted the partnership form of association; and their rights and obligations as shareholders are those defined by the established rules of law applicable to ordinary partnerships.”

A promissory note was signed by National Realty Co. by A. B. C. and D. trustees. The National Realty Co. was a voluntary association and the declaration of trust authorized the trustees to give notes. An action of contract was brought against the signers as individuals and the defendants claimed that they borrowed the money as trustees for the association and that the plaintiff knew these things and was a certificate holder. The court below excluded the declaration of trust on the ground that it varied the terms of the note and refused to rule that the signature was the signature of the association and was not the joint signature of the individual defendants. It also refused to rule that if the plaintiff knew of and was a party to the declaration of trust he was bound by its provisions. The court below found for the plaintiff. It was held on appeal that the note on its face purported to bear the signature of the association by the defendants as trustees. Since the Negotiable Instruments Act provides that a signature disclosing a principal shall not bind the agent, the defendants would not be individually liable if the National Realty Company existed and the defendants had authority to act for it. Extrinsic evidence was admissible to prove those facts and the declaration of trust should have been admitted. The agreement of trust created a voluntary association with the collective title of National Realty Co. 

Horgan v. Morgan, 233 Mass. 381, 124 N. E. 32.

<br>

### --- Page 0238 ---

Cuar. IT] TRUSTEES OF ASSOCIATIONS

The trustees were authorized to sign that name to the note. Hence the note was signed in the name of a disclosed and existent person by an authorized agent acting by express authority of the associate members of the company among whom was the plaintiff. The court also said that independently of the Negotiable Instruments Act the excluded evidence was admissible in support of the action considered as an equitable defense.

A judgment at law against the trustee of an association on a note signed by him as trustee is not a bar to a subsequent bill in equity on the original cause of action to reach and apply the assets of the trust in payment of the debt, though shareholders are also joined as defendants. The doctrine of merger does not affect liabilities collateral and subsidiary to the original cause of action, yet separable from it. It would seem that the same rule should apply even if the trustee is also a shareholder since the doctrines of merger and res judicata do not apply unless the action was brought against the party defendant in the same capacity as was the earlier action. An action against the trustee is not an election to hold him individually to the exclusion of the trust estate for the two remedies are not inconsistent.

Some curious decisions in relation to such trustees seem inconsistent with elementary principles of trusts. It has been held that the remedy for negligence of syndicate managers of what appears to have been an association for profit in extending a loan was an action for breach of contract.

Adams v. Swig, 234 Mass. 584; 125 N. E. 857. See Neville v. Gifford, 242 Mass. 124. See Lucky etc., Ass'n, v. Cox, 230 S. W. 858 (Tex. Civ. App.). See Cox v. Lucky Pat Oil & Gas Ass'n, 218 W. 105.

Frost v. Thompson, 219 Mass. 360, 368, 106 N. E. 1009.

The court did not have to decide this in Frost v. Thompson, 219 Mass. 360, 368, 106 N. E. 1009, but cited cases which sustain the principle above stated. See First National Bank v. Schuler, 153 N. Y. 163, 173 (Trustee); Troxell v. D. L. & W. R. R., 227 U. S. 434, 443, 57 L. ed. 586, 33 S. Ct. 274, (Administrator); Lander v. Arno, 65 Me. 26, 29 (Administrator); McBurme v. Seaton, 111 Ind. 56, 12 N. E. 101 (Guardian); Dibert v. D’brey, 248 Mo. 617, 661, 154 S. W. 1116 (Assignee).

Frost v. Thompson, 219 Mass. 360, 369, 106 N. E. 1099. Service on trustee under statute is sufficient to bring in the association. Moss v. Republic Supply Co., 240 S. W. 326 (Tex. Civ. App.)

### --- Page 0239 ---

§33] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT (Chap. II

tract at law and not an accounting in equity. It has also been held that shareholders cannot bring a cross bill for exoneration against the directors, who were also defendants in the original suit, for negligence in incurring a debt on the ground that the relation of principal and surety did not exist between them. Where the trustees of an association converted its property they were held liable in tort at law.34

Since liability for torts is joint and several in equity as at law, beneficiaries may bring a bill in equity for an accounting for wrongdoing by the trustees though all trustees are not within the jurisdiction, but if the purpose is to enjoin contemplated action by some of the trustees looking to the dissolution or winding up of the association, it is necessary that all trustees be within the jurisdiction and be made parties, otherwise there might be conflicting decisions in different jurisdictions as to the action contemplated. The provisions for selection of new trustees to fill vacancies must be strictly complied with. Thus where the deed of trust, empowering the remaining trustees to appoint the new trustee, provided that they express their choice by executing a formal certificate to that effect, a mere vote without such a certificate was held not to entitle the person elected to bring a bill for an injunction against the remaining trustees to certify their selection. Trustees have power to issue shares as provided in the deed of trust even after the land which was the original capital of the enterprise has been sold. The certificate holders are not necessary parties to an action by the trustees for breach of contract to purchase all the assets of the trust whether they are regarded as trustees or merely agents since they have

22 This case is perhaps explained on the theory that the members of the syndicate were independent contractors and not partners. The point was not discussed. Paton v. Clark, 156 Pa. 49, 53, 27 Atl. 116.

33 Manning v. Gasharie, 27 Ind. 399.

34 When trustees commit any act not inseparable from their liabilities as such they are amenable like other individuals in an action of tort. Denis v. Kennedy, 19 Barb. 517, 526.

35 American Cotton Oil Trust. Wall v. Thomas, 41 Fed. 620 (C.C.—N.Y.).

36 McFaddin v. Wiess, 168 S.W. 486, 490 (Tex.).

37 Yeaman v. Galveston City Co., 167 S.W. 710, 720 (Tex.)

### --- Page 0240 ---

Cuar. II] PROPRIETY OF INVESTMENT [$34

been given express power to represent the certificate holders and bind them in all transactions touching the property.* The subject of this section is further considered in chapter III, infra.

§ 34. Propriety of Investment by Trustees in Shares in Associations for Profit

A question has been raised as to the propriety of investment by trustees in shares issued by unincorporated associations. The objection is based on the possibility of liability of shareholders for debts of the enterprise. This particular objection does not apply to investments in the shares of pure trusts though there may be the same objection to the character of the enterprise that might be raised in the case of investment in the shares of corporations. An executor was instructed in Rhode Island that since there is no individual liability of holders of shares in a trust as distinguished from a partnership, the nature of the security was no objection to his holding it as trustee under the will though the court did not pass on the financial condition of the trust in question.?

The question now being considered applies to investment in shares in associations. These considerations may be eliminated if later decisions prove that the restrictions on liability of shareholders commonly inserted in declarations of trust are effective in eliminating liability of shareholders.

An important case in Massachusetts goes far to establish the propriety of investment in these shares. The case related to shares in the Massachusetts Electric Companies whose assets had consisted of securities issued by numerous street railways in eastern Massachusetts which were thereby operated as a single system. In common with other transportation companies the system got into financial difficulties during the Great War and was reorganized after a receivership. The court did not decide whether the Massachusetts

Simpeon v. Klipstein, 262 Fed. 821 (D. C.—N. J.) Also holding that representatives stand on their own citizenship in the Federal courts irrespective of the citizenship of the persons whom they represent.

R. I. Hospital Trust Co. v. Copeland, 30 B. T. 193, 98 Atl. 273.

<br>

### --- Page 0241 ---

§ 34] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [Chap. II

Electric Companies was a partnership or a pure trust. "Assuming for the purposes of this decision that it was a partnership does not render the investment unwarranted as a matter of law. On that assumption it was a partnership of a peculiar kind. It was not an ordinary business, commercial or trading partnership. The nature of its authorized investments seemingly removed it as far as possible from the common incidents of a copartnership adventure. Apparently it was guarded as fully as was practicable from speculative features and the oscillations of value incident to varying conditions of trade. There is nothing in the record to indicate that the amount of shares issued exceeded a conservative valuation of the securities owned. The rights of the shareholders were carefully guarded by the terms of the agreement. Their responsibility was reduced to a minimum so far as possible by written statement of obligations. It was expressly stated that the trustees had no power to bind the shareholders personally. All persons dealing with the trustees were confined by the agreement to the property of the so-called trust to the exoneration of shareholders. See Hussey v. Arnold, 185 Mass. 202, 204; Williams v. Boston, 208 Mass. 497, 501; Carr v. Leahy, 217 Mass. 438, 440; Rand v. Farquhar, 226 Mass. 91, 96. It was required of the trustees to stipulate in every obligation into which they might enter that the shareholders should not be held liable personally. Whatever may be held ultimately as to the force and effect of those terms in the trust agreement, they manifest an effort to reduce the liability of the shareholders to the lowest limit. In any event such liability of shareholders was not greater than the liability of stockholders in manufacturing corporations at the time the investment was made which was before the court in Harvard College v. Amory, 9 Pick. 446. See Child v. Boston & Fairhaven Iron Works, 127 Mass. 516, for an historical review of our statutes respecting stockholders’ liability for debts of the corporation. The exercise of sound judgment and good faith and a strict compliance with the terms of the agreement by the trustees would have a strong tendency to relieve the

<br>

### --- Page 0242 ---

Cuar. II) GOVERNMENTAL REGULATION (§ 35

shareholders from all responsibility. The kind of corporations in which the trustees were to hold stock were chiefly and primarily public service corporations operating mainly in this Commonwealth, and corporations incidental to or furnishing supplies to such public service corporations. The law of this Commonwealth for many years has made provision for careful supervision of the issue of stocks and bonds of public service corporations to the end that such securities may represent only honest investment necessary for valuable use to the public.

The agreed facts show that good faith and sound discretion, measured by the prevailing practice of men of experience and good judgment in such matters, was exercised in making the investment here assailed. That is the standard as established by the authorities to which reference has been made. Giving due weight to all the considerations affecting the trust agreement, no sufficient reason appears for declaring the investment unwarranted. The case is close, but falls within the rule of Harvard College v. Amory, ubi supra.” ®

The problem considered by the court in all probability has already become academic, since it is most unlikely that shares to which partnership liability attaches will be issued for public investment now that the distinction between trust and association has become established.

§ 35. Governmental Regulation of Associations for Profit

It was originally supposed that Eliot v. Freeman ! decided that real estate trusts could not constitutionally be taxed, but the court simply said that the original Federal Corporation Tax as written did not include them. It is to be noted, however, that the justification of the constitutionality of the Federal Corporation Tax was put on the ground that it was a tax on a privilege conferred by the State or Federal Government, and the real estate trusts were said to have no

2 Kimball v. Whitney, 233 Mass. 321, 329, 123 N. E. 665. The report contains a copy of most of the provisions of the declaration of trust. See § 24 ” 7500 U. 8. 178, 197, 55 L. ed. 424, 31S, Ct. 360.

<br>

### --- Page 0243 ---

§35] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [Chap. II

such privilege. The tax was held to embrace only such corporations and joint stock companies as were organized under some statute or derived from that source some quality or benefit not existing at common law. Hence a New York joint stock company was held subject to the tax because by statute it had the right to sue or be sued in the name of "its president or treasurer." The court said that the statutes of New York have legalized attributes assumed by the United States Express Company and made valid and effective its asserted rights.

The amendment to the Interstate Commerce Act authorizing regulation of rates provided that "the term common carrier as used in this act shall include express companies." It was held by the United States Supreme Court that this included the great express companies though they are partnerships. Judge Holmes said: "The power of Congress is hardly denied. The constitutionality of the statute as against corporations is established and no reason is suggested why Congress has not equal power to charge the partnership assets with a liability and to personify the company so far as to collect a fine by a proceeding against it in the company name." The court, however, referred to the New York cases to show the "semi-corporate standing of these companies."

The various acts imposing the Federal Income Tax expressly include associations with corporations as subject to the tax. They also make special provision for taxing partnerships and different provisions for taxing trusts. The case of Crocker v. Malley held that pure trusts were not subject to the provisions for taxation of associations though they had transferable shares. The only attempts at regulation in

2 Roberts v. Anderson, 226 Fed. 7, 12, 141 C. C. A. 121 (N. Y.).

3 United States v. Adams Express Co., 229 U. S. 381, 390, 57 L. ed. 1237, 33 S. Ct. 878.

The manager of a trading stamp association organized by twelve merchants for their own use from which they bought the stamps, is liable to a license tax imposed on any person, corporation or company engaged in the business of trading stamps. Sharpe v. Lexington, 221 S. W. 214 (Ky.).

4 Discussed at length in § 14, n. 13. See also this and other cases discussed in § 16 especially at n.

<br>

### --- Page 0244 ---

Cuap. IT] GOVERNMENTAL REGULATION [§ 35

Massachusetts are the filing statutes above noted, the stock transfer act of 1914 and a statute affecting court procedure. The filing statutes require trustees of a voluntary association under a written instrument or declaration of trust the beneficial interest in which is divided into transferable certificates of participation or shares to file copies of their declaration of trust in certain offices. A penalty is imposed for failure to obey. In view of the distinction between associations and trusts in the Massachusetts cases this seems inapplicable to pure trusts. No Massachusetts decisions have been found relating to these statutes. The stock transfer act is discussed later. The change in procedure provides that “a voluntary association created by a written instrument or declaration of trust, the beneficial interest in which is divided into transferable certificates of participation or shares, may be sued in an action at law for debts and other obligations or liabilities contracted or incurred by the trustees under such written instrument or declaration of trust or by the duly authorized agents of such trustees or by any duly authorized officer of the association in the performance of their respective duties under such written instrument or declaration of trust and for any damages to persons or property resulting from the negligence of such trustees, agents or officers acting in the performance of their respective duties and its property shall be subject to attachment and execution in like manner as if it were a corporation and service of process on one of the trustees shall be sufficient.” It is to be noted that all of these enactments are expressly limited to associations and do not apply to pure trusts. Statutes regulating procedure in actions by and against associations exist in several of the States and such statutes have been held constitutional.

G. L. c. 182 as amended by St. 1922 c. 272. See Malley v. Howard, 281 Fed. 363, (C.C.A.—Mass.). Note G. L. c. 110 § 5 requiring filing of certain information by persons doing business under names other than their own which may apply to some of these organizations.

G. L. c. 182, § 6; c. 223, § 40. See the rather surprising interpretation put on the act by Anderson, J. in Malley v. Howard, 281 Fed. 363 (C.C.A. Mass.) discussed in § 16 note 18. See § 23, notes 8 and 13.

<br>

### --- Page 0245 ---

§ 35] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [ Chap. II

There has been no decision directly passing upon the right to regulate unincorporated associations as distinguished from all other partnerships. Regulations discriminating between corporations on the one hand and unincorporated associations and individuals on the other raise a somewhat similar problem but one much easier of solution, since corporations having a franchise from the State are clearly subject to regulation.* The principle involved in the two cases must be the same, however, though its application will be different. When the restrictions placed upon corporations are less than those upon associations or individuals, that is, when the design is to restrict a business to corporations, the analogy becomes closer. Of such a case Judge McClain said: "The question is as to the right to discriminate between classes by way of regulation of the business," (unincorporated associations, partnerships and individuals on the one hand and corporations on the other). "That such discrimination may be made when based on a reasonable distinction involving the public welfare cannot be questioned; and if the distinction between classes is reasonable and not purely artificial and the statute is applicable to all who come within the limits of the classification, its constitutionality cannot be questioned." ® Of banking, the Supreme Court of the United States has said, "But if the State might require incorporation, it may give advantages to incorporated companies." !°

It has been held that shares in such associations cannot be taxed to the owner." But that the association, like other partnerships, must be assessed in the town of its principal place of business.' The distinction made between shares in corporations and shares in associations was that whereas

® Mallincrodt Works v. St. Louis, 238 U. S. 41, 55, 59 L. Ed. 1192, 35 S. Ct. 671.

* Restrictions on unincorporated building and loan associations greater than those on corporations held constitutional. Brady v. Mattern, 125 Ia. 158, 163, 100 N. W. 358.

0 Assaria State Bank v. Dolley, 219 U. S. 121, 127, 31 S. Ct. 189.

11 Hoadley v. County Commissioners, 105 Mass. 519, 526.

12 Ricker v. American Loan & Trust Co., 140 Mass. 346, 349, 5 N. E. 284; Williams v. Boston, 208 Mass. 497, 94 N. E. 808.

### --- Page 0246 ---

Chap. II GOVERNMENTAL REGULATION [§ 35

the former were property under Massachusetts decisions, the latter simply represented an undivided partnership interest in the net assets of the association which was otherwise taxed.

On the other hand, it has been held that shares in real estate trusts are not choses in action but substantial property rights and are subject to the succession tax like shares in domestic corporations.

It has been held that a corporate franchise tax, an excise on the corporation based on the excess in market value of its aggregate share capital over the assessed value of its tangible property, otherwise taxed, cannot be extended to associations.15 The reasons given were that the association did not ask any special privilege of the legislature and that its peculiar features were created by agreement of the members under their natural rights at common law. "We do not see how this peculiar feature (transferability of shares) can be called a commodity, subject to special excise, any more than the agreement of co-partnership itself or any clause or part of it or any other agreement, right or mode of transacting any business can be called a commodity and so liable to taxation at the will of the legislature." The true meaning of this decision has been the subject of discussion in subsequent decisions.

The legislature of Massachusetts exercised its constitutional right and asked the opinion of the justices on the constitutionality of a stock transfer tax including a tax on shares in unincorporated associations. They differed in

13 Hoadley v. County Commissioners, 105 Mass. 519, 526.

14 Peabody v. Treasurer, 215 Mass. 129, 102 N. E. 435.

Shares in a joint stock association of New York owned by a deceased resident in New York are personal property and taxed at full value though part of the assets of the association was real estate, which if it had descended to the heirs of the deceased would have been exempt. Re Jones, 172 N. Y. 575, 65 N. E.

Shares in a New York joint stock association owned by a resident of New Jersey are liable to a transfer tax on his decease on the proportion of their value which the property within the State bore to the entire property of the association. Re Willmer’s Estate, 138 N. Y. 8, 649, 153 App. Div. 804. See § 21, note 54, § 47a, note 1.

48 Gleason v. McKay, 134 Mass. 419, 425.

See Minot v. Winthrop, 162 Mass. 113, 38 N. E. 512.

### --- Page 0247 ---

§35] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT (Chap. II

opinion, a majority holding such a tax valid—three on the
ground that an excise in Massachusetts can be levied on a
privilege which is the exercise of a natural right. Four
judges denied this, and three held that transferability of
shares in a partnership is an immaterial distinction from
other partnerships and cannot be a basis for classification,
but one (now the Chief Justice) held that transferability
of such shares was not a natural right because a business
dependent on such elaborate provisions which require constant
resort to the courts for their enforcement may be
regulated by the legislature, but he did think that an excise
on the existence of such an association or on the holding of
property by it would be unconstitutional. It is not entirely
clear whether or not the justices thought that doing business
in the form of a partnership is taxable provided that the
excise applies to all partnerships.” The legislature of
Massachusetts, however, in enacting the stock transfer tax
of 1914 included a section making it applicable to transfers
of ‘‘certificates of participation or shares of all voluntary
associations existing under a written instrument or declaration
of trust the beneficial interest in which is divided into
transferable certificates of participation or shares.* No
decision on the constitutionality of this section of the law
has yet been rendered.

The same question will probably arise in other States
having stock transfer taxes where the statute in terms applies to associations but not to trusts. The United States Revenue Act of 1921, providing for stamp taxes on the issue and transfer of shares, expressly joined associations in the definition of corporations whose shares are subject to the tax, but did not include trusts. Rulings of the department of Internal Revenue make it clear that shares in pure trusts are not subject to these stamp taxes under the statute of 1921.

1 Opinion of the Justices, 196 Mass. 601, 615, 620, 626, 85 N. E. 545. See Williams v. U. S. Express Co., 195 Mo. App. 362, 191 S. W. 1087; Malley v. Bowditch, 259 Fed. 809 (C. C. A. Mass.) and § 16, n.

8 Acts of 1914, ch. 770, § 10, as amended by Acts of 1915, ch. 238, § 5.

3 The decisions of the department officials, however, are making a distinction

<br>

### --- Page 0248 ---

Chap. II] GOVERNMENTAL REGULATION [$35

So-called Blue Sky Laws regulating the sale of securities in many States apply to associations either by the express terms of the statute or under constitutional provisions defining the word corporations so as to include associations. In one case the statute was held applicable for this reason to the shares of an association organized under a declaration of trust with transferable shares, although the court declared it was not a partnership under the doctrine of Williams v. Milton and Crocker v. Malley. In fact, the trust contained elements of association which probably made it a partnership under those decisions, so that the case is probably not an authority for the claim that certificates of beneficial interest in pure trusts are subject to the statute.

No cases throw light upon the constitutionality of statutes imposing different regulations on trusts with transferable shares from those imposed upon ordinary trusts, so that the subject would seem more nebulous than the similar question relating to the right to regulate partnerships with transferable shares as distinguished from ordinary partnerships. The first statute regulating pure trusts was enacted in 1919 in Oklahoma and is considered in a subsequent section.*4. It must be borne in mind, however, that courts are inclined to adopt a liberal attitude towards legislative efforts at classification for taxation purposes. There have been a number of cases relating to non-profit associations where classifications as between different kinds of such associations have been upheld. These cases are discussed hereafter.

A somewhat similar question arises with reference to the right of one state to exclude citizens of other States from doing business therein until they have complied with certain statutory restrictions. These questions have arisen most often with reference to corporations, the right to exclusion when the trustees own a large part of the certificates of beneficial interest.

See § 14, note 15 and § 37 note 10.
Home Lumber Co. v. Hopkins, 107 Kans. 153, 190 Pac. 601. See State v. Lee, 288 Mo. 679, 233 S. W. 20.
21 See § 47a post.
See §71, post.

<br>

### --- Page 0249 ---

§35] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [Chap. II

include which is well recognized, except where complicated questions relating to interstate commerce arise. For this reason it has been held that corporations doing business other than interstate commerce in a State other than that of their incorporation must submit to statutes providing for service of process upon them. Although no square decision particularly relating to associations has been observed, the Supreme Court of Kansas after holding its ‘‘blue sky” law applicable to an association, which the court has previously held to be a pure trust and that an effort to sell its own shares was not doing business in the State within the meaning of the statute imposing conditions on the doing of business on a foreign corporation said, ‘‘it has been rightly decided that under the constitution, plaintiff is to be treated as a corporation, and if it should apply for permission to do business in the State it would be subject to the corporation law, so far as the same is applicable to that class of corporations. Of course there are provisions of the corporation law that are not applicable to companies like the plaintiff, and the legislature has not as yet enacted statutes peculiarly applicable to that class of corporations. It will be time enough to determine what regulations should be applied when they are imposed upon a company like the plaintiff or when a question arises as to their right to do business in the State.” **

Subsequently the question arose for direct determination. The plaintiff brought an action of contract against the defendant described as a foreign corporation. After judgment, the defendant filed a motion to vacate on the ground that it was a Massachusetts trust. This motion was overruled and the defendant appealed. The court said ‘This court has already decided that an organization of the character of the defendant is deemed to be a corporation within the meaning of the Kansas law making permission from the charter board necessary to enable a corporation to do business in this State.’ (Citing the Home Lumber case.) 33 Home Lumber Co. v. Hopkins, 107 Kans. 153, 190 Pac. 601. See ex parte Girard, 186 Cal. 718, 200 Pac. 593.

<br>

### --- Page 0250 ---

Chap. II] GOVERNMENTAL REGULATION (§ 35

Except for the section of the Kansas constitution relied upon in the case above cited, it may be conceded that 'Massachusetts Trusts' are not to be classed as corporations." The court held, however, that the constitution gave to the word corporation a special and enlarged meaning. "The kind of association there described is by the terms of the section itself authorized to sue and be sued. The definition so adopted is not necessarily to be applied to the word 'corporation' wherever it is used in the statute; but where the subject makes it pertinent and within the meaning of the legislation, this is the natural course. As was pointed out in the Lumber Company case, trusts of the character of the defendant have among other powers and privileges not possessed by individuals or partnerships, those of continuous existence and exemption from personal liability. They enjoy practically all the advantages of ordinary corporations, and no reason is apparent why they should not be subject to the same obligations and regulations." The constitution of Minnesota, the State in which the trust agreement creating the defendant association was executed, contains a provision quite similar in effect to that we are considering, although the language is somewhat different. The precise question was not passed on by the Minnesota Supreme Court, although statements on its part support the defendant. We find nothing to justify us in overruling the previous decision."

The court reasoned that the defendant is the kind of organization to which the Kansas constitution and statutes apply the name corporation and on which they impose regulations; it does not matter that the Minnesota constitution and statutes do not apply that term to it. "It was in a sense created under the laws of Minnesota inasmuch as it was organized there and the laws there in force permitted such organization by not forbidding it. That the permission resulted from an interpretation by the court of the common law, does not in this respect make the situation materially different from what it would be if the legislature had enacted a declaratory statute to the same effect."

<br>

### --- Page 0251 ---

§35) ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [ Cuar. II

We need not determine at this time how far a Massachusetts trust partakes of the characteristics of an ordinary corporation with respect to its residence being unchangeably fixed by the place of its creation, but what is presently necessary is to decide as we do that such an organization may be a non-resident of this State and as to it a foreign corporation, that is to say, not a corporation within the meaning of the law of some other jurisdiction, but an association known to our statutes as a corporation which is not a resident of this State.” The court did not decide whether delivery of a copy of a summons to one of the three trustees would be sufficient service, nor whether the possibility of such delivery would bar service by publication because the affidavit was sufficient on its face and the defendant having knowledge of the bringing of the action made no attempt to show its statements to be untrue until after judgment.”

Assuming that it is within the power of the State of Kansas to impose terms on which a non-resident trustee who has issued transferable shares may do business within that State without imposing like regulations upon other trustees, the only justification for these decisions which hold that an organization which is not a partnership is nevertheless to be treated as a corporation because it is an association, is that the court believes that they may be a trust with transferable shares which is an association, but is not a partnership. In other words, the element of control as well as the element of association is essential to partnership, but there may be association without control. Applied to the cases in question, the declaration of trust in the Home Lumber case probably constitutes a partnership under the Massachusetts decisions. No copy of the declaration of trust of the United States and Mexico Oil Company is yet available, but if it contains no elements of association and provides for action by beneficiaries as individuals in only the limited number of in-

Harris v. United States & Mexico Oil Co., 204 Pac. 754 (Kans.).

A Texas Syndicate in the form of a trust, selling its own certificates in Oregon is a "foreign trust company" doing a trust business within the meaning of the Oregon statutes. Superior Oil and Refining Syndicate, Limited v. Handley, 99 Ore. 146, 195 Pac. 159.

<br>

### --- Page 0252 ---

Chap. II] GOVERNMENTAL REGULATION [§35

Instances covered by the declaration of trust in Crocker v. Malley and Williams v. Milton, there is no justification whatever for calling those beneficiaries combined with the trustees an association, because they do not act as a unit on any occasion. The interpretation of the word 'association' in these constitutions and statutes is of course a matter for local determination. For convenience or brevity, it may be appropriate for a legislature to call something an association which is not an association and thereafter to refer to it as such, but that of course does not change the nature of the thing so referred to and should have no bearing upon the question whether the trustees of that organization are entitled to do business in the State without regulations not imposed on other individual non-residents or whether the shareholders are individually liable for the debts of the organization.

There is one important case relating to an ordinary partnership. A Kentucky statute authorized service upon absent partners whose firms had done business in the State by service on the resident agent. A suit was brought in this way in Kentucky against a partnership and judgment rendered against Illinois partners in an action on the judgment in Illinois. That State refused to recognize the judgment and the Supreme Court of the United States upheld that decision and distinguished the case of a partnership from that of a corporation, because the State had no power to exclude members of a partnership and so the doing of business by some of the partners in Kentucky was not an implied consent of the others to allow service of process upon them.

No case has been observed which declares that a non-resident trustee cannot be excluded from doing business in a State, but there are several cases that hold that a statute forbidding the appointment of a non-resident trustee is unconstitutional.

8 State v. Lee, 288 Mo. 679, 233 S. W. 20, 27
* Flexner v. Farson, 248 U. S. 289, 63 L. ed. 250, 39 S. Ct. Rep. 97.
See § 46, note 18.

<br>

### --- Page 0253 ---

§ 36. The Uniform Partnership Act

No discussion of the subject of this chapter would be complete without consideration of the effect on it of the codification of the law of partnership which was adopted in 1914 by the Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws. This Code has been enacted into law in some of the States but has not so far had the general approval that has been given some of the other recommendations of these Commissioners. It makes some changes in the law of partnership and makes certain many things that were before in confusion. As the result of twelve years’ discussion among the Commissioners and their expert advisers it is doubtless the best practical solution of a perplexing problem and without regard to its fundamental soundness will be welcomed for its promise of definite rules instead of

Alaska, Idaho, Illinois, Maryland, Massachusetts, Michigan, Minnesota, New Jersey, New York, Pennsylvania, Tennessee, Utah, Wisconsin, Wyoming.

In no department of the law has controversy been keener than between the advocates of the so-called ‘entity theory’ of partnership and the advocates of the so-called ‘common law’ theory. The supporters of the latter have regarded themselves as paladins of a cherished principle handed down from a golden age of the common law pure and undefiled and have affixed that worst stigma of the practitioner, ‘theorist,’ to those who sought to make consistently visible throughout the framework of the subject the basic logic which the instinct of merchants has always applied to the practical aspects of this purely mercantile relation. When the commissioners began consideration of this subject the late Dean Ames was employed to draw an act in accordance with the mercantile conception of partnership of which he had been one of the greatest advocates. Later, however, the defenders of the faith rallied their forces and gradually brought the Conference to adopt formally the other theory. Attempts to make such a code logically consistent then resulted in several drafts which have culminated in one which is in itself a conclusive confirmation of the soundness of the mercantile view. It has often been noted that those jurisdictions that were loudest in their approval of the ‘common law’ theory of partnership were the first to depart from it in reality when wrestling with specific problems. The proposed code, while asserting with emphasis the favorite rule of the supporters of the common law theory, viz., that the partners as individuals have a property right in the specific property of the partnership, creates for that property right a new name—tenancy in partnership—and then proceeds by definition to eliminate from it all essential incidents of property rights, leaving in it only a modified right to possession. The other important changes in the law which this code will produce all tend towards consistency with the mercantile view of partnership. If the act had emanated from a legislative committee should we suspect that the radicals had, in the language of the day, “put one over on” the conservatives?

<br>

### --- Page 0254 ---

Chap. IV. THE UNIFORM PARTNERSHIP ACT [§36

conflicting opinions. The Code defines partnership as "an association of two or more persons to carry on as co-owners a business for profit." It makes no distinction between small partnerships and large associations. On some points already discussed in this chapter it makes certain the rule towards which the courts seemed tending, but in the main it will effect few changes in the law applicable to what are herein called unincorporated associations. Since the problems involved in the modern associations formed under elaborate deeds of trust arise mainly from express agreement of the parties defining their relations, the Code will have no effect on them except where the plans of their organizers break down.

One of the most important parts of the new Code is that dealing with real property. Title to firm real estate may be acquired and conveyed in the partnership name. A conveyance in the firm name by any partner passes the title in such real estate to the grantee, but the firm may recover it, if the partner’s act does not "bind the partnership," unless the land has passed to a "holder for value without knowledge that the partner in making the conveyance has exceeded his authority." "Knowledge" is defined to include "knowledge of such facts as in the circumstances shows bad faith." Where title to real property is in the name of the partnership, a conveyance executed by a partner, in his own name, passes the equitable interest of the partnership, provided the act is one within the authority of the partner "as previously defined." Where title to real property is in the name of one or more but not all the partners and the record does not disclose the right of the partnership,

3 Section 6 of the Act. Creditors of an embarrassed firm agreed to let its successors take it over and carry it on subject to the control of a managing committee of creditors until their debts were paid. All profits were to be devoted to that purpose. Held under the Uniform Partnership Act, defendants not having held themselves out as partners and not having by their acts evinced intent to be other than lenders, they are not partners, Re Hoyne, 277 Fed. 668 (D. C.—Ill).

Section 8 (3).

* Section 3 (1).

Section 10 (2).

<br>

### --- Page 0255 ---

§ 36] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [ Cap. IT

the partners in whose name the title stands may convey title to such property, but the partnership may recover such property if the partners’ act does not bind the partnership” as previously defined ‘unless the purchaser or his assignee is a holder for value without knowledge.” * ‘Where the title to real property is in the name of one or more or all the partners, or in a third person in trust for the partnership, a conveyance executed by a partner in the partnership name or in his own name, passes the equitable interest of the partnership, provided the act is one within the authority of the partner” as previously defined.’ ‘‘Where the title to real property is in the names of all the partners A conveyance executed by all the partners passes all their rights in such property.” ” All of these provisions are simply the application of common sense to a business problem. So far as they change existing law they are wholly desirable.

The next important change in the present law relates to changes in the membership of the firm. ‘A person admitted as a partner into an existing partnership is liable for all the obligations of the partnership arising before his admission as though he had been a partner when such obligations were incurred except that this liability shall be satisfied only out of partnership property.” 1! ‘A conveyance by a partner of his interest in the partnership does not of itself dissolve the partnership.” }? ‘The dissolution of a partnership is the change in the relation of the partners caused by any partner ceasing to be associated in the carrying on as distinguished from the winding up of the business.” # “On dissolution the partnership is not terminated, but continues until the winding up of partnership affairs is completed.”’ 14

*Section 10 (3).
* Section 10 (4).
% Section 10 (5).
"Section 17.
412 Section 27 (1).
13 Section 29.
14 Section 30.

<br>

### --- Page 0256 ---

Chap. II] THE UNIFORM PARTNERSHIP ACT (§ 36

The effect of dissolution on existing liabilities of the firm is left unchanged by the Code.

The rights of creditors upon a change in the membership of a firm without a liquidation are further treated in an elaborate series of rules,* which, without changing the rule that change of membership is a dissolution, in substance provide that creditors of the firm as it was before the change in membership are creditors of the firm that exists thereafter (the new partners’ liability to be satisfied only out of the partnership property) and thereby establish their right to reach the property employed in the business. Under these rules a shareholder in a partnership association who sells his share does not thereby escape liability for debts contracted during his membership unless the courts find from the nature of the organization that the creditor impliedly agreed to accept his transferee as debtor in place of the transferor by way of novation; in the absence of such agreement the transferee is not personally liable for debts incurred before the transfer, but creditors may reach directly the property of the association whether their claims accrued before or after the transfer of shares in question; and transfer of shares effects a technical dissolution of the partnership. It is to be expected, however, that courts which have shown a tendency to classify associations as something distinct from other partnerships, will hold ultimately that the nature of these organizations is so familiar and the understanding of business men that their obligations are to be enforced only against the association as it exists at the time of enforcement so common that a novation is implied in every case of transfer of shares.

The creation by the act of a new property right to be known as tenancy ‘‘in partnership’’ is the only change in the law as now generally accepted regarding the nature of a partner’s interest in the property of the firm. While this as previously explained is apparently a mere change in nomenclature, it will probably overrule some decisions that have

4 Section 38.

4 Section 41.

<br>

### --- Page 0257 ---

§36] ASSOCIATIONS FOR PROFIT [Chap. II

been much criticised," which, however, are not of especial concern in the kind of partnership discussed in this chapter.

17 If the partner is to have theoretically a property right in the property of the firm in addition to his right to an accounting and payment of his share in the net assets (which in the Code is described as his interest in the partnership as distinguished from his interest in specific partnership property) it is difficult to see how his right to share in assets unexpectedly realized upon by one partner after dissolution would be barred by the statute of limitations which bars enforcement of the right to an accounting as in Knox v. Gye, L. R. 5 H. L. 656. This is borne out by § 30, which provides that on dissolution a partnership continues "until the winding up of partnership affairs is completed."

Cuar. Tl) IN GENERAL [§ 37

CHAPTER III
BUSINESS TRUSTS

§ 87. In General

Trusts for large numbers of beneficiaries whose interests
may or may not be represented by transferable certificates
are but a natural outgrowth of simple express trusts and the
rules of law applicable to them are those which have been
established with reference to all other trusts. The possibilities of the instruments creating such trusts are infinite,
because so far there has been little attempt by legislatures to
regulate or restrict them. The possibility that it may be
held that the rule against perpetuities or the rule against
restraints upon alienation apply to trusts of the character
here considered has been fully discussed and it is usual to
avoid this possibility by appropriate limitation upon the
duration of the trust. The forms of trust deeds are, therefore, various and the construction placed upon one is not
always helpful in determining the true meaning of another.
The ones more commonly used in modern business, how-

1 Hart v. Seymour, 147 Ill. 589, 35 N. E. 246; Howe v. Morse, 174 Mass.
491, 503, 55 N. E. 213 (these were trusts for associations for profit, but the
same principle must apply to pure trusts). Gray, Perpetuities, 3d ed., § 509. See § 19.

2 A trust deed was acknowledged, delivered and accepted by the trustees in
New York where the grantor then resided. The trust estate was stock in
corporations organized in four different States. No place of performance was
designated. Held: The instrument is to be construed by the law of New York
without reference to the residence of the trustees or the later residence of the
grantor. Mercer v. Buchanan, 132 Fed. 501 (C. C. - Pa.).

A trust agreement is to be construed according to the law of the place where
it is to be performed or have its entire beneficial operation rather than the
place of the contract. McLoughlin v. Shaw, 95 Conn. 102, 111 Atl. 62.

A holding trust deed was interpreted to confer on the trustees the power to
sell stock as well as buy it. Not accountable if act in good faith. Gould v.
Head, 41 Fed. 240 (C. C. - Col.).

Trustee held authorized to invest in real estate and erect and repair buildings on it, not to complete buildings on land of another. Digney v. Blanchard,
226 Mass. 335, 115 N. E. 424.

<br>

### --- Page 0259 ---

§37] BUSINESS TRUSTS (Chap. II

ever, fall into a few well-recognized classes, each having certain characteristics.

There are still other business relations which should properly be classified as trusts, but which have not yet been so defined by the courts. The relation between underwriters of securities and the managers of the syndicate, for example, has usually been deemed that of principal and agent. In underwriting agreements as formerly drawn this was probably the correct conclusion and the cases are therefore considered with other "unassociated groups" in Chapter IV. The members of these syndicates, however, do not contemplate incurring the liability of a principal for acts of their managers, nor do they need to exercise the control over their managers that a principal has over his agent. In the more recent underwriting agreements the transaction really takes the form of a trust in which the manager holds the funds, or rather the obligations of the subscribers to furnish funds, as trustee for them and contracts for their benefit, but as a principal holding legal title and not as an agent.

There have been occasional instances in the reports of trusts under wills empowering the trustee to continue the testator’s share in a partnership for the benefit of certain beneficiaries. These cases are cited as authorities in cases involving the more formal trusts for numerous beneficiaries.

The distinction between partnership associations and pure trusts has already been discussed at length. It is sufficient here to say that the essential elements of a partnership which are lacking in pure trusts are the element of association and the right on the part of the beneficiaries to direct and control the trustees in the management of the estates. The cases involving trusts organized for the purpose of conducting a business or for the purpose of holding securities or property as an investment for numerous beneficiaries.

* See §§8 and 50.

Jones v. Gould, 209 N. Y. 419, 424, 426, 103 N. E. 720. See § 53, note 3.

* Burwell v. Mandeville’s Exec., 2 How. 560; Ex parte Garland, 19 Ves. 110; Ex parte Richardson, 3 Mad. Ch. 79; Re Johnson, 15 Ch. D. 548.
See § 14.

<br>

### --- Page 0260 ---

Chap. III] IN GENERAL [§37

Beneficiaries whose interests are represented by transferable shares, so far as they relate to the nature of the organization are not repeated here.

The law of corporations has been obliged to deal with the problem of the 'one man company,' in which the sole proprietor of a business enterprise seeks the benefit of limitation of liability by organizing a corporation with the assistance of members of his family or friends, whose shares are paid for by him and in reality belong to him. The courts have generally upheld the validity of the incorporation; if the pertinent statutes have been literally complied with.' A somewhat similar problem may well arise in relation to the shares in business trusts whether the organization is a pure trust or a partnership. In the only case on this subject which has been noted, the facts stated in the report show plainly that the trustee was in fact the sole beneficiary. He had bargained for the purchase of certain land and had caused an agreement and declaration of trust to be executed between himself as trustee and four beneficiaries under the name of the "Marblehead Associates" for the purpose of developing and disposing of the land. Four of his friends signed the agreement and subscribed for shares, but they paid no money and took no part in the affair except forthwith to endorse to him the four certificates of beneficial interest issued in their names. The title to the land was conveyed to him as trustee under the declaration of trust. He used the certificates of beneficial ownership together with other securities of his own as pledges to secure notes signed by him as trustee and by him personally. The land scheme proved a failure and a personal creditor of the trustee attached the land as belonging to the defendant but standing in the name of the defendant as trustee. The court held that the attachment was valid saying, 'the consideration for the conveyance of the real estate was all paid by Wainwright. The record title was in him as trustee. All the certificates of beneficial interest in the trust were endorsed to him. No one else had paid anything for them.

7 Machen, Corporations, Chap. XVII.

<br>

### --- Page 0261 ---

§ 37) BUSINESS TRUSTS (Cap. III

So far as they were paid for at all they were paid for by him. No other person claimed any title to them as owner. He dealt with them as absolute owner. He had the general title to them even though he had pledged the title to them as security for money borrowed—he therefore was both trustee and sole beneficiary. He united in himself all legal and equitable interests arising out of the transaction. Both the legal and equitable title merged in him. “It was at most a mere naked trust which Wainwright was entitled to terminate for his own benefit at any moment and thereby to vest in himself the apparent legal as well as the beneficial and equitable title. It is of no consequence how many agreements, declarations of trust, or other documents may be used, the truth of the matter is all that need be considered. “It is not possible for a debtor to screen his property from his creditors or place it beyond the reach of direct attachment for his personal debt by the adoption of any such business contrivance as that here disclosed.”

It is believed that this decision will meet with approval. It is plain that where the trustee is also the sole beneficiary a merger results whether the right of the beneficiary is regarded as an equitable estate or as a claim against a trustee enforceable in equity. Difficulty will arise, however, in drawing a sound line between cases as plain as this and others which may be imagined. It has been suggested that a trustee should not be allowed to be a shareholder at all, but there seems no reason for the development in the case of Cunningham v. Bright, 228 Mass. 385, 117 N. E. 909. Three trustees were the three beneficiaries. Held: Merger. Greene v. Greene, 125 N. Y. 506, 510.

A holder of all the bonds cannot be appointed trustee under the mortgage when the original trustee has resigned. Re Wm. Radam etc. Co., 97 N. Y.S. 76 (App. Div.); See Simonds v. Rowe, 180 N. Y. S. 681, affd. 185 N. Y. 8. 954; Re May’s Estate, 185 N. Y. 8. 284

A declaration of trust giving the trustee absolute control and dominion over the property without accountability to any one is void. There must be beneficiaries, Axtell v. Coons, 89 So. 419 (Fla.).

See Pitney, J., in Chicago etc. Ry. v. Des Moines etc. Ry, 254 U. S. 196, 208, 65 L. ed. 219, 41 S. Ct. 81. An obscure case holds defendants partners though they had recorded a deed of trust with transferable shares on the ground that “they never held themselves out as doing business thereunder.” This is apparently an inverted estoppel; shall we call it a “retrostoppel.” Hayes Co. v. Wolff, 175 Wis. 501, 185 N. W. 512.

<br>

### --- Page 0262 ---

Cuapr. IIT} IN GENERAL [§ 37

business trusts of an exception to the general rule which permits one of several beneficiaries under a trust to serve also as trustee. It has been further suggested that if a trustee owns a majority of the shares in a trust, he, in fact, as beneficiary, controls the trustees and under the distinction established in Williams v. Milton and Crocker v. Malley the supposed trust becomes a partnership even though the beneficiaries have no right to elect or remove trustees. This reason does not necessarily apply to a case where there is more than one trustee, for the one who owns a majority of the shares and “controls” himself as trustee would not merely for that reason control his co-trustees. In a case where there is but a single trustee who owns a majority of the shares, but in which there are other real beneficiaries holding a minority of the shares, must we say that it is a partnership, merely because the holder of a majority of the shares is the sole trustee? The answer seems to be that the test, as laid down in the cases above referred to, is the right to control rather than the exercise of that power in fact. We must therefore confine ourselves to the rights created by the instrument of trust in determining what its nature is. Otherwise we may have an organization changing from trust to partnership and back again with the daily changes of share ownership. How the shares are owned and who in fact directs its operations is immaterial provided there are real beneficiaries in addition to the sole trustee. In the Cunningham case the court looked beyond the instrument itself, not for the purpose of determining whether it was a partnership or a trust, but to find out if there had been a merger of the legal and equitable title by their being vested in the same person. This would always have to be ascertained outside the instrument in question.

* Ogilby v. Hickok, 128 N. Y. 8. 860, affd. 202 N. Y. 614.

In Art. 7 of the Capital stock tax regulations of 1920 issued by the U. S. Dept. of Internal Revenue appears the following: “Where the trustees hold in their own right a sufficient number of the certificates of beneficial interest to constitute control as between the beneficiaries, the trust will be held to be an association regardless of the powers conferred upon the trustee by the instrument creating the trust.” See also other regulations and decisions of the Department of Internal Revenue § 14, notes 15 and 31.

<br>

### --- Page 0263 ---

§37) BUSINESS TRUSTS (Chap. III

The issue to contributors by a real estate promoter of syndicate receipts providing for a future declaration of trust or incorporation, neither of which was ever carried out, was a sufficiently definite description of the beneficiaries and their contributing interest.

An unusual instance of a trust for numerous beneficiaries whose interests are represented by transferable certificates involves a trust within a trust. It has arisen in the course of the management of numerous trusts under wills or inter vivos by incorporated trust companies. Though these professional trustees scrupulously plan their bookkeeping so that the affairs of the various trusts shall be kept wholly distinct, they have found it convenient to combine funds of various small trusts in a common investment usually in mortgages of real estate. This is often highly advantageous to such small trusts which cannot readily obtain profitable and safe mortgages. In some of the earlier instances the mortgage was taken in the name of the trust company "as Trustee," but without designating the particular trusts or their respective interests. A more elaborate plan provided for the creation on its books of a "general mortgage fund" or other similar account of which the trust company was technically, if not ostensibly, trustee and under which it issued certificates of participation in the name of each trust whose funds were used for the purpose. Mortgages were then taken in the name of the Trustee and earmarked as belonging to this fund or account. The certificates of participation were cancelled, transferred and reissued from time to time as the exigencies of the business required. One of these methods of investing trust funds has been held invalid because in effect it substituted an obligation of the trustee for a specific earmarked investment in the name of each individual trust.

It should be borne in mind that the laws of the different States are not uniform as to the validity of trusts. For

11 Bryant v. Shaw, 180 N. Y. S. 301, 303 (App. Div.).
12 Union Trust Co., In re, 219 N. Y. 514, 517, 519, 521, 114 N. E. 1057. See
also Barry v. Lambert, 98 N. Y. 300, 308.

### --- Page 0264 ---

Chap. III. IN GENERAL (§ 37

example, there are no trusts in the sense of the common law in Louisiana." Certain trusts for a term of years are invalid in New York." The trusts which gave their name to the Anti-Trust legislation at the close of the 19th century were held illegal since designed to maintain prices, or because they involved a violation of the law of corporations by the corporations composing them."

If a trust with transferable shares is illegal, the question at once arises, what is the legal relation of trustee and beneficiary? In a lower court of New York it was held that the trust being illegal, the beneficiaries became tenants in common of the trust res, who were entitled to maintain partition or otherwise exercise ownership." It would seem also to follow that if the trust were wholly void because prohibited by statute and the beneficiaries became tenants in common of the fund, the trustee in his acts in relation to the fund must have acted as the agent of the beneficiaries since he could not act as their trustee. The court of appeals of New York, however, does not accept that line of reasoning. In a case in which it was contended that the trust was prohibited by statute and that the beneficiaries therefore became liable as principals for the acts of the trustee as their agent, the court said "but lastly, even if we should adopt the plaintiff's view upon both of the foregoing points, we do not see how it would carry him forward to any position of success. The subscribers placed their money in the hands of Megargel under an authority contained solely and exclusively in this trust agreement. That was his only warrant for investing the moneys which he had received as special capital in the partnership. If it now should be held that this instrument was void and conferred no such authority, we fail to see how the relationship of principal and agent

13 'Fidei commissa, the trusts of the English law, cannot be created in Louisiana and enforced in our courts.' Partee, Trustee v. Successor of Hill, 12 La. An. 767; Marks v. Loewenberg, 78 So. 444, 448 and cases cited.

14 Greene v. Greene, 125 N. Y. 506, 510, 26 N. E. 739. See § 19 notes 8 and m. Bishop v. Am. Preservers' Co., 157 Ill. 284, 312, 41 N. E. 765.

4 See § 11, notes 2 and 3.
1 Bryant v. Shaw, 180 N. Y. S. 301, 303 (App. Div.).

### --- Page 0265 ---

§37) BUSINESS TRUSTS (Chap. III

would be established in respect of the contribution of this
capital or how an unlawful trustee can be turned into a
lawful agent. It would seem under such a contingency as
that that Megargel would have in his hands some money
contributed by the subscribers without any authority for
its use and the result would be either that he might retain
the moneys or that the subscribers might disaffirm everything that he had done with it and demand its immediate
re-payment." 18

If a trust is valid in the State where it is created will it be
enforced in another State where such trusts are illegal? These
questions have usually arisen with reference to trusts under
wills. It has been generally held that such a trust of personality, valid by the law of the testator’s domicile, is valid in
a State in which it is to be administered, although illegal if
created by one domiciled in that State.19 It has been further
held that a trust invalid where created, but valid where it is
to be administered will be upheld in the jurisdiction where
it was made.20 In considering a trust inter vivos the New
York court laid down the following rules. A trust of
personalty valid in the State or county where it is created is
valid in New York although the property is physically in
New York and the trust is to be administered there. A
trust of personalty to be carried from New York and paid to
foreign trustees to be administered in another State where it
is valid, is valid in New York. A trust of personalty made
in New York and to be administered there or a trust of realty
situated in New York is to be tested by the law of New
York.21

18 Crehan v. Megargel, 234 N. Y. 67, 136 N. E. 296.

19 Jones v. Habersham, 107 U. S. 174, 27 L. ed. 401; Fellows v. Miner, 119
Mass. 541; Sohier v. Burr, 127 Mass. 221; Sewall v. Wilmer, 132 Mass. 131;
Cross v. U. S. Trust Co., 131 N. Y. 330, 349, 30 N. E. 125.

20 A trust invalid where created, but valid where to be administered will be
upheld where made. Hope v. Brewer, 136 N. Y. 126, 143, 32 N. E. 558.

21 Peabody v. Kent, 138 N. Y. S. 32, 35.
