### --- Page 0266 ---

Chap. III] TRUSTS FOR CREDITORS [§ 38

trust executed and deposited in the State of Massachusetts
where the law has now become fairly well settled. It is
believed by those who have adopted this course that the
doctrine that a trust valid where made will be enforced in a
State which otherwise would not recognize it will prove
effective for their purposes. Thus a trust for a term of years
to be enforced in New York could probably be created under
a Massachusetts declaration of trust, although it might be
illegal in New York. These instruments also provide that
they are to be interpreted and given effect to in accordance
with the laws of Massachusetts. It is questionable, however,
how far the courts will apply the general rule, if it should be
established that none of the parties to the declaration of
trust are really domiciled in Massachusetts and the delivery
and deposit in Massachusetts is merely a device for the
purpose of accomplishing the above result. There were
obvious reasons for executing in New York the declaration
of trust of the Great Northern Ore certificates since two of
the trustees resided there and meetings of the trustees were
held there, yet the Supreme Court of Minnesota where most
of the business of the trust was conducted and where the
other trustees lived and the ore properties were located
suggests that it is doubtful by the law of which State the
validity of the trust agreement should be determined.”*

§ 38. Trusts for Creditors

An instance of trusts of this sort is that created by an
assignment for the benefit of creditors. It frequently
happens that the assignee is empowered to run a business
and apply its profits to the liquidation of the claims of
creditors. Even where the deed of assignment purports to
give the assignee this power to continue the business he
should, for his own protection, obtain the consent of creditors or he may be held personally liable for losses. Such

33 In re Thorne’s Estate, 145 Minn. 412, 177 N. W. 638.

1 Hill v. Cornwall, 95 Ky. 526; Cooper v. Lankford, 117 Ky. 792, 78 S. W.
197; Wilhelm v. Byles, 60 Mich. 561, 567, 27 N. W. 847; Quimby v. Uhl, 130
Mich. 198, 211, 89 N. W. 722; Brown’s Estate, 193 Pa. St. 281, 44 Atl.
443.

<br>

### --- Page 0267 ---

BUSINESS TRUSTS

assent may be found from execution of a deed of assignment containing such a provision. In some cases the agreement for this purpose which is signed by the creditors creates what is really a partnership," but in most instances it will be found that the creditors are simply beneficiaries acting as individuals and not really as a group or association. "The profits which the trustee applies to their claims are the debtors' profits, not theirs. They are not the proprietors of the business. They have not the right to direct and control the acts of the trustee as a principal would an agent. The trust is really created as security for their claims." *

§ 39. Car Trusts

Another form of trust with transferable shares familiar to investors is the so-called "Car Trust." This is a method of financing the purchase of rolling stock of a railroad by giving a first lien on the rolling stock when purchased and thus preventing it from coming under the lien of the general railroad mortgages. They have usually been organized so as to be held to be not partnerships.

2 Creditors of an insolvent business had it transferred to trustees who carried it on for their benefit to pay off their claims and then turn it over to the original owners. The net income which was to be distributed was always to be regarded as the property of the old firm. A majority of the creditors could make rules for the conduct of the business and decide to discontinue it. An action was brought on bills of exchange accepted by an agent of the trustees against certain creditors who had signed the indenture of trust. Held: Not a partnership because no mutual agency of the certificate holders. Cox v. Hickman, 8 H. L. C. 268. The definition of partnership announced in this case (known as the mutual agency test) has been generally repudiated.

+ Smith v. Williams, 178 Ill. 420, 53 N. E. 358; Sterns Co. v. Williams, 178 Ill. 626, 53 N. E. 361; Wells-Stone Mercantile Co. v. Grover, 7 N. D. 460, 465, 75 N. W. 911; Re Stanton Iron Co., 21 Beav. 164.

1 Railroad directors and promoters formed car trusts. There was a trustee who issued certificates in each trust to the subscribers. Brown, J., said, "The car trust associations were not corporations or partnerships nor legal entities of any description, but were simply car trust certificates in the hands of various persons who were represented by the petitioner, McGourkey." Held on peculiar facts of case that the purchases were really made by the corporation and not by the car trusts, and the rolling stock came under the general mortgage. McGourkey v. Toledo Co., 146 U. S. 536, 557, 36 L. ed. 1079, 13 S. Ct. 170.

Trustees of a car trust signed a second lease with the railroad reducing the rate of interest. They signed as trustees but expressly said they executed only on behalf of such certificate holders as should consent, etc. Some did not

<br>

### --- Page 0268 ---

Chap. III] TRUSTS FOR FINANCING CORPORATIONS [§40

§ 40. Trusts for Financing Corporations

Trusts under mortgages executed by corporations to secure issues of bonds are, in some aspects, examples of the sort of trust under consideration. Ordinarily the functions of the trustee are purely formal and the real business relation exists between the mortgagor corporation and the bondholders as creditors. With this relation we have no concern.1 In the relation of the trustee to the bondholders will be found sometimes illustrations of the law applicable to trusts conducting business enterprises.2 When such a trustee gets absolute title by entry and possession for the statutory period, he may execute the trust by liquidating and dividing the net proceeds among the bondholders.* In one case he was held justified in leasing the road instead of liquidating.3 It sometimes happens that upon a foreclosure the trustee is obliged to take possession and operate the business for a time, though he usually does this through a receiver appointed by the court.

During foreclosures or reorganizations it is customary for different classes of security-holders to organize “pro-consent and the trustees sued on the old lease for the higher rate for them, the lower rate having been paid the others. Held: No inconsistency in allowing recovery on the old lease in this way. It does not matter what authority the trustees had to bind the minority when they did not purport to exercise it. “In our view whether the car trust is a partnership or a joint stock association or a quasi-corporation is not of the least importance.” Humphrey v. N. Y., etc. R. R. Co., 121 N. Y. 435, 447, 24 N. E. 695.

Where the trust deed provided for a real association, it was held to be a partnership. Ricker v. American L. & T. Co., 140 Mass. 346, 5 N. E. 284.

1 In this treatise we are not concerned with the mutual obligations between the trustees and the corporation or between individual bondholders and the corporation. For a full discussion of the subject see Jones on Corporate Mortgages and Bonds.

2 When the trustee is to certify bonds and superintend the sale of them and the application of the proceeds and to do other things involving discretion, it constitutes the transaction of business and when done in a State by a foreign corporation subjects it to regulation as such. Farmer’s Co. v. Lake Street Ry. Co., 173 Ill. 439, 51 N. E. 55.

A by-law authorising bondholders to vote for directors was held invalid as contrary to the statutes providing for vote by stockholders. The interests of stockholders and bondholders are diverse. Durkee v. People, 155 Ill. 354, 40 N. E. 626.

3 Haven v. Grand Junction Co., 12 Allen 337.

4 Sturges v. Knapp, 31 Vt. 1.

<br>

### --- Page 0269 ---

$40 BUSINESS TRUSTS { Chap. II

tective committees” in order that prompt action may be taken when the consent of the security-holders is needed. Under formal agreements the stocks or bonds are deposited with the committee or its agent, title to the securities usually vests in the committee as trustee and negotiable receipts or certificates are issued in their place. The terms of these depositary agreements vary so that it is hard to generalize about them. It has been held, however, that bonds deposited with a reorganization committee with broad powers cannot be withdrawn after the foreclosure sale, since under the depositary agreement they had been irretrievably subjected to use on the purchase price. The same case held that though it is the best practice to submit to the bondholders a reorganization plan before foreclosure, failure to do it was not a defect in the foreclosure.

Subscription agreements to aid in financing embryonic or feeble corporations are frequently called “syndicate agreements.” Similar agreements to purchase and resell or guarantee the sale of corporate securities have borrowed from insurance the designation of ‘underwriting.’ The relations resulting from such agreements are usually in substance, if not in form, trusts with transferable shares, since the legal title to that for which the subscriptions are expended is vested in the ‘Syndicate Managers’ and transferable certificates or receipts are issued by the managers evidencing the interest of the subscribers.

As these are intended to be temporary arrangements, there is seldom any agreement for meetings or other organi-

* Fidelity Trust Co. v. Washington-Oregon Corp., 217 Fed. 558, 593 (D. C.—Wash.); Mills v. Potter, 189 Mass. 238.

* United States & Mexican Trust Co. v. Same, Trustee et al, 250 Fed. 377, 381 (C. C. A.—Kans.). Bondholders who had deposited their bonds with a protective committee cannot complain that the rate of interest charged on a loan to the committee was usurious because they were not parties to the contract and by the agreement the committee was forbidden to make them parties, but was authorized to borrow and pledge the bonds. St. Paul Co. v. American Trust Co., 222 S. W. 137 (Mo.).

1 The syndicate manager was deemed an agent in Heckscher v. Edenborn, 203 N. Y. 210, 222. Complete copy of such an agreement is printed in Bradley v. Borden, 223 Mass. 575, 578, 112 N. E. 416. Compare the cases of informal subscription agreements considered in § 2 n. 4, ef seg.

<br>

### --- Page 0270 ---

Chap. II] TRUSTS FOR FINANCING CORPORATIONS (§ 40

organization and the fullest powers of control of the assets are vested in the managers, so that it is rare that these agreements constitute partnerships.®

In one case in which it was not necessary to determine “the precise technical character of the managers, whether they were trustees or agents,” the court comments upon the loose use of the term syndicate and underwriting as follows.

“The rights of the holders of the syndicate receipts depend upon the terms of the ‘underwriting agreement.’ The subscribers are referred to as forming a ‘syndicate.’ The promoters are termed ‘syndicate managers’ and the venture is called a ‘syndicate’. These words are employed widely in the dialect of finance. The word ‘underwriting’ simply means that the persons joining in the scheme agree to furnish the necessary money and to take the shares which cannot be sold to outsiders. A syndicate in this connection means an association of persons with a community of interest in the fund raised for the purpose of carrying on the particular undertaking. The members share the profits and bear the losses in proportion to their respective interests. The underwriting syndicate was simply an association of those who furnished the money to make the purchase with an agreement that the managers should have full control of the venture and divide the proceeds or assets among the members of the association in proportion to their financial interests. So far as concerned each other their relations and rights were analogous to those of co-partners. Hambleton v. Rhind, 84 Md. 456, 487, 36 Atl. 597. Tyser v. Shipowner’s Syndicate, [1896] 1 Q. B. 135. Morrison v. Earls, 4 Ont. 434, 476. Their powers with respect to each other were not those of partners. Indeed it is expressly provided in the agreement that the subscribers shall not be partners. But respecting the vital point of sharing profits and bearing losses in proportion to investment, the association bears the earmarks of a partnership. But whether as between

*One member of a syndicate is not chargeable with notice of what another knew about the rights of the plaintiff in stock purchased. Lougee v. Picktell, 250 Fed. 737, 738 (C. C. A—Ky.).

<br>

### --- Page 0271 ---

$40) BUSINESS TRUSTS (Cuar. I

themselves they were simply beneficiaries under a trust or quasi partners is not involved in the issues here presented. The holders of syndicate receipts or certificates get no mysterious advantage out of the use of these phrases.

It is submitted that there is no element of association in such agreements and that so far as the subscribers bear a relation to each other it is not “analogous to those of co-partners.” Though the subscribers share profits and bear losses, they do not intend to make themselves personally liable for losses, as do partners, but to risk only the amount of their contribution. In any event the sharing of profits and losses is no longer accepted as a conclusive test of partnership. There is certainly nothing in these agreements which justifies a finding of partnership by estoppel, and unless that can be found, it is submitted that their relation to third parties is precisely the same as their real relation to each other and it is wholly unnecessary and confusing to give encouragement to novel sub-classifications by the use of the words “quasi partners” or “joint adventurers.” The full text of the syndicate agreement in question in Minot v. Burroughs is not printed in the report but appears in full in the original papers in the case. It is in the usual form and contains no provision for meetings or other joint action between subscribers and the title to the shares is to be vested in the managers.

It is usually provided in these agreements that a transferee of a syndicate receipt thereby becomes a party to the original agreement. This cannot literally be correct, for a contract obligation cannot thus be imposed on one by a mere transfer of title without more. He doubtless takes his title however subject to the fulfillment of such obligations, and by causing the certificate he buys to be transferred.

Minot v. Burroughs, 223 Mass. 595, 602, 112 N.E. 620.

See Minot v. Burroughs, 223 Mass. 595, 606, 112 N.E. 620. One who bought certificates in an illegal trust, which certificates contained a stipulation binding him as their holder to all the terms of the trust agreement “voluntarily made himself a party to a scheme under an executed agreement, which he alleges to have been illegal and how can he now say that it is one which he can disaffirm and as to which upon the plea of disaffirming he can ask a court of equity to intervene and compel in his favor an accounting and distribution by.

<br>

### --- Page 0272 ---

Cuap. III] TRUSTS FOR FINANCING CORPORATIONS [§ 40

ferred and a new one issued in his name he may well be held to have bound himself in contract.

A promoter of a syndicate owes the same fiduciary relation to it as a promoter to a corporation. “If one asks another to trust him, he assumes the position of a trustee for many purposes by the act of asking.” The same court said that the relation arose when he invited others to come into the scheme and that it followed that he owed a duty to disclose secret profits. The mere fact that a promoter disclosed that he was buying from himself was held not enough. The court said he should have disclosed his 100% profit, whereas “the document contains an apparatus of words which diverts attention from the thought of the vendor’s profit and creates by suggestion the belief” that he was to make his profit by the purchase of other stock. The following language of the court is also interesting:

“As for the existence of a confidential relation at the time of purchase, it is to be remembered that the Collins-Megargel contract was made in contemplation of this syndicate, both were parts of the launching of the Glenrock Company, and the creation of the company (and therefore of the stock) was simultaneous with the syndicate agreement, whereof one object was to get the money wherewith to pay for the stock.

“Time here, merely as time, is not of the essence, and we think it clearly shown that the object of the contract was to give a coating of legality to a preconceived intent (1) immediately to sell to the syndicate at a profit of 100% and (2) to conceal the profit from the syndicators. The profit plainly depended on successful concealment; the trusteeship was sought to get the profit. Under such facts, the whole transaction must be regarded as unitary, and defendant the trustees?” He is in pari delicto. Relief cannot be granted without recognizing the agreement. Unckles v. Colgate, 148 N. Y. 529, 537, 43 N. E. 59. See Heckecher v. Edenborn, 203 N. Y. 210, 224, 96 N. E. 441.

Trust receipts issued by a bank against shares of stock deposited with it are not negotiable instruments. Hearne v. Gillette, 91 So. 634 (La.).

Gates v. Megargel, 266 Fed. 811, 817 (C.C.A.—N. Y.) (Text of syndicate agreement appears at page 813.) Certiorari denied, 41 Sup. Ct. 13. Reported in the lower court as Gregg v. Megargel, 248 Federal, 960, 963, 254 Federal 7%.

<br>

### --- Page 0273 ---

§ 40] BUSINESS TRUSTS [Chap. III

held as a fiduciary ab initio, because he agreed to take stock only to pass it along to himself as trustee. Since liability grows out of duties equitably imposed by a voluntarily assumed relation, it may be said generally that one who seeks or creates an agency or trusteeship or any fiduciary position, for the purpose and with the intent of secretly profiting therefrom, is not acting in good faith, and from the time he forms the intent, occupies such a position that any profits resulting from his dealings with the concern whose agency he seeks must be accounted for.

In some cases the court has not attempted to distinguish between concealment by syndicate managers before the organization of the syndicate and concealment after the formal trusteeship has been created, but has ruled that the fiduciary relation existed as between members of the syndicate. If in fact the syndicate in question was a partnership, this ruling would be correct, but not if the members were mere beneficiaries of a trust. In fact, however, in almost all these cases, the one on whom the fiduciary relation was imposed was the syndicate manager or trustee. It is of course, well settled that the syndicate manager must account for secret profits.

§ 41. Voting Trusts

Voting trusts of stock in corporations are a form of trust in which the beneficial interest is frequently represented by transferable certificates similar to the certificates of stock which have been deposited by the shareholders with the voting trustee and for which they are substitutes. The trust is an active one because the trustee through his voting

18 Gates v. Megargel, 266 Fed. 811, 819. (C.C.A.—N.Y.)

4 Longworth v. Cavanaugh, 228 S. W. 83, 92. (Mo.)

1 M. got an option on a mine at $3000. He formed a syndicate to buy it at $60,000, he to pay one-half. After the payment his $3000 was returned. It was worth more than $6000. Plaintiffs seek to recover the secret profit. Held: M. really contributed nothing so his stock in the corporation that was formed should be canceled and the others left sole owners. Dissent that M. should only account for his profit to the syndicate in which he himself should share. Plaintiffs are not seeking rescission, but a secret profit by a fiduciary. Moe v. Lowry, 69 Col. 371, 194 Pac. 363.

<br>

### --- Page 0274 ---

Cuap. III] VOTING TRUSTS (§41

power controls the policy of the corporation and the trust is usually formed to insure a consistent policy on the part of the corporation for a certain period for the benefit of its other security holders. The duties of the trustee, however, are more limited than in most of the other forms of trust under consideration. A voting trust is legal from the standpoint of the law of corporations if the holders of all the shares have an equal privilege (after fair information) of availing themselves of the trust agreement, if they shall so choose, and if the object of the trust is the equal benefit of all shares. Even control of the vote by third parties has been upheld. The holder of the voting trust certificate and not the trustee is treated as the real owner of the shares for purposes of taxation, and for enforcement of a statutory right to sue for a winding up. A transfer of stock to a creditors’ committee to act as voting trustee was not such an assignment as to constitute an act of bankruptcy. Interesting questions have arisen as to the application of stock transfer taxes. Where the trustees issued certificates as directed by the vendor of the property for which the shares were issued the issue by the trustees was held taxable as a transfer. So was the final transfer restoring the stock to the certificate holders.

1 Warren v. Pim, 66 N. J. Eq. 353, 59 Atl. 773. Such agreements were upheld in Venner v. Chicago City Ry. Co., 258 Ill. 523, 538, 101 N. E. 949; Brightman v. Bates, 175 Mass. 105, 55 N. E. 809; Lord v. Equitable Soc., 194 N. Y. 212, 238, 87 N. E. 443; Boyer v. Nesbitt, 227 Pa. St. 398, 76 Atl. 103; Carnegie Trust Co. v. Security Co., 111 Va. 1, 68 S. E. 412. Contra English v. Rosenkrants, 111 S. E. 198 (Ga.); Sheppard v. Power Co., 150 N. C. 776, 64 S. E. 894. See Cone v. Russell, 48 N. J. Eq. 208, 21 Atl. 847; Woodruff v. Wentworth, 133 Mass. 309; Winsor v. Coal Co., 63 Wash. 62, 114 Pac. 908; Consolidated Corp. v. Dickey, 269 Fed. 942 (C. C. A—Ga.).

2 Elger v. Boyle, 126 N. Y. S. 946, 69 Misc. 273; Lafferty’s Estate, 154 Pa. St. 430, 26 Atl. 388; Re Pittock’s Will, 102 Ore. 47, 199 Pac. 633. But see Billings v. Marshall Co., 210 Mich. 1, 177 N. W. 222; Gage v. Fisher, 5 N. D. 297, 307, 65 N. W. 809, 813; Fennessy v. Ross, 5 App. Div. 342, 346, 39 N. Y. S. 325.

5 Re C. W. Bartelson Co., 275 Fed. 390 (D. C.—Fla.). 

6 U. S. Radiator Co. v. State of N. Y. 208 N. Y. 144, 151. 

7 Chicago Co. v. State, 189 N. Y. S. 457 (App. Div.).

### --- Page 0275 ---

$42] BUSINESS TRUSTS (Cuar, III

§ 42. Combinations

The original Standard Oil Trust, which was held in Ohio
to be a partnership,1 was held in New York not a partnership but a trust.2 It was a highly organized association with
transferable shares and on a bill in equity to compel a transfer of shares on the books of the trustees, which was defended
on the ground that the transferee was a rival oil producer
and did not seek membership in good faith, the court held
that the trustees had no more discretion than a corporation
and compelled the transfer.* It would seem that the desire
of the court to enforce the transfer and to avoid a supposed
obstacle in the familiar rule of delectus personae applicable to
ordinary partnerships, led it into the declaration that this
association was not a partnership.

§ 43. Rights of Creditors

The important question in regard to all these forms of
organization is as to the rights of creditors in the collection
of their claims. In the absence of some stipulation in the
contract to the contrary, it is well settled that one who
contracts with a trustee has a contract right at law against
the trustee personally, since courts of law purport to ignore
the existence of trusts and the trustee does not act as agent
for his beneficiaries but is himself the principal. An action

1 State v. Standard Oil Co., 49 Ohio St. 137, 176, 30 N. E. 279.

1 Rice v. Rockefeller, 134 N. Y. 174, 179, 31 N. E. 907.

3 Ibid.

1 See nn. 32 and 33.

2 Taylor v. Davis, 110 U. S. 330, 334, 28 L. ed. 163, 48 S. Ct. 147; American,
etc. Co. v. Converse, 175 Mass. 449, 56 N.E. 594; Philip Carey Co. v. Pingree,
223 Mass. 352, 111 N. E. 857; Feldman v. Preston, 194 Mich. 352, 160 N. W.
655, 658; Fay v. Day, 183 N. W. 565 (Neb.). Roger Williams Bank of Groton
Co., 16 R. I. 504, 17 Atl. 170; Ex parte Garland, 10 Ves. 110, 118 (trustee carrying on business under directions in will); Cutbush v. Cutbush, 1 Beavan 184
(ditto); Lucas v. Williams, 4 D. F. & J. 438 (ditto); Fenwick v. Laycock, 2
Q. B. 108 (ditto); Re Evans, 34 Ch. D. 597, 600 (ditto).

In actions on notes of trustees, it has been held that the addition of the word
“trustee” to the name of the maker was merely descriptio personae. Comes v.
Clark, 12 Cal. 168; Fiske v. Eldridge, 12 Gray 474; New v. Nicholl, 73 N. Y.
127. So of a guaranty under seal. Carr v. Leahy, 217 Mass. 438, 440, 105
N. E. 445.

Whether a trustee for creditors and plaintiff intended credit to be given to

<br>

### --- Page 0276 ---

Chap. III] RIGHTS OF CREDITORS [§43

at law against the trustees of a real estate trust was brought
by a creditor and an attachment of real estate of the trust
was attempted. Later the trust became insolvent and was
placed in the hands of a receiver. The attaching creditor
sought by petition to have his claim allowed in priority. This
was refused on the ground that on no theory had a valid
attachment been made. The court said: "The trustees held
the legal title to all the property and they alone could make
contracts. Ordinarily, in the absence of special limitations,
trustees bind themselves personally by their contracts with
third persons. Actions at law upon such contracts must be
brought against them and judgments run against them
personally. This is because the relations of the cestui que
trust to their contracts are only equitable and do not subject
them to proceedings in a court of common law and the
property held in trust is charged with equities which hold it
aloof from the jurisdiction of a court of law to take it and apply
it in payment of debts created by the trustees. Such
debts, if proper charges upon the trust estate, can be paid
from it under the authority of a court of equity . . . If the
trustees contracted in the usual way without referring to
anything which would limit the liability resulting from an
ordinary contract, they are personally liable to these peti-
tioners and judgment can be obtained and enforced against
them individually; but the trust property cannot be held
under an attachment nor sold upon an execution for their
personal debts." Where the trust itself is void by reason
of a merger of title of trustee and beneficiaries as in the case
of the so-called "one man" trust, the personal creditors of
the trustee can attach the supposed trust res as his individual
property.

The trustee may also be held personally liable for torts
committed in the execution of the trust by himself or his
agents. The liability of trustees, in cases where suits are
brought against him personally or in his representative capacity,
was a question of fact for the jury. Megowan v. Peterson, 173 N. Y. 1, 5, 65 N. E. 738.

* Hussey v. Arnold, 185 Mass. 202, 204, 70 N. E. 87.

Cunningham v. Bright, 228 Mass. 388, 117 N. E. 909.
* Negligence of servants. Parmenter v. Barstow, 22 R. I. 245, 47 Atl. 365;

<br>

### --- Page 0277 ---

§ 43] BUSINESS TRUSTS (Chap. III

more than one, has been held to be joint and several.*
A judgment at law against a trustee is not a bar to a subsequent suit in equity to reach the assets of the trust or an election to hold him individually. Stipulations in contracts by trustees exempting the trustee from personal liability have been held effective. The Supreme Court of the United States in a case of ordinary trust has said:

“When an agent contracts in the name of his principal, the principal contracts and is bound, but the agent is not. When a trustee contracts as such unless he is bound no one is bound, for he has no principal. The trust estate cannot promise; the contract is therefore the personal undertaking of the trustee.”

“Of course when a trustee acts in good faith for the benefit of the trust, he is entitled to indemnify himself for his engagements out of the estate in his hands, and for the purpose a credit for his expenditures will be allowed in his accounts by the court having jurisdiction thereof.”

“If a trustee acting for the benefit of the trust wants to protect himself from individual liability on the contract, he must stipulate that he is not to be personally responsible, but that the other party is to look solely to the trust estate.” °

A stipulation of that sort in a deed of assignment for the benefit of creditors, however, was held insufficient to exempt

Fallardeau v. Boston Ass’n, 182 Mass. 405, 65 N. E. 797; Fallardeau v. Hoar, 192 Mass. 263, 78 N. E. 456; Curry v. Dorr, 210 Mass. 430, 97 N. E. 87.

A trustee under a mortgage securing bonds while in possession and operating the business is liable for negligence like any other employer. Ballou v. Farnum, 9 Allen 47; Daniels v. Hart, 118 Mass. 543; Stratton v. European R. R., 76 Me. 269; Wright v. Railroad, 151 N. C. 529, 535, 66 S. E. 588.

*So that one whose right of indemnity might be barred could be omitted. Re Frith (1902), 1 Ch. D. 342.

7 Frost v. Thompson, 219 Mass. 360, 368, 106 N. E. 1009. See § 33, n. 19.

® Glenn v. Allison, 58 Md. 527; Shoe, etc. Bank v. Dix, 123 Mass. 148; Rand v. Farquhar, 226 Mass. 91, 115 N. E. 286. Mitchell v. Whitlock, 121 N.C. 166, 28 S. E. 292. Feldman v. Preston, 194 Mich. 352, 160 N. W. 655, 658 (Dictum). See Hussey v. Arnold, 185 Mass. 202, 204, 70 N. E. 87; Rothschild v. Kennedy, 86 Ore. 566, 169 Pac. 102 (Assignee for creditors carrying on business); see §§ 29 to 31, inclusive.

In Glenn v. Allison, supra, the agreement was implied from reference in a mortgage to the deed of trust which provided that the trustee should not be personally liable.

® Taylor v. Davis, 110 U. S. 330, 335, 28 L. ed. 163.

<br>

### --- Page 0278 ---

Chap. III] RIGHTS OF CREDITORS [§ 43

the trustee in the absence of express agreement between the trustee and creditor." A covenant by trustees, "as such trustees but not otherwise," to repay a loan is merely a covenant to repay the money out of any trust fund coming into their hands and does not impose any personal liability upon them. It has been held that when goods are sold to a trustee conducting a business with the intention on the part of the seller to hold the trust estate and not the trustee, if it later turns out that the trustee did not have authority to make the assets of the estate liable, the trustee cannot be held personally.1

It is equally well settled that one who contracts with a trustee has no right of action against the beneficiaries personally. 10

The decisions have been less clear as to the right of the creditor to reach the funds of the trust. It is plain that, in order to recover, the creditor must show that "the obligation was incurred by the trustees in the course of their duty in administering the trust out of which satisfaction is sought." *

10 James McCoy Co. v. Smith, 181 Ia. 707, 65 N. W. 88.

11 Re Robinson’s Settlement, (1912) 1 Ch. 17.

11 Michael v. Jones, 84 Mo. 578, 583; Fehlinger v. Wood, 134 Pa. St. 517, 523 (dictum).

12 Everett v. Drew, 129 Mass. 150.

Transferable scrip issued to finance an invention. Mayo v. Moritz, 151 Mass. 481, 484, 24 N. E. 1083; Preferred shares in an organization held to be a trust. R. I. Hospital Trust Co. v. Copeland, 39 R. I. 193, 98 Atl. 273.

13 A by-law of the Buena Vista Fruit Company, an association organized under a deed of trust, provided "that the treasurer shall 'make, sign, endorse and accept for and in the name and behalf of the company promissory notes, drafts and checks in the regular course of its business.' The note in suit was made in accordance with this by-law. It is not signed by the trustees, but by the treasurer of the association in its name. Article 11 of the declaration of trust provides that 'all contracts and engagements entered into by the trustees, and all conveyances and instruments executed by the trustees, shall be in the respective names of the trustees and shall provide against any personal liability on their part, and stipulate that no other property shall be answerable than the property in the hands of the trustees.' Plainly the note in suit is not the obligation of the trustees executed in accordance with the power thus conferred, but the note of the company executed by its agent pursuant to its by-laws. Neither in fact nor in legal contemplation is it the note of the trustees. It follows that the plaintiff has not brought his case within the principle of law on which his bill is framed and on which he recovered in the Superior Court, viz., that when a trustee has incurred an obligation in conducting the trust, the person to whom that obligation is due can satisfy it out of the trust

<br>

### --- Page 0279 ---

§ 43 BUSINESS TRUSTS (Chap. III)

At law it would seem that the creditor has no right to attach the trust fund, but there seems no reason why such right should not be given by proper proceedings in equity. So where a policy of mutual insurance issued by an unincorporated association provided for an action against the manager of the fund, who was not a member, which action should be conclusive against all the members, the court held that he was also trustee of a common fund and that a bill in equity would lie against him by a policy holder to reach the fund. The trustee has a right of exoneration out of the trust fund for any liabilities he has properly incurred estate. Hence the decree in favor of the plaintiff was wrong, founded as it was upon that principle of law. Frost v. Thompson, 219 Mass. 360, 106 N. E. 1009. See § 14, note 10.

Pillgrem v. Pillgrem, 18 Ch. D. 93 (trustee under will carrying on business in own name); Feldman v. Preston, 194 Mich. 352, 160 N. W. 655, 658. See Farhall v. Farhall, 7 Ch. 123.

In a few cases the trust estate has been reached in an action at law.

In an action of tort for negligence in a court having both law and equity jurisdiction, one of the defendants was a trustee operating a railroad for the benefit of creditors and he was held liable in his official capacity and the trust estate subjected to payment. The court said also that he might have been held liable personally. Wright v. Railroad, 151 N. C. 529, 535, 66 S. E. 588.

Defendants were sued as trustees in tort for negligence. Under the deed of trust they “were empowered to deal with the property as if they were absolute owners thereof. It is also provided that no responsibility whatever shall result to them by reason of misconduct of agents or employees and also ‘for negligence there shall be no liability or responsibility’ upon the part of the trustees.” Held: “It was clearly intended that the risk of any loss in this respect should be assumed and borne by the estate.” A judgment against the trustees as such was affirmed. Prinz v. Lucas, 210 Pa. St. 620, 60 Atl. 309.

Mason v. Pomeroy, 151 Mass. 164, 167, 169, 24 N. E. 202 (trustees carrying on a factory under terms of a will); Woddrop v. Weed, 154 Pa. St. 307, 26 Atl. 375 (trustee carrying on under a will a business which became insolvent).

“The general rule undoubtedly is that a trustee cannot charge the trust estate by his executory contracts unless authorized to do so by the terms of the instrument creating the trust. Upon such contracts he is personally liable and the remedy is against him personally. But there are exceptions to this general rule. When a trustee is authorized to make an expenditure and he has no trust funds and the expenditure is necessary for the protection, reparation or safety of the trust estate, and he is not willing to make himself personally liable, he may by express agreement make the expenditure a charge upon the trust estate. In such a case he could himself advance the money to make the expenditure and he would have a lien upon the trust estate, and he can by express contract transfer this lien to any other party who may upon the faith of the trust estate make the expenditure.” Trust under a will. New v. Nicholl, 78 N. Y. 127, 131.

Blanchard Co. v. Hamblin, 162 Mo. App. 242, 252, 144 S. W. 880.

<br>

### --- Page 0280 ---

Chap. III] RIGHTS OF CREDITORS : [§43

personally in the management of the trust estate." It is entirely consistent with the usual principles of equity jurisdiction to give the creditor a right to avail himself of the trustee's right against the trust fund, and there are decisions to this effect.* In the absence of agreement, however, there would be no reason for holding that such a creditor had any lien upon the trust fund. A receiver therefore, seeking to recover dividends paid by the trustee to shareholders out of the proceeds of borrowed money, though before the trust became insolvent, cannot collect either from the shareholders or the trustee. So far as he represents creditors he cannot recover because creditors have no lien and so far as he represents shareholders he cannot recover because they received the payments and so cannot complain. If the trustee is absent from the State or is insolvent (and these are the only cases in which it will be important for the creditor to have this right), the creditor should be allowed to proceed against the trust estate without joining the trustee. The creditor's right would, of course, be limited to the extent of the trustee's right in any given case and if any equity exists against the trustee in favor of the trust estate with respect to the particular right of exoneration in question, the creditor must be equally restricted. Thus the holder of a note guaranteed by the managers of an underwriting syndicate as security for which the agreements of subscribers were

$43] BUSINESS TRUSTS (Cuapr. IIE

pledged, having notice that the signature of the defendant subscriber was obtained by false representations in a prospectus, could not recover.** In some States, however, the creditor has been allowed to reach the trust fund regardless of the state of the accounts between him and the estate.**

The difficult problem arising from the practice of trustees conducting a business to stipulate against any personal liability of themselves, as well as their beneficiaries, for debts incurred in the conduct of the business, which results in an obligation not binding on any individual, has been already discussed.** The object to be attained is to devise a workable business method which shall result in an equitable lien of creditors on the assets of the trust without personal liability of any one and without interfering with free sale and transfer of the assets for the time being in the trust estate. Such an obligation can be enforced by the courts according to the intent of the parties. It has been suggested that the form of English security known as the floating charge will attain the desired result, at least until the attitude of our courts to these limitations on individual liability is more clearly defined. By conferring the benefit of such floating charge on all creditors, all questions of priorities will be eliminated. Whether such instruments are negotiable or not has also been considered.*

The beneficiaries are not necessary parties defendant in actions on contracts or torts of the trustees, when the trust instrument makes him their representative, even though the purpose of the suit is to reach the trust fund.” A court of equity will set aside preferences given by a trustee out of the trust estate after it is insolvent and distribute it equally to all creditors. One member of a syndicate is not chargeable

%§ 31, n. 11 et seg.

Cuar. III] COLLATERAL ATTACK UPON TRUSTS [§ 48a

§ 43a. Collateral Attack upon Trusts.

The appointment of a trustee by a court having jurisdiction, like any other judicial decree, cannot be attacked by any one except by appeal or some similar direct proceeding for that purpose. It is proper to speak of any attempt in other litigation to question the validity of such appointment as a collateral attack. The assertion of a group that the State has conferred on them artificial personality should be forbidden only in some appropriate direct proceeding for that purpose, such as quo warranto in the name of the attorney-general on behalf of the sovereign whose privilege has been abused. Attempts of litigants not shareholders to question the validity of incorporation has likewise been described as collateral attack. The extent to which the doctrine may properly be applied in the law of corporations has been discussed in a closely reasoned treatise.1

The doctrine now generally accepted is that a defendant cannot escape liability for debt by disputing the validity of the incorporation of the plaintiff with whom he has dealt.

It has always been the law, however, that a creditor seeking to collect his debt from individuals who have done business in association may sue them as partners and may dispute the effectiveness of their defence that their form of association was a valid protection against individual liability for the debts of the business. Thus members of associations and joint stock companies have always been held individually liable to creditors of the business like any other partners.3 So most States hold that shareholders in a defectively

the widow and other beneficiaries. The trustee did so until the business became insolvent and the trustee also. He allowed creditors to confess judgment against the trust estate by way of preference. Held: These judgments will be set aside and the trust fund administered for all the creditors of the business. A trust estate embarked in trade is liable to creditors for debt. Woddrop v. Weed, 154 Pa. St. 307, 312, 26 Atl. 375.

*® Lougee v. Pickrell, 250 Fed. 737, 738. (C. C. A.—Ky.).

1 Collateral Attack upon Incorporation, by Edward H. Warren, 20 Harvard Law Review, 456, 21 Id. 305.

3See § 24.

<br>

### --- Page 0283 ---

§ 43a ] BUSINESS TRUSTS (Chap. III

organized corporation which has not progressed far enough to become a corporation de facto are liable as partners.* Those States which have refused to impose individual liability in such cases have not questioned the right of the plaintiff to attack the alleged incorporation, but have decided that no partnership existed. Most of the cases involving the distinction between trust and partnership arose under taxation statutes, but in the few cases where creditors sought to hold the contributors to the share capital of a business trust on the ground that the control of the shareholders over the trustee made them partners as well as beneficiaries, no question was raised as to the right of the creditor to pursue them and in so doing to attack the effectiveness of the insulation against liability which they set up in their answers.

There are, however, passages in texts on trusts which affirm broadly that the validity of a trust may be questioned only by one who will be entitled to some interest in the property in case the trust is declared invalid. Most of the cases cited in support of this are not in point. Others are cases where a trustee is suing and a defendant who is otherwise liable seeks to prevent recovery by disputing the validity of the trustee’s title. Here the doctrine of corporations above referred to is properly applicable, because it is really a doctrine of broad application in the nature of an estoppel to dispute the title of one whom the defendant has previously recognized as entitled,® or one whose title in no way affects the obligation of the defendant.*

The other cases cited are instances of collateral attack on judicial appointment of trustees and not on the validity of the whole trust scheme.

In the recent case of Crehan v. Megargel ® in the Court of Appeals of New York it was held that a creditor of a Limited

### --- Page 0284 ---

Cuar. I] COLLATERAL ATTACK UPON TRUSTS [§ 43a

Partnership, whose special capital had been raised by means of a trust with transferable shares declared by the nominal special partner, could not question the validity of the trust in a suit to hold the contributors of the capital of the enterprise as partners. It had been said in the Appellate Division “Besides it is difficult to understand how the trust may be attacked collaterally by the plaintiff in this action.”*

The Court of Appeals was more explicit though it also decided the case on the ground that the trust was not a trust for years and so could not have violated the New York rule against perpetuities as claimed by the plaintiff.

“In the second place, we are unable to conclude that the plaintiff in this action can attack the validity of the trust agreement even if it be subject to successful attack. He is not a party to it. He has no interest in the property which is the subject of the trust. He is not seeking to enforce any lien thereon which is obstructed by the trust agreement. He is not defending himself against any attack which is based upon the trust agreement. He is an utter stranger to the entire proceeding, simply seeking to have an agreement held invalid in order that he may establish a personal liability against the parties who entered into it. No authority has been cited which sustains his right to make such an attack and we know of no principle which authorizes it.”

The court in this dictum may not have intended to affirm that only a trustee or beneficiary can question the validity of a trust which violates the rule against perpetuities or otherwise rebut a defence based on the affirmance of a trust which is illegal or which is combined with the relation of partnership. If limited to the facts of the instant case they simply affirmed that as the plaintiff was one stage further removed from the beneficiaries than a creditor of an association, who has dealt with its trustee, he cannot attack the trust because if it is invalid he is not a creditor of the organization which claimed to be a trust. Even as so limited, it is submitted that the conclusion is erroneous. If the defendant sets up the existence of a trust in defence of a claim that by

*Crehan v. Bailey, 192 N. Y. 8. 290, 299.

<br>

### --- Page 0285 ---

§ 48a BUSINESS TRUSTS Chap. III

furnishing capital to a partnership he became himself a partner, he must establish that a legal trust was created, otherwise the nominal trustee who was the conduit by which the capital was contributed must be deemed to have been an agent of the defendant and so have subjected him as principal to any liability he incurred. In other words, the defendant cannot set up as a protection against liability a relation to the special partner which if not agency is in violation of express statutes.

If, however, the court meant to decide that creditors of a business trust cannot contend that the trust violates the local rule against perpetuities or any other statute regulating trusts in an action to collect his debt from the contributors of the capital, a protection is given to those who invest in the shares of such trusts which it has not hitherto been supposed they possessed.

§ 44. Mutual Obligations of Trustee and Beneficiary.

The obligation of good faith on the part of the trustee toward the beneficiary is the origin of the whole law of fiduciaries, which has come to play so important a part in the adjustment of our complicated modern relations. Even a provision giving "absolute and uncontrolled discretion" to a trustee does not empower him to lend to himself. In the language of the court, "his discretion, however broad, does not relieve him from obedience to the great principles of equity which are the life of every trust." 2 He cannot make a secret profit at the expense of his beneficiary. Thus syndicate managers must make full disclosure to subscribers and must account for secret commissions * but not neces-

1 See § 33.

2 Carrier v. Carrier, 226 N. Y. 114, 125.

+ Campbell v. R. R., 1 Woods 368 (U. S.) (trustee for bondholders during foreclosure proceedings); Ashuelot R. R. v. Eliott, 57 N. H. 397 (trustee for bondholders in possession); Allison’s Estate, 183 Pa. St. 555 (Assignee for creditors). But a syndicate manager who was also a beneficiary could bid in at a judicial sale on foreclosure of a mortgage. Hardwick v. Wormsch, 180 S. W. 485 (Mo. App.).

* Heckscher v. Edenborn, 203 N. Y. 210, 222.

5 Gregg v. Megargel, 248 Fed. 960, 963. (D. C.—N. Y.)

necessarily for their entire profits. The trustee must act impartially for the equal benefit of all the beneficiaries. He has been held entitled to take measures to enforce equality among beneficiaries. On the other hand, it was not a breach of a syndicate agreement conferring the broadest possible powers on its managers, for them to "attempt to satisfy what was deemed a moral obligation and to carry out in good faith an understanding—which may have been nebulous and unenforceable and yet deemed to be in accordance with the requirements of business probity. The maintenance of an ethical standard so high as to outrun legal obligations is not incompatible with the underwriting agreement." ® Acting in good faith, a trustee for bondholders was held justified in bidding in the mortgaged property at foreclosure sale to protect the interests of the bondholders and reselling it, although the mortgage did not contain the usual provision authorizing him to do so, but subject to the right of the bondholders to hold him accountable for negligence in reselling at a loss. In a foreclosure bill, intervening bondholders asked the removal of the plaintiff trustee under the mortgage because it had become depositary under a bondholders' agreement under which a majority of the bonds had been exchanged for transferable certificates and title to the bonds deposited with the plaintiff had been vested in a committee of the bondholders as trustees for the certificate holders. The court held that under the terms of the mortgage a majority of the bondholders had the right to instruct the trustee and that this could be delegated by them to the

committee, or rather that the committee, as trustees, were

A question may arise as to the time when the fiduciary
obligation comes into existence. If the trustees of a business
enterprise directly or through an agent solicit purchasers
of the certificates representing the beneficial interest, they
will doubtless be held bound to make full disclosure to such

in one person for the success of the enterprise at a critical

whereas the subsequent purchaser is not solicited by the

mitted that this is unsound. Whether the manager is trustee or agent the relationship is the same to all members of the syndicate and there must be the same fiduciary obligation toward all. It is possible that the application of the rule may differ in certain cases. After full disclosure to original subscribers, it would be unreasonable to expect him to anticipate all transfers with a like disclosure, though he should repeat it, if the circumstances would disclose to a reasonable man that the conduct of a particular purchaser would be altered by the information.

A trustee is liable for losses due to failure to use the care of an ordinarily prudent man. The requirement in a syndicate agreement that the trustee should "endeavor in good faith to accomplish the purposes of this undertaking" did not "require sound judgment and business capacity." The trust deed usually contains a stipulation that the trustee shall be liable only for his own wilful default or neglect. These stipulations have been held valid, and he has been held not liable for failure to act through mistake or misconception of his duty. Wilful default by a trustee means "intentionally making way with the trust property, and a wilful neglect means such reckless indifference to true interests of the trust as to amount to or partake of a wilful violation of duty. The difference between neglect of duty by a trustee and a wilful neglect or default by a trustee is not unlike the difference between the liability of one who is

A clause making the trustee liable only "for the result of his own gross negligence or bad faith" did not protect him from a wilful and intentional breach of trust committed by acting plainly beyond his powers. A trustee is not liable for misconduct of a co-trustee if he himself is blameless.* It is not necessarily negligent to allow the co-trustee to handle funds alone,* but he cannot sleep on his trust.** The beneficiary is entitled to information at reasonable times as to the trust estate,* and to accounts. A subscriber to a syndicate is bound by the terms of the syndicate agreement conferring broad powers on the managers and cannot interfere before the purposes of the undertaking are accomplished and require the managers to reverse action they have taken in good faith and take new action contrary to the judgment of the managers.* There is no objection to the trustee being also interested as beneficiary in the trust estate.™ A voting trustee may sell his own stock and that is not a ground for obliging him to distribute the trust stock. The purchaser of voting trust certificates may compel the transfer of stock by the trustee* and so can the purchaser of a transferable receipt issued by a depositary who bought in at foreclosure as trustee for

But a claimant of stock deposited by another cannot compel the trustee to surrender it until the trust certificate is surrendered to him.?* Such certificates have been held not evidence of a debt and not taxable as such.*4 A transfer of voting trust certificates is taxable under the New York statute.** It is doubtful if beneficiaries can maintain partition. The usual provisions for winding up a syndicate will permit either a distribution of the assets in specie” or liquidation.** The managers of such a syndicate are entitled to a reasonable time after the time limited for termination of the syndicate to complete the winding up. Until that time elapses they are not bound to account and a suit by a subscriber is prematurely brought.®

The right of a trustee to indemnity against the trust estate has already been referred to. It is, of course, limited to obligations properly incurred in the performance of his duties. Thus a trustee running a ferry boat was not entitled to reimbursement out of the trust fund for damages recovered against him for assault and battery in ejecting passengers from the boat.‘! The trustee also has a right against his beneficiary personally for indemnity against liabilities incurred by him arising out of his ownership of the trust property, such as a liability for assessments on shares in corporations held by him in trust for a syndicate. ‘? It has been held, however, that a beneficiary was not bound by a stipulation in the declaration of trust obligating her to reimburse the trustee for certain expenditures unless she

assented to it and that if she did not execute the declaration of trust, she might show by oral evidence that her consent was absolute or conditional, that is, that she reserved the right to withdraw. If she assented without qualification she could not afterward annex a condition to her assent without the consent of the parties interested.* In other words, the issue in this case was not whether the trustee had a right of indemnity against the beneficiaries, but who were the beneficiaries.

Although a court of equity is the proper tribunal to adjust the mutual rights of trustee and beneficiary, it has been held that after the trust has been terminated by sale of the trust property under foreclosure proceedings, the trustee may bring an action of contract at law against the beneficiaries on an express agreement in the declaration of trust by the beneficiaries to reimburse the trustee for certain expenses.* A trustee in most of the United States is entitled to receive compensation for his services,* and when reasonably necessary to employ counsel and pay his charges out of the estate.* Syndicate managers, though trustees, are also obligees of the subscription agreements of the signers and may bring an action against a delinquent subscriber, though they have pledged the subscription contracts to one who loaned money to the syndicate. It is not necessary that the lender should sue. Since the contract is with the syndicate managers, it may be rescinded for their fraud on tender

§ 45. Representation of Beneficiaries by the Trustee

The ordinary rule is that the beneficiaries must be parties
to all suits in equity affecting the trust, but not to actions at
law.?. The trustee may, however, be held to represent the
beneficiaries to such an extent that a decree against him
will bind them though not formally joined as parties. It
cannot be doubted that under some circumstances a trustee
may represent his beneficiaries in all things relating to their
common interest in the trust property. He may be invested
with such powers and subjected to such obligations that
those for whom he holds will be bound by what is done

is also a contract with the other subscribers.)

case the pledgee had also made himself trustee of the loan by issuing certificates
of participation.

§45] BUSINESS TRUSTS (Chap. III

against him as well as what is done by him. The difficulty lies in ascertaining whether he occupies such a position, not in determining its effect if he does. If he has been made such a representative, it is well settled that his beneficiaries are not necessary parties to a suit by him against a stranger or to one by a stranger against him to defeat it in whole or in part. In a bill in equity to foreclose a mortgage under the Soldiers' and Sailors' Civil Relief Act when the mortgagors were trustees of a trust with transferable shares and some of the shareholders were in military service, it was held that failure to join the shareholders as parties should not prevent an order of sale. The court said "their interests are fairly and sufficiently represented by the trustees, whose duty it is to act in their interest. There is no adversary relation or action between them and the trustees." The trustee of a trust with transferable shares represents the beneficiaries where the rights between the trustee and beneficiaries are not at all involved, such as an action for breach of contract to buy all the assets of the trust. The beneficiaries are then bound by the judgment against the trustee in the absence of fraud or collusion. Provisions authorizing the trustee to sue and be sued contained in the declaration of trust of a real estate trust with transferable shares were held to make

The trustee under the usual form of mortgage to secure
bondholders in many respects is the representative of his
beneficiaries. Thus notice to him of the existence of a prior
encumbrance on the property mortgaged has been held to
be notice to the bondholders. The trustee does not adequately
represent the bondholders, however, in a suit to
obtain authority, under general equity jurisdiction over
trusts, to release some of the mortgaged property, where the
mortgage gave the trustee no power to make such release.
In such a case the court said, "All of the reasons against
allowing a trustee discretion to exercise authority not conferred
upon it are equally cogent against allowing it to
represent the cestuis que trust in a suit whose purpose is to
obtain that authority. (Citing cases.) In an action to foreclose the mortgage (Shaw v. Norfolk County R. R. Co., 5 Gray

S. 290) or, to state the proposition generally, in defending or executing the trust, the trustee represents the bondholders, who, though their rights are directly affected, are individually not necessary parties. Of the rule of allowing a few of numerous parties to represent all the court says they are not true representatives, but the court proceeds from necessity on the theory that enough are present to protect the interests of those absent and facts must be alleged in the record so that the court can decide that. "A trustee, however, is a true representative, but only within the limits of his authority." It is interesting to note that the plaintiff in this case held "certificates of beneficial interest" in certain stocks held by a trustee as security for an issue of bonds and was seeking to enforce a sale of his certificates which was contingent on the release of the securities by the trustee. The trustee, except where otherwise provided in the plainest terms, is the representative of all the bondholders, regardless of their assent or dissent, in matters of reorganization.

The trustee is the appropriate party to foreclose the mortgage. He cannot delegate this duty to the holders of a majority of the bonds and is liable to a minority for damages sustained by such a course. The trustee should conduct the foreclosure suit impartially for the benefit of all and cannot, in order to avoid removal by three fourths of the bondholders under the provisions of the mortgage, agree that counsel designated by the bondholders committee should conduct the foreclosure and such an agreement may

In a foreclosure suit by the trustee, the corporation cannot set off a claim against the individual bondholder.” Bondholders can proceed themselves against the mortgagor to protect the trust estate only if redress cannot be obtained through the trustee.* Thus it is held that if the trustee refuses or unreasonably neglects to act, bondholders may proceed independently after request to the trustee,?! or they may proceed against the trustee and compel him to act.2? A bondholders’ suit begun ten years after the bonds were overdue to impress a trust on assets of a successor corporation was barred by laches.2* When by the terms of the mortgage the trustee is entitled to indemnity against loss or expense before he can be compelled to act, and refuses to proceed without it, the bondholders must furnish it before they are entitled to act independently.*4 When, however, the trustee has taken a position where his interest conflicts with his duty as trustee, no request is needed,*® and so when

§ 46. Management of Trust Estate

Courts will not interfere with the directions prescribed in the trust deed for the management and control of the trust, unless the preservation of the estate requires it.2 In some trusts, such as trusts for bondholders the powers and duties of the trustee are usually specifically defined and limited in the trust mortgage. In syndicate and underwriting agreements, it is customary to give the managers very broad powers. The phrase sometimes used is "as broad powers as if they were the owners thereof." It has been held that "the purpose of this agreement was to confer upon the managers the widest possible powers" and that whether technically trustees or agents, their powers would be the same. "It was not necessary for the managers to get the consent of the certificate holders to any plan of action within the scope of the powers conferred upon them. The underwriting agreement expresses the idea that the whole success of the venture might depend upon a continuous concentration of absolute power within its general scope in an harmonious honest management, free from the possibility of interference from conflicting views on questions of expediency. The managers, acting in good faith, obviously were intended to be masters of the affairs."* Under such broad powers

Wheelwright v. St. Louis Co., 56 Fed. 164 (office vacant); Ettlinger v. Persian Co., 142 N. Y. 189, 36 N. E. 1055.

In California it is held that when the trustee needs act only at the request of holders of a majority of bonds and is therefore justified in refusing to act, a minority may proceed directly. Citizens Bank v. Los Angeles, 131 Cal. 187, 63 Pac. 462. See Western Penn. Hospital v. Mercantile Co., 189 Pa. St. 269, 42 Atl. 183.

For an unusual form of trust certificate in which the same rule was applied, see Land Title & Trust Co. v. Asphalt Co., 127 Fed. 1 (C. C. A.—N. J.); Land Title & Trust Co. v. Tatnell, 132 Fed. 305 (C. C. A.—N. J.).

Bondholders were allowed to intervene in a receivership without joining the trustee when he was out of the jurisdiction. Anthony v. Campbell, 112 Fed. 212.

1 Winthrop v. Attorney General, 128 Mass. 258; Pennington v. Met. Museum of Art, 65 N. J. Eq. 11, 23, 55 Atl. 468

2 Johns v. Johns, 172 Ill. 472, 50 N. E. 337.
3 Minot v. Burroughs, 223 Mass. 595, 601, 607, 112 N. E. 620; Weitse v.

<br>

### --- Page 0298 ---

Cuap. IIT] MANAGEMENT OF TRUST ESTATE [§46

It has been held that the managers might pledge certain securities of the syndicate, not in pursuance of a legal obligation, but in conformity with their ideas of business probity, the court saying that “the maintenance of an ethical standard so high as to outrun legal obligation is not incompatible with the underwriting agreement.” The same court in the same volume held that another syndicate agreement which conferred on the managers “discretion and authority—without any check or control from a superior tribunal” could not issue stock as a reward for faithful past services, such recognition of a moral obligation not being within the “purpose of the agreement.”

The trustee cannot delegate his authority, but, of course, may appoint agents to perform purely ministerial functions. He cannot form a partnership with another company and thereby divest himself of the personal duty to manage and control the business of the trust estate confided to his care. In the absence of any provisions to the contrary in the trust instrument, the trustees must act jointly in matters involving the exercise of their discretion. Subsequent implied or express ratification will make binding a contract under seal executed without formal authorization. In purely ministerial matters or in an emergency, to preserve the fund, one may act alone. Courts uphold stipulations in the trust deed giving a majority of the trustees or less than all the power to act even in matters involving discretion.

Burrage, 190 Mass. 267, 276, 76 N. E. 508. (Interpretation of a pooling agreement respecting voting trust certificates holding that the protective committee had power to give an option or contract to sell the stock and that their notice to shareholders as to the sale was sufficient.)

Bradley v. Borden, 223 Mass. 575, 590, 112 N. E. 416. For other cases relating to powers of syndicate managers see § 50 n. 2.

§46] BUSINESS TRUSTS (Cuar. TT

even in such cases it has been held that those not acting are entitled to notice of the contemplated action and an opportunity to be heard. Trustees, like individuals, partnerships and corporations may adopt for business purposes a name under which the business may be carried on.

A trustee has the right under certain circumstances to seek the advice of a court of equity as to his rights and obligations in advance of acting.14 He cannot, however, seek ratification and confirmation of his acts after he has performed them.15 This is not a means whereby the trustee can relieve himself of the entire burden of managing the trust. The requisites for a bill for instructions have been said to be "the possession of a fiduciary fund of which some disposition is necessarily to be made presently; conflicting claims or the probability thereof; and the existence of no other means of determining rights or demands so as to protect a trustee from the risks of future liability or controversy."10 A decree on such a petition for instructions, when the beneficiaries are parties, is res judicata as to them, if they later try to make the trustee account on a different basis.

A trustee may transact business in other States under the protection of the Constitution of the United States and a statute purporting to prohibit the transfer of any real or personal property in Indiana to a non-resident trustee except by will was held unconstitutional.

The distribution made by the trustees to the holders of

### --- Page 0300 ---

Chap. III. CHANGE OF TRUSTEE [§ 47

Great Northern Ore certificates has been held to be a distribution of income and not of principal under the terms of the declaration of trust.

Certain rules established by courts of equity for the protection of the interests of beneficiaries have proved inconvenient in the administration of trusts under modern business conditions. It is customary to provide expressly in the trust instrument that the trustee shall have power to change investments, that his receipts shall be a sufficient discharge to persons dealing with him and that such person shall not be bound to see to the application of purchase money to the purposes of the trust. By statute in Massachusetts those rules have now been abrogated, so that so far as business is carried on in that State the customary stipulations to avoid those rules have become unnecessary. As most business is interstate, however, it is not yet safe to omit these stipulations from the declaration of trust.

§ 47. Change of Trustee

A court of equity can always remove a trustee for cause. Assignees for the benefit of creditors have been removed for misconduct and incapacity and a successor appointed on petition of creditors for whose benefit the assignment was made. Trustees under mortgages securing bonds have been removed because of residence in a State at war with that in which the trust is being administered, and even for removal of residence to a foreign country, but not for mere temporary absence. A trustee of a voting trust has been removed for electing himself as officer at an excessive salary and he has

1 Bowdoin v. Fairchild, 103 Atl. 715 (R. I.); See Matter of Bunker, 137 N. Y. 8, 104.

2 G. L. c. 208, § 19-21.

§47] BUSINESS TRUSTS (Cap. III

been made to account for the salary.* The legislature cannot by statute substitute a new trustee, for that would impair the obligation of contract. Where the trust deed itself provides for the removal of a trustee and appointment of his successor, the beneficiaries may remove him so long as they act in good faith. Its provisions should be strictly followed or the acts of the successor may be held invalid.10

Where a will provided for the filling of vacancies by the remaining trustees and vacancies were so filled for a time, but finally the surviving trustee died without having filled vacancies, the court appointed a trustee.

It was provided in a declaration of trust that on the death of a trustee the new trustee should be chosen by the survivors by a declaration in writing signed and acknowledged by them and by the successor. One of three trustees died and no appointment of a successor was made in the form above required though a minute was made in the records of the trustees that the two survivors had agreed on the plaintiff. The court held that this record was not enough to constitute plaintiff a trustee and refused a decree for specific performance of the alleged agreement.11 A voting trustee resigned and a new one was substituted in accordance with the terms of the trust deed. There was litigation as to some stock and the trustee wished to be fully protected. He was allowed to file a bill in equity to have the court accept his resignation, pass on his accounts and appoint his successor.12 So an assignee for the benefit of creditors may be permitted

* Elias v. Schweiger, 50 N. Y. 8. 180, 27 App. Div. 69. See Lawrence v. Curtis, 191 Mass. 240, 77 N. E. 314; Barbour v. Weld, 201 Mass. 513, 87 N. E. 909.

trustees under mortgage securing bonds. Knapp v. R. R. Co., 20 Wall. 117, 122; Fletcher v. Rutland Co., 39 Vt.

Cuar. III CHANGE OF TRUSTEE [§47

by the court to resign. A new trustee may be appointed where a vacancy exists from any of the usual causes.

An important question that is frequently overlooked arises from changes of trustee pursuant to the terms of the instrument of trust, usually evidenced by recording a certificate of the remaining trustees. Does the title to real estate pass to the new trustee without a formal conveyance from the old trustees? The declaration of trust should always provide that retiring trustees may be compelled to execute appropriate conveyances. The law is well stated in the following case.

A bill in equity was brought to redeem from a tax sale. The land when taxed stood in the name of a trustee for the Oakland Park Land Company, an unincorporated association under a declaration of trust, which named the beneficiaries, who also signed the declaration. The declaration provided that on the death of a trustee his successor might be appointed by the judge of the Probate Court for the county where the land was situated, or appointed by holders of not less than a majority of the shares. The original trustee died. His successor was appointed by the Probate Court. He died and his successor was appointed by the holders of a majority of the shares. The original deed to the first trustee did not specifically refer to the declaration of trust, but recited that the consideration was paid by B, as he is trustee of a voluntary association known as the Oakland Park Land Company and the grant was to the said B, trustee, and the habendum to the said B, trustee, his successors and assigns. The court said that the conveyance was not expressed with accuracy, but its intent was not difficult to discover. The payment of the consideration by the association would raise

43 Andrews v. Wilson, 114 Ky. 671, 71 S. W. 890.

44 Brown v. Parker, 97 Fed. 446 (C. C. A—Kans.) (assignee for creditors); Claflin Co. v. Middlesex Co., 113 Fed. 958 (C. C.—Ark.) (ditto); Tuttle v. Merchants Bank, 19 Mont. 11 (ditto); Rogers v. Pell, 166 N. Y. 565, 60 N. E. 265 (ditto).

16 The court seemed to be of opinion that a formal deed was necessary from retiring trustees of the Adams Express Co., a New York joint stock association. Reffon Realty Corp. v. Adams Land & Building Co., 128 Md. 656, 662, 98 Atl. 199.

§47] BUSINESS TRUSTS (Chap. II

a resulting trust even though the grant were to B alone.
The addition of the title trustee was enough to disclose a
design to bring the land under the operation of the declaration
of trust.

"At common law the appointment of new trustees in
succession by the parties (not in execution of a special power)
did not vest the title in the new trustees without conveyance.
Peabody v. Eastern Methodist Society in Lynn, 5 Allen, 540.
It was said by Wells, J., in National Webster Bank v. Eldridge,
115 Mass. 424, 428, that 'the appointment of the
trustee in succession not having been made under the authority
of the statute, it would follow that the provisions
of General Statutes, c. 100 s. 9, would not operate to vest the
title in them.' That decision was rendered in 1874. The
difficulty there pointed out was remedied by Statute 1878, c.
254, whereby it was provided that the title to property held
by trustees should vest in their successors 'chosen or appointed
in conformity to any written instrument creating
a trust in place of former trustees thereunder.' This statute
is embodied in effect in R. L. c. 147, § 6. It follows
that the title to the land in question, having vested in B, as
trustee, devolved upon his successors in turn appointed
according to the terms of the declaration of trust without
special conveyance. (Citing cases.) The plaintiff is therefore
the proper person to bring a petition to redeem. He
took title to the trust property by selection and acceptance,
which was the only qualification required by the trust.
Bisbee v. McKay, 215 Mass. 21, 23.

On reorganization of a corporation it became necessary
for one set of voting trustees to be succeeded by another
under a new agreement but with the same beneficiaries,
certain managers were empowered to order the transfer from
the old trustees to the new and exercised that right by direct
transfer. The State contended that there were in legal
effect two transfers of legal title and that two transfer taxes
were due. The court held that there was only one transfer,

Glazier v. Everett, 224 Mass. 184, 187, 112 N. E. 1007.
