Chap. V) LIABILITY OF MEMBERS (§ 64

This rule has been strictly applied in a suit by a trustee of an unincorporated club against a member for indemnity

Thaw, 5 Mo. App. 279, 286 (Masonic lodge); Hammerstein v. Parsons, 38 Mo. App. 332, 335 (benefit certificate); Riffe v. Proctor, 99 Mo. App. 601, 608, 74 S. W. 409 (church); Hornberger v. Orchard, 39 Neb. 639, 642, 58 N. W. 425 (social club); Sizer v. Daniels, 66 Barb. 426, 433 (political committee); Hosman v. Kinneally, 86 N. Y. S. 263, 43 Misc. 76 (see 90 N. Y. S. 357) (socialistic labor party. Action by employee of a newspaper conducted by trustees and supported by fixed contributions from members); Lightbourne v. Walsh, 89 N. Y. S. 856, 97 App. Div. 187; Siff v. Forbes, 119 N. Y. S. 773, 135 App. Div. 39 (loan to socialist labor party); Devogs v. Gray, 22 Ohio St. 159, 169 (trustees of a church); Eichbaum v. Irons, 6 Watts & S. 67 (Pa.) (dinner committee appointed at a public meeting); Ash v. Guie, 97 Pa. St. 493, 498 (Masonic lodge); Franklin Co. v. Gorman, 76 Pa. Super. Ct. 276, 279; Winona Lumber Co. v. Church, 68 S. D. 498, 503, 62 N. W. 107 (trotting park); Robbins Co. v. Cook, 42 S. Dak. 136, 173 N. W. 445 (Members of an unauthorized commission appointed by the Governor); Arkins v. Dominion Live Stock Association, 17 Ont. Pr. Rep. 303, 305 (club). See Whitehead Co. v. Donovan, 210 Ill. App. 268.

Business agent of a labor union made contract with plaintiff for use of union label which union later broke. He sued individual members of the union. Held: Cannot sue them as individuals. Hint that proper remedy is equity to reach funds of union. Ehrlich v. Willenski, 138 Fed. 425.

Members of a church signed a call to a pastor agreeing to pay him $1,000 per year. He performed the services. The signers were held personally liable. Thompson v. Garrison, 22 Kan. 766. Contra, Paddock v. Brown, 6 Hill 530 (N. Y.). See Neill v. Spencer, 5 Ill. App. 461, 472.

After foreclosure of a church mortgage a bill was brought under the code to collect the balance of the debt from the members of the church without alleging their authorization of the indebtedness. Held: Bill dismissed. Such an association is not a partnership within the meaning of the code. Its members are not individually liable for its debts unless authorized or ratified. First Bank v. Rector, 59 Neb. 77, 79, 80 N. W. 269.

Action against a large number of members of an unincorporated congregation of Roman Catholics on an account stated between a former priest represented by the plaintiff and his successor and others alleged to be authorized by the defendants to represent the congregation in such matters. Held: The liability is not based on partnership but on agency and the defendants are bound not because of membership but because of authorization or ratification. Sheehy v. Blake, 72 Wis. 411, 414, 416, 39 N. W. 479. Evidence was held sufficient to show authorization or ratification. Sheehy v. Blake, 77 Wis. 394, 399, 46 N. W. 537.

Action on a note given for church furniture sold by plaintiff. The note was signed individually by two who wrote after their names "Deacons Mount Carmel Church." The action was brought against them and the trustees of the church in their official capacity. Held: An unincorporated association cannot be sued as such. The members who incur the liability or later assent to or ratify it are liable as individuals. Burton v. Grand Rapids Co., 10 Tex. Civ. App. 270, 31 S. W. 91.

Members of a religious association who authorized the organization of a corporation to conduct a college were not liable for the debts of the college. Kemp v. New Mexico, etc., church, 25 N. Mex. 418, 184 Pac. 484.

In one case it was said that the liability was that of partners but because

<br>

### --- Page 0435 ---

§ 64] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS (Car. V

against a liability on a lease incurred solely by reason of his acting in the capacity of trustee. The English privy council made an exception to the rule which it had previously laid down in regard to like actions by trustees of associations for profit,? and held that because of the rule that members of clubs incur no individual liability beyond their subscriptions unless they in fact authorize or ratify the act imposing the obligation, the trustee could not enforce a right to indemnity against the defendant merely because of the fact of membership.®

The fact that the defendant made payments on account as treasurer was held not ratification so as to bind him as an individual member.‘ Ratification by a national convention of a political party did not amount to ratification by the individual members of that party.’ The mere fact of membership and payment of dues is not enough, nor attendance at a meeting at which were read and approved the minutes of the former meeting that ratified the act of a committee.’ But knowledge that the plaintiff was performing

it was a non-trading partnership there was no implied authority to issue notes (farmers’ telephone line) Schumaker v. Sumner Tel. Co., 161 Ia. 326, 142 N. W. 1034. See similar loose language in regard to a religious community, Teed v. Parsons, 202 Ill. 455, 460, 66 N. E. 1044, and a college fraternity, Korsted v. Williams, 80 Wash. 452, 457, 141 Pac. 887. Members of a church have even been said to be ‘liable on its contracts as joint promissors or partners.” Thurmond v. Cedar Springs Church, 110 Ga. 816, 36 S. E. 221. In one case there was a dictum that the members were liable whether partnership proved or not. Webster v. San Joachin etc. Ass’n 32 Cal. App. 264, 162 Pac. 654.

An allegation that services were performed at the request of the association is sufficient. Zimmerman v. Prior, 188 Pac. 836 (Cal. App.).

2 Hardoon v. Belilios, (1901) A.C. 118, 125. See Ex parte Chippendale, 4 D. M. & G. 19, 52.

* Wise v. Perpetual Trustee Co., (1903) A. C. 139, 149.

4 Devoss v. Gray, 22 Ohio St. 159, 169.

5 Siff v. Forbes, 119 N. Y. S. 773, 135 App. Div. 39.

* Lightbourne v. Walsh, 89 N. Y. S. 856, 97 App. Div. 187 (association publishing a newspaper).

1 Meriwether v. Atkin, 137 Mo. App. 32, 37, 119 S. W. 36 (lodge).

Contract by a printer against members of a college class for printing of a volume on a written contract with A, who had been elected by the class business manager of the publication. There was evidence that the plaintiff gave credit to the class. Judgment for plaintiff. Held: ‘‘It was competent for the court to infer from all the evidence that the defendants who were present at the class meeting at which it was voted to publish a volume to be called ‘The Brown and Blue’ either voted to publish the volume or assented to the vote. This is true of the vote by which Arnold was elected ‘business manager of the

<br>

### --- Page 0436 ---

Chap. V] LIABILITY OF MEMBERS [§ 64

ing her part of the contract was sufficient. Publication from time to time to members of a church of a liability to its pastor for unpaid salary and loans after an accounting with him was held sufficient evidence to warrant a finding of ratification. Of course if the contract was made before a member joined an association, or after he retired, he cannot be held to have authorized it. Loosely reasoned decisions implying that something less than authorization or ratification would suffice are not likely to be followed.*

publication. The contract made by Arnold was apparently within the scope of his employment, at least the court will so find. Willcox v. Arnold, 162 Mass. 577, 578, 39 N. E. 414.

* Heath v. Goslin, 80 Mo. 310 (board of regents of a school).

Members of a lodge who attended meetings in a hall hired for it by its trustees without express authority must be held to have ratified the contract though they did not know the terms of the lease. "He may ratify by voluntarily assuming the risk without inquiry or he may deliberately ratify on such knowledge as he possesses without caring for more." Ehrmanntraut v. Robinson, 52 Minn. 333, 335, 54 N. W. 188.

Members of an association formed to protect irrigation rights must have known of the employment of plaintiff as attorney. Fort Morgan Co. v. Sterling Co., 64 Col. 222, 171 Pac. 72.

* Sheehy v. Blake, 77 Wis. 394, 399, 46 N. W. 537.

1 Hornberger v. Orchard, 39 Neb. 639, 643, 58 N. W. 425 (social club); Barry v. Nuckolls, 2 Humph. 324 (Tenn.) (dramatic society, rent).

11 Rhoads v. Fitzpatrick, 166 Pa. St. 294, 296, 31 Atl. 79.

12 A citizens' meeting voted to continue a newspaper in Welsh and appointed an editor and authorized him to find a publisher who was to be guaranteed by the committee against loss to the extent of £300. He engaged plaintiff, but no formal guarantee was written. He saw the resolution. Held: Defendant who attended the meeting is liable. With the others he should bear the publisher harmless to the extent of £300. Waterlow v. Cotton, 2 Wkly. Rep. 562.

Action for price of a supper ordered by committee of arrangements appointed by a political club to get up a ball. Held: Though in ordinary cases of joint contract, if one defendant sets up denial of the joint contract plaintiff must prove the joint contract and hold him though other defendants have defaulted, "But when the contract is made on behalf of a firm or an association and the liability of the firm or association is proved, it is enough for the plaintiff to show that the persons who appear and defend are members of such firm or association and so are liable with the other defendants whose membership is admitted." Downing v. Mann, 3 E. D. Smith (N. Y.) 36, 46, 9 How. Pr. (N. Y.) 204.

An unorganized club without constitution or by-laws was held not a joint stock association under the New York statute. But it was said that all the members were liable to one who had furnished goods to it from time to time on the order of its steward, having never been notified that they had arranged to have the steward buy for himself and then sell to them. Park v. Spaulding, 10 Hun 128.

The members of a mutual benefit association are personally liable for the

<br>

### --- Page 0437 ---

$64) NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS [Cuar. V

There are cases, however, where it was properly held that the purpose of the association was such that mere membership necessarily implied authorization of contracts such as that in suit."

benefit provided by the constitution and by-laws because under statute beneficiary may sue the association by its officers. Strauss v. Thoman, 111 N. Y. S. 745, 748, 60 Misc. 72.

A caterer sued some of the members of a committee appointed at a public meeting to arrange a dinner to celebrate the election of Harrison. Held: He can recover. Non-joinder of others of committee not pleaded in abatement. Defendants were not agents of a club. "A club is a definite association organized for indefinite existence; not an ephemeral meeting for a particular occasion to be lost in the crowd at its dissolution." All defendants who concurred in the order are liable. Some who at first objected but finally yielded are still liable. "Every member present assents beforehand to whatever the majority may do and becomes a party to the acts done, it may be, directly against his will. If he would escape responsibility for them he should protest and throw up his membership on the spot." (Gibson, J.) Eichbaum v. Irons, 6 Watts & S. (Pa.) 67, 40 Am. Dec. 540.

See Green v. Young Zion Church, 109 S. E. 517 (Ga. App.).

13 Lawler v. Murphy, 58 Conn. 294, 313, 20 Atl. 457 (benefit certificate); Cheney v. Goodwin, 88 Me. 563, 567, 34 Atl. 420 (association formed to bring a shoe factory to town); Richmond v. Judy, 6 Mo. App. 465, 468 (members of a campaign committee may be assumed to know that their association will incur expense for meetings); Lynn v. Commercial Club, 31 S. D. 401, 141 N. W. 471 (commercial club); Ridgely v. Hobson, 3 Watts & S. 118, 122 (Pa.) (members of association formed to run a public library are liable for books purchased by agent); Sergeant v. Goldsmith Dry Goods Co., 22 S. W. 259 (Tex.) (Members of mutual insurance association liable for supplies and services purchased and for reinsurance.) Cockerell v. Aucompte, 2 C. B. N. S. 440, 3 Jur. N. S. 844 (club to buy coal at wholesale for members); But see contra, Fleming v. Hector, 2 Gale 180, 6 L. J. Ex. 43, 2 M. & W. 172, where it was said that express authority to buy on credit was necessary where a fund was provided for payment, even though defendant knew the coal was being bought. Wood v. Finch, 2 Foster & Fin. 447.

At a mass meeting a "Guarantee Committee" was organized to contract with a promoter to pay him $70,000 to build a railroad. The contract was authorized and a sub-committee was appointed to borrow money to make the first payment. This was done. Later a constitution and by-laws were adopted and officers elected whose powers were limited, so that a vote of the association would be necessary to authorize them to borrow. They renewed their original notes at a different bank without such vote. The notes were signed by certain individual members of the association. Action against the makers of the note. Answer claimed contribution against all members of the association. Amended petition joined all members as defendants. Held: All members of the association were jointly and severally liable. The original note being authorized, its renewal by officers of the more formal association required no special authority. The makers had a right of contribution against the other members. Since the contract with the promoter was for the purpose of carrying out the very purposes of the association, when the work has been done it is too late to object that defendants were not present at the meeting at which a substituted contract was expressly authorized with a construction company. Hardy v. Carter, 163 S. W. 1003 (Tex.). See Leake v. Venice, 195 Pac. 440 (Cal. App.).

<br>

### --- Page 0438 ---

Chap. V] LIABILITY OF MEMBERS [§ 64

Members who authorize or ratify are liable whether or not they intended to become liable or understood the law." If the plaintiff agreed that the members should not be individually bound, they are not liable,* and likewise if it was agreed that the plaintiff should look only to a certain fund for payment.’ In equity the members are held individually liable.

1. Lawler v. Murphy, 58 Conn. 294, 313, 20 Atl. 457 (benefit association).

48 Heath v. Goslin, 80 Mo. 310, 314.

48 Lightbourne v. Walsh, 89 N. Y. 8. 856, 97 App. Div. 187. So where the plaintiff in fact knew at the time he performed services for the association, that its members with whom he contracted intended not to become personally liable, but that he be paid out of the association’s funds, he cannot hold them individually. Attorney defending patent suits. Burt v. Lathrop, 52 Mich. 106, 17 N. W. 716; McCabe v. Goodfellow, 1833 N. Y. 89, 95, 30 N. E. 728 (reversing 15 N. Y. S. 377) (attorney prosecuting for a “Law and Order” league); Jones v. Hope, 3 T. L. R. 247, note, (C. of App.) (solicitor for a volunteer corps).

The rule applies even when the defendant is the contracting member.

Plaintiff sued defendant for repairs on a steamer which defendant had bought for the purpose of turning it over to an association being formed of which plaintiff and defendant were to be members. Held: Defendant was personally liable for the contract unless by agreement the plaintiff was to look to the association for payment. It is immaterial for whose benefit it was intended. Wells v. Turner, 16 Md. 133, 143.

A contract of a Baptist congregation with its minister construed to be a contract payable only out of a fund to be raised by voluntary contributions. Evidence of the custom of Baptist churches known to the plaintiff was properly admitted. Riffe v. Proctor, 99 Mo. App. 601, 609, 74 S. W. 409. So of a contract between a Chamber of Commerce and a factory to give a bonus for location in the town. Warman Co. v. Redondo Chamber, 34 Cal. App. 37, 166 Pac. 856.

Individual members of an Odd Fellows Lodge are not liable to a fellow member for sick benefits, for the constitution and by-laws show that the payments are to be made by the lodge, not by its members. Myers v. Jenkins, 63 Ohio St. 101, 116, 57 N. E. 1089.

Defendant signed note as trustee of a religious society for a loan secured on specific property, the intention being that that property alone should be liable. Held: Defendant not personally liable, though it was not a corporation. Elwell v. Tatum, 6 Tex. Civ. App. 397, 401, 24 S. W. 71, 25 S. W. 434.

By statute in Pennsylvania members are not liable individually, but in an action against some in the name of the whole, they will be ordered to see the claim paid out of the treasury. Wolfe v. Limestone Council, 233 Pa. St. 357, 82 Atl. 499.

A member of a committee to organize a railroad was sued by a surveyor employed by the committee with the approval of the defendant for pay for his services. There was a verdict for the defendant. Held: Properly left to the jury to decide whether under all the circumstances the agreement was that credit be given to the defendant or only to a fund to be raised. If the latter and the fund was raised, the agreement became absolute and the defendant would be liable. Higgins v. Hopkins, 3 Ex. 162, 167. See Landman v. Entwhistle, 7 Ex. 632.

<br>

### --- Page 0439 ---

§ 64] ; NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS [Chap. V

usually liable only after the joint assets are exhausted. Liability of a member once incurred is not terminated by withdrawal from the association.* In two trade union cases it was held that a contract made by the association was binding on individual members on the theory that it was made by implication a term of the contract of employment of each individual member. A contract between an employer and a union made by letter is to be construed like a contract between individuals. Provisions in the by-laws for discharge of a minister were imported into his contract of employment.* A vote of a union authorizing the executive committee to hire a lawyer was not conclusive of his right to recover where the committee told him that the union was not employing him but that he would be paid by volun-

1. Patch Mfg. Co. v. Capeless, 79 Vt. 1, 63 Atl. 938.

18. Sizer v. Daniels, 66 Barb. 426, 433 (political committee).

The International Typothetae made a contract with the International Pressman’s Union covering relations of members, strikes, hours, etc. Both unincorporated and representing local unincorporated associations. Action by individual members of local of former against officers of Ohio Union. Minority of Union did not approve of this controversy. Not all members of Union work for members of Typothetae or vice versa. Dictum, that contract though in form made by two international bodies was really a contract between each member of a local of the Typothetae who had members of the Union in his employ and those members, so that it became the terms of the separate contracts of employment. All members of Typothetae not indispensable parties. But contract invalid for lack of authority or ratification by Union of acts of its officers. Barnes v. Berry, 169 Fed. 225, 228, 94 C.C.A. 501.

An agreement between a railroad and a union fixing runs and rates of pay to be in force two years is a usage and not a contract. As such it becomes an implied term of the contract of employment of a workman. There was nothing in it about the period of service of employees, and so that period would be indefinite and could be terminated by either party at any time. Hence discharged employee cannot recover for loss of time after his discharge. Hudson v. V.N.O., etc. Ry. 152 Ky. 711, 718.

But see contra, Hudson v. Cincinnati Co., 152 Ky. 711, 154 S.W. 47. A miners’ union not being a business enterprise cannot bind individual members by agreement with operators respecting performance of work and time and manner of payment. Burnetta v. Marceline Co., 180 Mo. 241, 79 S.W. 136.

Members of employers’ association sue union for not supplying union help according to a contract under seal between the two associations. Held: Doubtful if states cause of action and whether members can sue. If agreement under seal, the members certainly could not bring any action on it. Barsilay v. Loewenthal, 119 N.Y. 8, 612, 184 App. Div. 502.

* Gilchrist Co. v. Metal Polishers Union, 113 Atl. 320 (N.J.).
1 Evangelical Cong. v. Hass, 187 Neb. 667, 681 (Neb.).

<br>

### --- Page 0440 ---

Chap. V] LIABILITY OF MEMBERS [§ 64

voluntary subscription. Where an association knowingly took over property which had been cleared of liens by credit given by the plaintiff and others, the association was held to have taken it subject to such claims.*

Members of an association are not liable for the torts of a fellow member unless "they have authorized or participated in it or have aided in some way in its perpetration after knowledge of its illegal purpose or facts from which such knowledge may be inferred."* Members of a club, who signed its lease as "Managers," when there was no such office, are not individually liable for its negligent occupation of the premises whereby pipes were allowed to freeze and overflow on a neighbor, without proof that they were charged with some duty other than what membership implies.25 They have been held liable for negligence of an agent whom they placed in charge of their premises whether they knew of his specific act or not, and likewise for the

32 Foster v. Local Union, 112 S. E. 917 (S. C.).

33 Hosman v. Kinneally, 90 N. Y. S. 357, 45 Misc. 411 (association sued under statute).

34 Hill v. Eagle Co., 219 Fed. 719 (C. C. A.—W. Va.) (trade union); United Mine Workers v. Coronado Coal Co., 258 Fed. 829, 837 (C. A.-Ark.); Segenfeld v. Friedman, 193 N. Y. S. 128, 117 Misc. 731. Hence defendant was liable as surety on a bond for a Masonic lodge that he signed in consequence of false representations of a member. Sewall v. Breathitt Lodge, 150 Ky. 542, 545, 150 S. W. 677.

A declaration in libel against members of a merchants' association for blacklisting him as owing it $3.38 held not demurrable. No discussion of principles of association. White v. Parks, 93 Ga. 633, 20 S. E. 78.

Defendant joined a so-called protective association which sent series of letters to debtors threatening to publish their names in a book to be distributed to members. Envelope said it was association for collection of bad debts. Defendant sent first letter in system, and plaintiff and various local and central agents or officers then sent others of the series. Held: Libellous and not privileged. Real object of association was collection by threats not protection. (No discussion of association's liability. Probably defendant would be liable on principle of agency.) Muetse v. Tuteur, 77 Wis. 236, 46 N. W. 123, 20 Am. St. Rep. 115, 9 L. R. A. 86.

A fraternal insurance corporation was held liable for acts of members of a subordinate lodge, as its agents, causing personal injuries to an initiate, Ange v. Sovereign Camp, 173 N. C. 33, 91 S. E. 586.

35 Miller v. McElin, 208 Ill. App. 605, 609.

Plaintiff sues for suffering and death of husband by attack of a bear escaped from grounds of club of which defendants were members where he was being kept as a prize. One defendant, president of the club, did not know he was there. Held: The club was not properly incorporated and so is a voluntary association. The members of the club employed agent in charge of the premises.

<br>

### --- Page 0441 ---

§ 64] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS (Chap. V

torts of a business agent of a trade union." The officers of a local lodge of a fraternal beneficial association who negligently allowed a sword to trip a candidate whom they were initiating in accordance with ceremonies prescribed by the Grand Lodge of the State were acting as agents of the Grand Lodge. His agreement to be bound by the constitution and laws and to conform to the forms and ceremonies of initiation made in his application do not deprive him of his right of action, because he did not know what they were when he agreed to it. Where the secretary without objection announced at a meeting his intent to publish in its report of the meeting a libellous letter read at the meeting by the president and later did so, it was held that the members authorized it.9

A national association cannot be held liable on contracts expressly made with the State association. It has been held that the liability of members of an unincorporated association is joint and several.

§ 65. Liability of Members of Associations of Employers and Employees

In considering the liability of non-profit associations and their members for torts, one naturally thinks of the litigation that has grown out of modern combinations of employers and employees. A thorough consideration of these cases, however, involves analysis of the law of monopoly, competition and unfair trade which is beyond the scope of this treatise. It is moreover not strictly a part of the law of associations, for although in almost every case an association whether of employers or employees was involved,

ises and were liable for his acts whether they knew about them or not. Dissent as to the defendant who did not know about it. Vredenburg v. Behan, 33 La. Ann. 627, 640, 645.

7 Clarkson v. Laiblan, 202 Mo. App. 682, 216 S. W. 1029.

Grand Temple v. Johnson, 171 S. W. 490 (Tex. Civ. App.).

Pandolpho v. Bank, 273 Fed. 48, 50 (C. C. A.—Ariz.).

Crawley v. Am. Soc. of Equity, 153 Wis. 13, 18, 139 N. W. 734. (Farmers’ Union).

31 Fredenhall v. Taylor, 23 Wis. 538, 26 Wis. 286; Vader v. Ballou, 151 Wis. 577, 139 N. W. 413, (political committee); Crawley v. Am. Soc. of Equity, 153 Wis. 13, 19, 189 N. W. 734, (Farmers’ Union).

<br>

### --- Page 0442 ---

Cuar. V] ASSOCIATIONS OF EMPLOYERS AND EMPLOYEES [§ 65

the principles of law upon which the case turned would have been as applicable to an unassociated group of individuals as to an organized association. For these reasons only an outline of the cases will be attempted.1 These cases arise where economic pressure is brought to bear by a group for its own benefit against some other group or individual members of that group. Obviously acts which when done by isolated individuals, are harmless, become seriously damaging when done in concert by many.2

In the language of the Supreme Court of the United States "Society itself is an organization and does not object to organizations for social, religious, business and all legal purposes. The law therefore recognizes the right of working men to unite and to invite others to join their ranks, thereby making available the strength, influence and power that come from such association. By virtue of this right powerful labor unions have been organized. But the very fact that it is lawful to form these bodies with multitudes of members, means that they have thereby acquired a vast power in the presence of which the individual may be helpless. This power, when unlawfully used against one, cannot be met, except by his purchasing peace at the cost of submitting to terms which involve the sacrifice of rights protected by the Constitution: or by standing on such rights and appealing to the preventive powers of a court of equity. When such appeal is made it is the duty of government to protect the one against the many as well as the many against the one."2

Prima facie any damage intentionally caused by one man to another is tortious unless it is justified by the occasion.3 So in the warfare of competition by combinations,

1 For a detailed analysis of the cases, see "Crucial Issues in Labor Litigation," by Jeremiah Smith, in 20 Harvard Law Review, 253, 345, 429, and "Competition and the Law," by Bruce Wyman, 17 Green Bag 210.

2 Pickett v. Walsh, 192 Mass. 572, 78 N. E. 753; Retail Lumber Dealers Association v. State, 95 Miss. 337, 48 So. 1021.

2 Gompers v. Buck's Stove & Range Co., 221 U. S. 418, 437, 55 L. ed. 797, 31 S. Ct. 492.

3 Bowen, L. J., in Skinner v. Shew, (1893) I Ch. 413, 422; Holmes, J., in Aikens v. Wisconsin, 195 U. S. 194, 204, 49 L. ed. 154, 25 S. Ct. 3; Huskie v. Griffin, 75 N. H. 347, 350, 74 Atl. 595.

<br>

### --- Page 0443 ---

§ 65] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS [Cuar. V

both as regards a true competitor and as regards the other necessary element of business enterprise, capital or labor as the case may be, in cases where strikes do not result in acts otherwise illegal, the issue is whether or not the courts recognize, as a justification for the damage caused by the combination, the immediate purpose of the acts complained of.

The justification usually recognized for the damage caused by pressure exerted by associations is a direct competitive financial or business interest of the defendant to be advanced by the act in question. Thus strikes for increased wages or better working conditions are legal,* but a strike to advance the private interests of an individual is unlawful.' The latter rule was applied in a case where the defendant unions were affiliated with a Building Trades Council which was an unincorporated association made up of delegates from local unions. By the working rules of this council every grievance of an employee was to be investigated.

5 Mogul Co. v. McGregor, 23 Q. B. D. 598, 613. It used to be said that an association became lawful or unlawful according to the means employed to attain its end. Comm. v. Hunt, 4 Met. 111.

It is of course true that illegal acts, such as violence in the midst of a legal strike, will render those responsible for it liable in damages and will be enjoined. That, however, is a problem of the law of torts by individuals. The association is not a necessary element of it. The questions of the law of conspiracy come nearer to our subject. Thus it was held that an organisation for the propagation of theories involving the present social system and division of property became an unlawful conspiracy if it advocated violent means or provided for drilling of troops in violation of the militia law. Spies v. People, 122 Ill. 1, 12

* Iron Molders Union v. Allis-Chalmers Co., 166 Fed. 45 (C. C. A.—Wis); Lohse v. Fuelle, 215 Mo. 421, 114 S. W. 997.

7 A strike to force an employer to get rid of the plaintiff, a foreman, instituted because of personal hostility of one Dacey and a desire to advance his private interests was unlawful and plaintiff may recover damages from the officers and members of the union though the discharge was after a vote of all employees, union and non-union. The plaintiff may recover all his arising from the discharge and the inability to procure employment which may continue indefinitely. Hanson v. Innes, 211 Mass. 305, 97 N. E. 756.

Union may strike to compel re-employment of a fellow worker who they think was improperly discharged. Walter A. Wood Co. v. Tookey, 186 N. Y. 8. 95.

After a strike, the strikers returned to work, but one of their number who had been offensive in the negotiations was refused employment. The rest then struck again. Held: Illegal strike. Mechanica Co. v. Lynch, 236 Mass. 504, 128 N. E. 877.

<br>

### --- Page 0444 ---

Chap. V] ASSOCIATIONS OF EMPLOYERS AND EMPLOYEES [ § 65

gated by its executive committee, and, if the employer did not comply with its decision, he was to be declared unfair and interviewed and if he still refused to comply, the union men were to be withdrawn from his shop. It was held that these rules attempted to force employers to submit to the decision of the union as to individual grievances, such as the discharge of an employee for drunkenness or inefficiency. ‘‘Such a strike would be a strike in the nature of a sympathetic strike, that is to say, it is a strike not to forward the common interests of the strikers but to forward the interests of an individual employee in respect to a grievance between him and his employer where no contract of employment exists.” ‘‘We do not mean to say that a labor union cannot combine to support a committee to take up individual grievances in behalf of several employees. What we now decide to be illegal is a combination that such grievances (that is to say, grievances between an individual member of a union and his employer which are not common to the union members as a class) shall be decided by the employees and that decision enforced by a strike on the part of all.”’ ®

Associations of employers have been held lawful when the purpose of rate fixing was only incidental.? Most of the cases against employers’ associations, however, have arisen under the Sherman Act and under State anti-trust acts, by which have been forbidden both combinations to raise prices and combinations to boycott one who does not comply with their regulations.“ A majority of the Ne-

* Reynolds v. Davis, 198 Mass. 294, 300, 84 N. E. 457.

* An association to fix rates of commission in the trade, with penalty of expulsion, forbidding members to deal with one expelled was not unlawful for the penalties were only incidental, Harelson v. Tyler, 281 Mo. 383, 219 S. W. 908, 914. See Booker v. Louisville Board, 188 Ky. 771, 224 S. W. 451.

10 Rourke v. Elk Drug Co., 77 N. Y. S. 373, 75 App. Div. 145. Refusal to buy from wholesalers who sell directly to consumers held illegal, Retail Lumber Dealers’ Association v. State, 95 Miss. 337, 344, 48 So. 1021. Fire Insurance Tariff Association to fix rates may be penalized by state statute, German Alliance Insurance Co. v. Hale, 219 U. S. 307, 318; 55 L. ed. 229, 31 S. Ct. 246. But a live stock exchange may refuse to deal with a member expelled for uncommercial conduct, Gladish v. Kansas City Live Stock Exchange, 113 Mo. App. 726, 89 S. W. 77. See Hopkins v. U. S., 171 U. S. 578, 587, 43 L. ed. 290, 18 S. Ct. 499. See note 41 post.

§ 65] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS [Chap. V

Nebraska court held that while the State has no authority
under a statute punishing restraint of trade to interfere with
the ordinary relations of capital and labor, it may enjoin a
union which conspires by assaults, threats and other disorderly conduct to prevent the transportation of goods.
A combination of employers to meet a union demand for
higher wages is legal.

A strike to force the plaintiff to join the union by compelling the plaintiff’s employer to discharge him is illegal.
Whether a strike to establish the closed shop is illegal is
more difficult of solution and the courts have differed on it.
The right of the employer which is said to be invaded is
described as his right to "the good will of his employees,
precisely as a merchant is entitled to the good will of his
customers, although they are under no obligation to continue to deal with him." In New Hampshire it is said

11 State v. Employers of Labor, 102 Neb. 768, 169 N. W. 717, 170 N. W. 185.
The Clayton Act takes out of the Sherman Act legal strikes, but not those not
entered into for the betterment of labor conditions. U.S. v. Norris, 255 Fed.
423 (D. C.—Ill.).

42 Cote v. Murphy, 159 Pa. 420, 28 Atl. 190.

48 Fairbanks v. McDonald, 219 Mass. 291, 106 N. E. 1000.

14 It was held illegal in Plant v. Woods, 176 Mass. 492, 57 N. E. 1011, and
in Berry v. Donovan, 188 Mass. 353, 74 N. E. 603; Snow Iron Works v. Chadwick, 227 Mass. 382, 116 N. E. 801; Bausch Machine and Tool Co. v. Hill,
231 Mass. 30; 120 N. E. 188; Smith v. Bowen, 232 Mass. 106, 121 N. E. 814;
Grimes v. Durnin, 114 Atl. 273, (N. H.); Erdman v. Mitchell, 107 Pa. St. 79,
56 Atl. 327; Lucke v. Clothing, etc. Assembly, 77 Md. 396, 26 Atl. 505; See
Heitkamper v. Hoffman, 164 N. Y. S. 533; Lehigh Co. v. Atlantic Works, 111

Labor union. Closed shop strike. Bill by non-union employees. Held:
"What one individual may lawfully do, a combination of individuals has the
same right to do provided they have no unlawful purpose in view." (P. 223.)
Courts are in conflict on subject, but Illinois holds closed shop purpose lawful
(p. 225). Unions believe it essential to success of their organization. Kempt
v. Division No. 241, 255 Ill. 213, 99 N. E. 389; Acc. Shaughnessy v. Jordan,
184 Ind. 499, 111 N. E. 622. (Dictum.) See National Ass’n v. Cumming, 170
N. Y. 315, 63 N. E. 369; Garside v. Hollywood, 150 N. Y. S. 647. Picketing to
enforce closed shop was enjoined. Marks Arnheim, Inc. v. Hillman, 189 N. Y.
S. 369 (App. Div.). See Schwartz v. Hillman, 189 N. Y. S. 21.

16 Hitchman Coal and Coke Co. v. Mitchell, 245 U. S. 229, 252, 62 L. ed.
260, 38 S. Ct. 65.

Unions of labor and capital may collectively bargain and both are entitled
to free and equal access to the pool of unemployed labor for the purpose of
securing recruits by peaceable appeals to reason. The mine check-off system is merely an assignment of wages. Gasaway v. Borderland Corp., 278
Fed. 56, 65 (C. C. A.—Ind.).

<br>

### --- Page 0446 ---

Cuar. V] ASSOCIATIONS OF EMPLOYERS AND EMPLOYEES [ § 65

that it is a question of fact in each case whether the real purpose of the defendants is an honest effort to benefit themselves or a malicious intent to injure the plaintiff.* It is now settled by the Supreme Court of the United States that an employer may enjoin members of a union not in his employ who seek to unionize his business by forcing employees to leave his service, even where their employment is terminable at will. This case also decided that an employer may make non-membership in a union a condition of employment. Since the real purpose of the defendants was to unionize the plaintiff’s mine and not a bona fide effort to enlarge their union, an injunction was granted. "A combination to procure concerted breaches of contract by plaintiff’s employees for the purpose of inflicting damage constitutes a violation of the plaintiff’s legal rights as much as a breach of the peace." An agreement between employer and employees for a closed shop is valid,* however, but a strike to enforce the closed shop is not justifiable unless the agreement clearly provides that the employer is to give all his work to the union to which the strikers belong."

6 White Mt. Freezer Co. v. Murphy, 78 N. H. 398, 101 Atl. 357. See National Fireproofing Co. v. Masons’ Ass’n, 169 Fed. 259 (C. C. A. N. Y.).

1 Hitchman Coal & Coke Co. v. Mitchell, 245 U. S. 227, 251, 257, 62 L. ed. 260, 38 S. Ct. 65. Cyrus Currier & Sons v. International Union, 115 Atl. 66 (N. J. Eq.); Skolny v. Hillman, 187 N. Y. 706. Contracts with an employer not to join a union are legal. Nashville Co. v. Lawson, 144 Tenn. 78, 229 S. W. 741. A strike in breach of contract that there should be no further labor troubles for a year is illegal. An international union, one of whose functions is to aid strikes of locals and whose officers assisted the local, was enjoined. Gilchrist Co. v. Metal Polishers’ Union, 113 Atl. 320 (N. J. Eq.). A strike to enforce a valid contract is, of course, legal. Nat. Fireproofing Co. v. Masons’ Ass’n, 169 Fed. 259 (C. C. A.—N. Y.); Greenfield v. Central Labor Council, 192 Pac. 783 (Ore.).

8 Shinsky v. Tracey, 226 Mass. 21, 114 N. E. 957. Did not violate local anti-trust law. Reihing v. Local Union, 94 N. J. L. 240, 109 Atl. 367. Miner’s "check-off" system violates Sherman Act. Borderland Corp. v. Int. U. M. W., 275 Fed. 871 (D. C.—Ind.) The arbitration clause applies only to employees and is not enforceable after a strike. Re Division 132, 188 N. Y. S. 353 (App. Div.).

19 Smith v. Bowen, 232 Mass. 106, 121 N. E. 814. Specifications called for union labor. A non-union painter took the subcontract for painting intending to do the work himself. Held: Union men entitled to threaten to strike unless he was discharged. They had acted in reliance on the specifications. The court assumes they had a contract right, but this seems wrong. Local Branch No. 8, 8 Ohio App. 437, 441. Strike to compel renewal of a closed shop

<br>

### --- Page 0447 ---

§ 65] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS [Cuar. V

A bill in equity was brought to restrain the officers and representative members of a trade union from forcing the plaintiffs to enter into a contract involving collective bargaining with the union and from forcing the plaintiffs to run their business as a closed shop. At the meeting of the local it was voted to submit to the photoengraving establishments in Boston a form of contract conferring on the union the absolute right of collective bargaining, of preferential employment with a minimum wage scale and providing that permanent employees should not be temporarily laid off even if there was not enough work to keep them employed. It fixed the ratio of apprentices to journeyman and provided for an arbitration of disputes. It further provided:

“No contracts individual or otherwise conflicting with this agreement shall be entered into and all contracts of employment must be submitted and executed in accordance with the by-laws and constitution of the international union.” The agreement was presented to the plaintiff who employed a large number of union men. He refused it. The union voted to strike on the non-acceptance of the contract. An injunction was granted. The court in its opinion said: “The question for decision is not whether an individual employee who has contracted to perform labor can abandon his contract leaving his employer to whatever remedy in damages he may have. It is whether by concerted action using the strike as a mass weapon the defendants could lawfully compel the plaintiffs to yield to their demands. The proposed agreement was presented as an entire contract to be unconditionally accepted. If the plaintiffs declined to enter into the agreement the underlying purpose manifestly was to enforce acquiescence through the coercive power of a strike, which even where there is both a legal and an illegal purpose is of itself illegal. ... The provision that the employer must retain and pay more employees than were

agreement held illegal. Cooks’ etc. Union v. Papageorge, 230 S.W. 1086 (Tex. Civ. App.). A strike pursuant to an agreement with an employers’ association from which plaintiff had been expelled is illegal. Brescia Co. v. Stone Masons’ Ass’n, 187 N.Y. 8. 77, 82 (App. Div.).

<br>

### --- Page 0448 ---

Chap. V ] ASSOCIATIONS OF EMPLOYERS AND EMPLOYEES [ § 65

actually or reasonably required for carrying on his business, and that disputes not covered by the agreement must be submitted to arbitration, even if proper subjects for negotiation where the parties are willing to negotiate, were, until accepted, mere proposals, the refusal of which was wholly insufficient to justify the measures adopted by the defendants. The plaintiffs could not be compelled to make an involuntary contract or to substitute compulsory arbitration for due process of law... "It is true the agreement reads, that the plaintiffs in the employment of journeymen and apprentices shall give preference to union men by notifying the union officers when additional journeymen and apprentices are needed, and if the union cannot furnish and supply competent help the employer may secure such help from other sources and no express requirement is found for the discharge of non-union men already under employment. No prolonged discussion, however, is needed to make plain that this was merely an indirect method which must culminate in a closed shop. The position of non-union employees under the practical working of the agreement would gradually become so unbearable and intolerable that as they retired and were gradually eliminated by the process of selection the plaintiffs necessarily must resort solely to union workmen to recruit their industrial force." 70

Limitation of apprentices is not an illegal purpose.21 A strike to prevent the use of helpers by employees engaged in piece work which diminished the amount of work available for strikers in dull seasons is justifiable.22 A combination

Foleom Engraving Co. v. McNeil, 235 Mass. 269, 126 N. E. 479. When a union presented a contract and made an appointment with the employer to discuss it and without excuse he failed to keep the appointment, strike justified. Walton Lunch Co. v. Kearney, 236 Mass. 310, 128 N. E. 429. See Rice, etc., Co. v. Willard, 242 Mass.—A strike to force abandonment of individual contracts is not a strike in defence of collective bargaining. It is not a bargain unless voluntary on both sides. United Shoe Machinery Co. v. Fitzgerald, 237 Mass. 537.

21 Snow v. Wheeler, 116 Mass. 179, 185.

*1 Minasian v. Osborne, 210 Mass. 250, 255, 96 N. E. 1031. It does not violate the Sherman Act. Munroe v. Colored Ass’n 135 La. 893, 898, 66 So. 260.

<br>

### --- Page 0449 ---

§65] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS [Cuar. V

to force the plaintiff to give the defendants work which the plaintiff does not want done and which will subject him to loss, such as requiring not less than a certain number of musicians at a motion picture theatre, is illegal. Where the employees sought to compel the proprietor of a motion picture house to employ a union operator instead of running the machine himself, it was enjoined.** Whether a strike for 'recognition of the union' is a legal purpose depends upon exactly what is meant by that phrase in a given case. Compelling the use of the union label has been held to be a lawful purpose.™

A strike for more than one purpose, one of which purposes is illegal, is an illegal strike. An ingenious attempt was made to escape an injunction by making a formal renunciation of the one illegal purpose. The Court said that the renunciation amounted to nothing more than a declaration of the motive of certain members of a union in sustaining an illegal strike. 'Their liability is determined by the character of the strike, not by their motive in electing to be parties to that strike: their liability is determined by the fact that they elected to remain parties to an illegal strike and not by their motive in doing so.' 'To get out of the strike and to keep in the union they hit upon the novel scheme of renouncing the illegal purpose of the strike, but that was a futile proceeding because it was nothing more than a statement of their motive in continuing members of the union and so of necessity parties to the illegal strike.'

% Haverhill Strand Theatre, Inc. v. Gillen, 229 Mass. 413, 118 N. E. 671. (Majority of the court); Contra (perhaps because of the terms of the constitution of Montana) Empire Theatre Co. v. Cloke, 53 Mont. 183, 163 Pac. 107. A strike to compel an employer to continue a department which he had decided to abandon is not justified. Welinsky v. Hillman, 185 N. Y. 8. 257.

24 Roraback v. Motion Picture etc. Union, 140 Minn. 481, 168 N. W. 766, appeal denied, 169 N. W. 529; Hughes v. Kansas, etc. Local, 282 Mo. 304, 221 S. W. 96, certiorari denied, 41 S. Ct. 7.
N gmellier v. Haverhill Shoe Mfgrs. Ass'n, 221 Mass. 554, 559, 109

* Justin v. Reiff, 164 N. Y. 8. 522, 524.

Bausch Co. v. Hill, 231 Mass. 30, 120 N. E. 188.

* Bausch Co. v. Hill, 231 Mass. 30, 120 N. E. 188. When the original controversy is not one that justifies a strike no subsequent demands can make it so. Vonnegut v. Toledo Co. 263 Fed. 192, 203 (D. C.—Ohio.)

<br>

### --- Page 0450 ---

Cuar. V] ASSOCIATIONS OF EMPLOYERS AND EMPLOYEES [ § 65

Whether a purpose is or is not legal is a question of law for the court.”

A strike for the purpose of causing employers to break agreements with a plaintiff union to employ only members of that union is illegal.*° An agreement between an employer and a union to give all work to the members of the union is valid and an expelled member who had joined a rival union cannot enjoin the union from interfering with his employment by insisting on its contract rights.*! The same plaintiff, however, when discharged through the influence of the union by the employer for whom he had been working for many years under a contract at will was allowed to recover damages from its members in spite of such a contract with his employer, because they were not seeking the protection of the contract for the economic purpose of providing work for their own members, but to compel discipline in their own ranks.*? A plaintiff, who had a contract for musicians with a union, did not like the ones they furnished and desired to employ other members of the union who were willing to work but feared fines and expulsion if they did so. The court refused an injunction on the ground that he had made a contract voluntarily and must abide by the union rules.** A strike to enforce payment of a fine or to force obedience of an employer to union rules is illegal.*4

Interference with the plaintiff’s right to a free flow of labor is illegal, not only if the purpose is illegal, but also if the method is illegal. This means not merely crimes and torts in the ordinary sense, but methods which when used

® Haverhill Strand Theatre, Inc. v. Gillen, 229 Mass. 413, 118 N. E. 71.

% Tracey v. Osborne, 226 Mass. 25, 114 N. E. 959.

31 Shinsky v. Tracey, 226 Mass. 21, 114 N. E. 957. But a contract binding members of a union to furnish labor cannot be made by union officers expressly or impliedly unless authorized expressly or impliedly by the members of the union. A custom or practice of furnishing men when requested even if known and not disapproved by the union was not enough. Snow Iron Works v. Chadwick, 227 Mass. 382, 116 N. E. 801.

32 Shinaky v. O'Neil, 232 Mass. 99, 121 N. E. 790.

33 Rhodes Bros Co. v. Musicians’ Union, 37 R. I. 281, 92 Atl. 641.

34 Grassi Contracting Co. v. Bennett, 160 N. Y. 8. 277, 285, 174 App. Div. 244,

<br>

### --- Page 0451 ---

§ 65] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS [Cuar. V

by an aggregation of individuals are so oppressive and coercive that they have been held to be illegal.**

Some courts have held that picketing is illegal even when used only as a weapon by striking employees against their former employer.* The cases in regard to it, however, almost always shade into the problem of boycott. “Where a line of guards consisting of one or more is stationed in front of a place of business, everyone knows that such guard is there for the purpose of intimidating and preventing the public from dealing with the person whose place of business is picketed.” It is when the purpose passes from publicity to a threat that it becomes illegal.** Regulation of picketing by statute has now begun. These statutes have usually been interpreted so as not to permit intimidation.** The Arizona statute, however, was interpreted by

38 This is a rule not limited to labor struggles, but is of broad application to those concerted torts which are coming to be described (perhaps erroneously historically) under the name of conspiracy. See Willett v. Herrick, 242 Mass. 000.
* Heitkamper v. Hoffman, 164 N. Y. v. 533; Pierce v. Stablemens’ Union, 156 Cal. 70, 103 Pac. 324; Rosenberg v. Retail Clerks’ Ass’n, 39 Cal. App. 67, 177 Pac. 864, Moore v. Cooks’ Union, 39 Cal. App. 538, 179 Pac. 417. But see contra Alaska S. & Co. v. Int. Longshoremen’s Ass’n, 236 Fed. 964, 969 (D. C.—Wash.). (But the union is responsible for the acts of its pickets, if they go beyond peaceable persuasion); Southern Cal. Co. v. Amalgamated Co., 186 Cal. 604, 200 Pac. 1; Seubert v. Reiff, 164 N. Y. v. 522, 524; Baasch v. Cooks’ Union, 99 Wash. 378, 169 Pac. 843; Root v. Anderson, 207 S. W. 255, (Mo. App.). Picketing with violence makes a strike illegal. Southern Cal. Co. v. Amalgamated Co., 186 Cal. 604, 200 Pac. 1; Grand Shoe Co. v. Children’s Shoe Workers Union, 187 N. Y. v. 886; Pre’Catalan v. International Federation, 188 N. Y. v. 29. Evidence of violence insufficient, Piermont v. Schlesinger, 188 N. Y. v. 35.

Picketing after a strike is really dead is mere nagging and should be enjoined. Dail Co. v. Willys Co., 263 Fed. 171, 183, 192, Quinlivan v. Dail, 274 Fed. 56, 65 (C. C. A.—Ohio). They are not obliged to use the language of the parlor if there is no intimidation. Walter A. Wood Co. v. Tookey, 186 N. Y. v. 95.

* St. Germain v. Bakery etc. Union, 97 Wash. 282, 201, 166 Pac. 665. Stuyvesant v. Reiner, 181 N. Y. v. 212; Michaels v. Hillman, 183 N. Y. v. 195.

% Importunity became intimidation. Number of pickets limited. American Steel Foundries v. Tri-City Central Council, 257 U. S. —, 42 Sup. Ct. 72, 76; Keuffel v. International Ass’n, 116 Atl. 9 (N. J.); Local v. Stathakis, 135 Ark. 86, 205 S. W. 450.

%* A statute authorizing picketing was held not to legalise it in a strike solely to compel recognition of the union. G. Heitkamper v. Central Labor Council, 192 Pac. 765 (Ore.); or intimidation, McMichael v. Atlanta Co., 151 Ga. 776, 108 S. E. 226. The Clayton Act allows picketing with reasonable



<br>

### --- Page 0452 ---

Chap. V] ASSOCIATIONS OF EMPLOYERS AND EMPLOYEES [§ 65

the State Supreme Court to permit mass picketing, using abusive and libelous epithets against employer and customers. This statute was, therefore, held unconstitutional by the Supreme Court of the United States as depriving the owner of the business and his premises without due process of law and by denying the remedy by injunction equally with other litigants as denying employers the equal protection of the laws. The dissenting opinion of Brandeis, J., has appended an elaborate classification of the decisions on picketing and boycott.

It is generally held that the conflict of financial interests must exist directly between those who exert the pressure and those upon whom it is exerted, that is that pressure through third parties not personally concerned in the conflict such as the so-called “secondary” boycott is illegal. A strike against an employer to compel three of his clerks to pay a fine imposed on them by the union was said to be such a boycott, based on the false statement that there was a

numbers and without intimidation. Kinlock Telephone Co. v. Local Union, 265 Fed. 312 (Mo.); that is, it allows a combination to do what individuals may do, Langenberg Co. v. United Co., 266 Fed. 127, 129 (D. C.—Mo.).

Truax v. Bisbee Local Union, 19 Ariz. 379, 171 Pac. 121.

Truax v. Corrigan, 42 Sup. Ct. Rep. 124.

Vonnegut Co. v. Toledo Co., 263 Fed. 192, 203 (D. C.—Ohio); Maryland Lodge v. Adt., 100 Md. 238, 251, 50 Atl. 721; Pickett v. Walsh, 192 Mass. 572, 78 N. E. 753; Burnham v. Dowd, 217 Mass. 351, 104 N. E. 841; Branson v. I. W. W., 30 Nev. 270, 294, 95 Pac. 354; Loewe v. California, etc. Federation, 189 Fed. 71; Beck v. Railway Teamsters, 118 Mich. 497, 77 N. W. 13; Lohse Patent Door Co. v. Fuelle, 215 Mo. 421, 114 S. W. 997; Barr v. Essex, Trades Council, 53 N. J. Eq. 101, 30 Atl. 881; Seubert v. Reiff, 164 N. Y. S. 522, 524; Auburn Dyeing Co. v. Wardell, 227 N. Y. 1, 11, 124 N. E. 97, distinguishing Bossert v. Dhuy, 221 N. Y. 342; Finnigan v. Butler, 182 N. Y. S. 671, 673. Both the union and its employers were enjoined. Burgess Co. v. Stewart, 184 N. Y. S. 119, 112 Misc. 347. See Booker & Kinnard v. Louisville Board, 188 Ky. 771, 224 S. W. 451.

The Clayton Act did not legalize the secondary boycott. It applies only to disputes as to terms and conditions of employment. It does not confer the right on all members of the trade association to which the disputing employees belong. The boycott is dealt with in a clause limited to peaceful and lawful influence on neutrals. Does not justify threats or persuasion to bring about strikes of employees of complainant’s customers i. e. secondary boycott.

The Clayton Act entitles individuals to injunction for violation of the Sherman Act, which the conspiracy was. Before the Clayton Act it was uncertain if individual had remedy. Dissent. Duplex Co. v. Deering, 254 U.S. 443, 473, 465; 65 L. ed. 349, 41 Sup. Ct. 172.

<br>

### --- Page 0453 ---

§65] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS (Chap. V

strike.* A secondary boycott was held illegal at common law and also a violation of the State anti-trust law. Sometimes strikes are called to compel an employer to take sides in a controversy between rival groups of employees. The purpose of obtaining additional work is legal and a strike will be sustained in which bricklayers refuse to lay bricks unless they are also given the work of pointing and cleaning them, though the effect of this is to deprive the non-union pointer or cleaner of his business, the court being unable to say that pointing and cleaning was something foreign to the work of a bricklayer. A strike, however, against an employer to force him to compel the owner of another building to give the pointing on that building to the defendant union passes beyond a case of competition in which the owner of the building is left to choose between two competitors, and is in fact a strike to compel a contractor to join a boycott of the owner of the building. 'It is a combination by the union to obtain a decision in their favor by forcing third persons who have no interest in the dispute to force the employer to decide the dispute in the union's favor. Such a strike is not a justifiable interference with the right of the plaintiffs to pursue their calling as they think best.** A plasterers' union could not strike against their sub-contractor to compel him to force the owner of the building to compel another sub-contractor to give the plasterers the work of applying cement through a cement gun."

The Supreme Court of the United States has said, "Courts differ as to what constitutes a boycott that may be enjoined

Harvey v. Chapman, 226 Mass. 191, 115 N. E. 304. A strike to compel dismissal of an employee who was claimed to be short in his accounts as an officer of the union was illegal. Giblan v. National Union, (1903) 2 K. B. 600. See Webb v. Drake, 52 La. Am. 290, 26 So. 791.

Webb v. Cooks' etc. Union, 205 S. W. 465 (Tex. Civ. App.); See Paine v. Neal, 244 U.S. 459, 471, 61 L. ed. 1256, 37 S. Ct. 718, which held that a private person cannot sue under the Sherman Act.

Pickett v. Walsh, 192 Mass. 572, 589, 78 N. E. 753. An employer who in fear of a general strike removes a sub-contractor whose employees have struck to enforce performance of a closed shop agreement, becomes a coconspirator, Lehigh Co. v. Atlantic Works, 92 N. J. Eq. 131, 111 Atl. 376.

New England Cement Gun Co. v. McGivern, 218 Mass. 198, 203, 105 N. E. 402.

### --- Page 0454 ---

Chap. V] ASSOCIATIONS OF EMPLOYERS AND EMPLOYEES [§ 65

or hold that there must be a conspiracy causing irreparable
damage to the business or property of the complainant.
Some hold that a boycott against the complainant, by a
combination of persons not immediately connected with
him in business, can be restrained. Others hold that the
secondary boycott can be enjoined, where the conspiracy
extends not only to injuring the complainant, but secondarily coerces or attempts to coerce his customers to refrain
from dealing with him by threats that unless they do, they
themselves will be boycotted. Others hold that no boycott
can be enjoined unless there are acts of physical violence
or intimidation caused by threat of physical violence. But
whatever the requirement of the particular jurisdiction as
to the conditions on which the injunction against a boycott
may issue, when these facts exist, the strong current of
authority is that the publication and use of letters, circulars
and printed matter may constitute a means whereby a
boycott is unlawfully continued. "The agreement to act
in concert when the signal is published, gives the words
'unfair' 'we don't patronize' or similar expressions a
force not inhering in the words themselves, and therefore
exceeding any possible right of speech which a single individual might have." "Unions may, however, refuse to work
with non-union material and a strike against the use of
non-union goods of a single manufacturer, which are also
manufactured by members of the union, where the strike is
without personal hostility, is legal. An agreement between
members of an association of employers not to trade with
non-members is not an illegal purpose, though the means

Gompers v. Buck's Stove and Range Co., 221 U.S. 418, 437, 439, 55 L. ed.
318. Ct. 492.

Bossert v. United Brotherhood, 137 N. Y. 8. 321, 77 Misc. 592.

Bossert v. Dhuy, 221 N. Y. 342, 365, 117 N. E. 582. See Paine v. Neal,
244 U.S. 459, 471, 61 L. Ed. 1256, 37 S. Ct. 718.

Mogul Co. v. McGregor, (1892) A. C. 25.

An association may be formed for the purpose of advancing its members’
interests by discriminating against other persons, if such discrimination
is based on proper and legal grounds, e. g. failure to pay bills
due members of the association. Brewster v. Miller, 101 Ky. 368, 418. W.
301.
An association of retailers may agree not to patronize wholesalers who sell

<br>

### --- Page 0455 ---

§65] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS [Cuar. V

employed to enforce the agreement may be illegal. Merchants may lawfully form an association whose constitution binds them to notify each other when one is unwilling longer to extend credit to a customer and forbids others to extend him credit without assuming the delinquent debt. Such a notice given in good faith is privileged and not basis for action for libel.

"A combination to black-list is the counter weapon to a combination to boycott and is open to similar legal to mail order houses and circulate a list of such. Montgomery Ward & Co. v. South Dak. Ass’n, 150 Fed. 413 (C. C.—8. D.).

An association of fire underwriters for regulation of premiums, prevention of rebates, compensation of agents and non-intercourse with companies not members, was held not an illegal conspiracy. Continental Ins. Co. v. Board of Fire Underwriters, 67 Fed. 310.

An association of keepers of sailors’ boarding houses designed to control the business of shipping seamen is a legitimate commercial device. Bowen v. Matheson, 96 Mass. 499.

An employer’s blacklist was held legal. Bradley v. Pierson, 148 Pa. St. 502, 24 Atl. 65.

But a liverymen’s association that prohibited members from doing business with those who do not patronize members exclusively was held an illegal monopoly. Gatzow v. Buening, 106 Wis. 1, 81 N. W. 1003.

An agreement between employers and unions restricting competition but primarily for their own benefit and not aimed at the plaintiff is not illegal conspiracy. National Fireproofing Co. v. Masons’ Ass’n, 169 Fed. 259 (C. C. A—N. Y.). Contra, Curran v. Galen, 22 N. Y. 5. 826, 2 Mise. 553.

One livestock exchange adopted rules forbidding members to trade with non-members. Members of a rival exchange sued members of first exchange, alleging conspiracy to boycott and monopolize. Held: Mere adoption of rules does not constitute a cause of action for plaintiff. No causal connection established. The rule applies only to the members and can only remotely affect outsiders. Implies that if members in fact refused to trade and carried out the rule it might be different. Downes v. Bennett, 63 Kan. 653, 661, 66 Pac. 623, 88 A. S. R. 256, 55 L. R. A. 560. See note 9 ante.

But a wholesaler was held to have no action against the secretary for threatening to send a notice of boycott. Bohn Mfg. Co. v. Hollis, 54 Minn. 223, 55 N. W. 1119. But it must not be formed for the purpose of restraining interstate trade. Eastern States Lumber Association v. United States, 234 U. S. 600, 58 L. ed. 1490, 348. Ct. 951. See note 10 ante.

A granite manufacturing association had a by-law imposing, in effect, a fine on members dealing with outsiders. Held: Though the object was not illegal, viz., competition, yet coercion by fines is illegal and the defendant is liable in damages. Martell v. White, 185 Mass. 255, 67 N. E. 1085. See Boutwell v. Marr, 71 Vt. 1, 42 Atl. 607.

By-laws of an association to prevent wholesalers selling to outsiders provided for a claim by members against such wholesalers and required members to boycott. Held: A dealer could enjoin a member from making such claim. Jackson v. Stanfield, 137 Ind. 592, 36 N. E. 345.

Putnal v. Inman, 76 Fla. 573, 80 So. 316.

<br>

### --- Page 0456 ---

Cuar. V) ASSOCIATIONS OF EMPLOYERS AND EMPLOYEES [ § 65

objections when directed against persons with whom those combined have no trade dispute or when the concerted action coerces the individual members by implied threats or otherwise, to withhold employment from those whom ordinarily they would employ.” §*

During the great war a corporation engaged in working for the government of the United States was granted an injunction against a strike merely for that reason *‘ and a strike to compel a closed shop in a factory largely engaged on government work during the war was held illegal on the ground that the government had afforded other means for the adjustment of all difficulties between capital and labor.** Actual violence with sufficient frequency to warrant the conclusion that it was part of the program, makes the strike illegal.“ Handing out ‘unfair cards’ containing false and libelous statements was enjoined.” Usually the plaintiff seeking to enjoin a strike is the employer. In one case it was held that one who had a contract for the delivery of cars from a selling agent of a manufacturer may enjoin a union from maintaining an illegal strike

® Cornellier v. Haverhill Shoe Mfrs. Assoc., 221 Mass. 554, 559, 109 N. E. 643; (suit by striking workman against an employers’ association); United States v. King, 229 Fed. 275, 279, 8. c. 250 Fed. 908 (Farmers’ Association. The court said the Clayton Act exempted them from dissolution but did not permit them to adopt methods of carrying on their business which are not permitted to other lawful associations).

Tort for libel against a member of an employers’ association for causing the secretary of the association to threaten to put the plaintiff's name on the black list for failure to pay bills. A by-law of the association forbade any member to sell to any one whose name was on this list. The defendant claimed privilege but the court said that no by-laws or any understanding about the by-laws would have afforded the defendant a justification. ‘‘A man cannot justify a libel by proving that he has contracted to libel. More specifically a false statement of a kind manifestly hurtful to a man in his credit and business and intended to be so, is not privileged because made in obedience to the requirements of a voluntary association got up for the purpose of compelling by a boycott the satisfaction of its members’ claims to the exclusion of a resort to the courts.” Weston v. Barnicoat, 175 Mass. 454, 458. In a similar case where the association was incorporated, the plaintiff was allowed to bring quo warranto and oust the corporation of its franchise. Hartnett v. Plumbers’ Supply Assoc., 169 Mass. 229, 47 N. E. 1002.

+ Wagner Electric Co. v. District Lodge, 252 Fed. 507.

% Rosenwasser Bros. v. Pepper, 172 N. Y. 8. 310.

“ Michaels v. Hillman, 183 N. Y. 8. 195.

® Godin v. Niebuhr, 236 Mass. 350, 128 N. E. 408.

### --- Page 0457 ---

§65] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS [Chap. V

which prevents the manufacturer from fulfilling its contract.58

In most of these cases the litigation is between the individual damaged and members of the association as representatives of the whole and an injunction is sought against all members of the association. In some cases individual members of the association are sued to recover damages suffered from the acts complained of, such as the circulation of known false statements even though for a lawful purpose, where the defendants "either authorized or adopted the circulation of the untrue notice or it was sent out in pursuance of a common purpose agreed on by the combination." The most famous case of this sort (The Danbury Hatters’ case) was a complaint under the Sherman Anti-Trust Act for conspiracy to boycott. It is often exceedingly difficult to produce evidence of authorization or ratification by the defendants of the acts complained of.

% Dail Co. v. Willys Co., 263 Fed. 171, 191 (D. C.—Ohio); Vonnegut Co. v. Toledo Co., 263 Fed. 192, 197 (D. C.—Ohio); Quinlivan v. Dail, 274 Fed. 56, 65 (C. C. A.—Ohio).

© L. D. Willcut & Son Co. v. Bricklayers’ Union, 200 Mass. 110, 85 N. E. 897; Thomas Russell & Sons v. Stampers', etc. Union, 107 N. Y. 8, 303. Injunction will not be granted on evidence merely of misconduct of individuals. Authorization must be proved. J. T. Parkinson Co. v. Building Trades Council, 154 Cal. 581, 98 Pac. 1027; Aluminum Co. v. Local No. 84, 197 Fed. 221. McMichael v. Atlantic Co., 151 Ga. 776, 108 S. E. 226.

® Hanson v. Innes, 211 Mass. 301, 305, 97 N. E. 756; Boutwell v. Marr, 71 Vt. 1, 42 Atl. 607, 76 Am. St. Rep. 746, 43 L. R. A. 803 (defendants were members of a manufacturers’ association); Gatzow v. Ruening, 106 Wis. 1, 14, 81 N. W. 1003, 80 Am. St. Rep. 17, 49 L. R. A. 475 (conspiracy by trade union to deprive plaintiff of a hearse at a funeral). Every member was responsible for the acts of the others and particularly for the acts of any officer. United Traction Co. v. Droogan, 189 N. Y. 8, 39.

® Martineau v. Foley, 231 Mass. 220, 120 N. E. 445.

2 Loewe v. Lawlor, 208 U. S. 274, 52 L. ed. 488, 28 S. Ct. 301, Lawlor v. Loewe, 235 U. S. 522, 535, 59 L. ed. 341, 35 S. Ct. 170.

* Action of conspiracy against labor union for strike to enforce closed shop held maintainable and damages collectible from members "so implicated as to be responsible for what they helped to set in motion or helped on." Roofing Co. v. Jose, 12 Ont. L. R. 200.

Lawlor v. Loewe, 187 Fed. 522, 526 (C. C. A.—Conn.); Lawlor v. Loewe, 209 Fed. 719 (C. C. A.—Conn.); Lawlor v. Loewe, 235 U. S. 522, 59 L. ed. 341, 35 S. Ct. 170. See Hill v. Eagle Co., 219 Fed. 719 (C. C. A.—W. Va.).

If an agreement not to work on non-union trim enforceable by fine is unlawful, defendants are liable for anything done to carry it out even if they did not participate, for the agreement is part of the organic law of the association. Irving v. Neal, 209 Fed. 471, 576 (D. C.—N. Y.).
