v. Maret, 45 Tex. Civ. App. 593, 101 S. W. 278.

Upon a written promise to pay money "to the treasurer of" an unincorporated society, the members of the society, not the treasurer, are the proper
parties plaintiff. Ewing v. Medlock, 5 Porter 82 (Ala.).

An unincorporated company borrowed money and took a note to the order
of "the Captain of Company A." At the time of suit there was no captain
and by vote of the company the note was transferred to plaintiff to hold and
collect for the company. Held: This gave him sufficient interest to maintain
the bill. (Query, if bill in equity.) Waugh v. Andel, 21 Ill. App. 389.

The treasurer of an unincorporated church may sue in his own name on subscriptions made to him as treasurer for building a church if in pursuance of the subscription he had commenced the construction. The words "as treasurer," etc., the association having no corporate existence, may be regarded as surplusage. McDonald v. Gray, 11 Ia. 508.

Statute required a society to file certificate with clerk of election of trustees. Held: This is not a condition precedent to action by trustees on claim of the society. Hence, society cannot sue again claiming former suit invalid. Roberts v. Hill, 137 Ind. 215, 36 N. E. 843.

§70) NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS (Chap. V

of the second part where the defendant is only a party of the second part and the contract is expressly several.

By statute in several States unincorporated associations, like corporations, may sue and be sued in their association names.** Such a statute is constitutional if it does not take away the right to sue individual members.* In some cases such a statute has been held to apply only to associations for profit. Other statutes have been held to include non-profit associations.* In several of the States the constitution or statutes provide that the word "corporation" shall include all joint stock companies or associations having powers or privileges not possessed by individuals or partnerships. It has been said that since a benefit society by statute has privileges, an unincorporated trade union could probably maintain an action in the name of the

W. 970; The Minn. statute relates to associates in business, but applies to a trade union having a separate mutual benefit department; Taylor v. Order of Ry. Conductors, 89 Minn. 222, 94 N. W. 684; Also to a union which provides a union printers' home, an old age pension fund and death benefits. Fitzpatrick v. International Union, 184 N. W. 17 (Minn.); Bruns v. Milk, etc. Union, 242 S. W. 419 (Mo. App.); Saunders v. Adams Express Co., 71 N. J. L. 270, 57 Atl. 899, 58 Atl. 1101; King v. Nichols, 2 Ohio Dec. But see Gieske v. Anderson, 77 Cal. 247, 248, 19 Pac. 421, where a treasurer of an association was allowed to sue a former treasurer for funds he had retained.

By statute in Massachusetts an unincorporated church is entitled to receive and to sue for gifts and donations, so that an information in the name of the Attorney General is not an appropriate remedy for breach of trust with reference to its trust funds. W. 506 (Tex. Civ. App.).

### --- Page 0488 ---

Chap. V) ACTIONS BY AND AGAINST [§70

association. The statute does not take away the right of action at common law against the individual members, or the right to a representative action in equity, or an action by the members. In proceeding under the statute, however, the association is the only proper defendant. A judgment in an action under the statute can be enforced against the property of the association. In some States the statute expressly limits the levy of execution on a judgment under it to property of the association. Some States have held that such a statute permits an action at law between the association and a member. Under the Michigan Statute it was held that an action in the name of the association could be maintained to recover for services of members in the plaintiff band on a contract with the association, though a number of the members had later withdrawn from the band and repudiated the action. Under the New York statute an association of over seven members may sue and be sued in the name of its president “ or treasurer, but not both.” In actions under this statute it must be alleged and proved that all the members are liable jointly or severally.5! With the possible

By statute in New York creditors of a joint stock association must first sue the president and collect out of the assets of the association. If they cannot collect, then they may sue individual members of association. The recovery in the first suit is not made conclusive in the second. The association here was liable as undisclosed principal for the acts of its superintendent. But since undisclosed, the liability did not continue after it had transferred its assets to a corporation. Like dormant partners, not bound to give notice of termination of authority. If it had been known as principal, notice of termination of authority would have been necessary. Allen v. Clark, 65 Barb. (N. Y.) 563, 574, 575.

Statute authorized action by president of voluntary association regardless of whether members of association reside or transact their business in the State. Clancy v. Terhune, 1 N. Y. City Ct. 239. See Coombe v. Harford, 99 Me. 426, 433, 59 Atl. 529.

“As between an individual member on the one hand and the association in its official capacity on the other, the association is to be regarded as a quasi corporation capable of being sued by such member for any breach of its official obligations. The proper description of a party is "H. J. as treasurer of the A club." Apart from these statutes an association in New York cannot sue.*

Where a suit in equity is brought against an association, certain of whose members have been made parties, it was held proper to enter judgment for damages and costs against the association as well as the individuals. Apart from statute, litigation between an association and a member should be in equity rather than at law. A statute providing for service of process on an agent of an unincorporated association does not deprive members of their rights without due process of law.

In a suit to define the jurisdiction of the Federal Trade Commission the court said: "A voluntary association, having many members, may be brought into court by service on its officers and such of its members as are known and can be conveniently reached, sufficient being served to represent

sent all the diverse interests.” Whether this was meant as an application of the doctrine of representative suits in equity or an extension of the doctrine of the Coronado case is not clear.** Rights of action may be barred by the period of limitation.”

The application to associations of the rules of evidence is usually uninteresting,” but in cases of conspiracy by trade unions has occupied the highest court. An injunction was sought against union miners seeking to unionize a non-union mine whose men were employed on condition that they could not work in the mine while they belonged to the union. Declarations were held admissible to show the illegality of the combination, the fact of the combination being shown otherwise. “When any number of persons associate themselves together in the prosecution of a common plan or enterprise, lawful or unlawful, from the very act of association there arises a kind of partnership, each member being constituted the agent of all, so that the act or declaration of one, in furtherance of the common object, is the act of all, and is admissible as primary and original evidence against them.”

The dissenting opinion took the ground that an agreement to join was not joining and so the contract of the men with the plaintiff was not broken; that the effort to unionize the plaintiff’s mine was a lawful purpose and persuasion to leave the employ was not unlawful.*! Pleas of guilty by some defendants in a conspiracy case were held admissible

Opinion of officers as to meaning of by-laws inadmissible when free from ambiguity. Brendon v. Worley, 28 N. Y. 8. 557, 8 Misc. 253.

Under by-laws no dissolution of association should occur so long as sixty voted to maintain it. In an action arising out of an attempt of the majority to consolidate, evidence of members voting against consolidation should have been admitted. Rosenthal v. Reinfeld, 96 N. Y.S. 199, 48 Misc. 652.

Testimony of a witness as to the number of members of a congregation that desired affiliation with a certain church organization held improperly excluded. Dochkus v. Lithuanian Soc., 206 Pa. St. 25, 30, 55 Atl. 779.

against others who had encouraged and applauded their acts.

The books of the association are admissible evidence to prove its action.* Words written by the secretary of a parish in the minutes of the meeting about remarks made by the plaintiff were held not libelous.* The "attendance" card of a trade union is admissible to prove attendance at a meeting. When the defendant failed to produce under subpena its constitution and by-laws, oral evidence as to the manner of its organization and its membership were admitted. And where there is no formal record on a subject, the acts of the association may be proved orally like the acts of an individual. In a suit to enjoin expulsion, oral evidence to contradict the records of a trade union was admitted.**

On the issue whether a certain book was a church record or a private memorandum of the pastor, the court said: "It appears that during the whole time it was kept, Dr. Puffer was the minister of the parish and pastor of the church, that the book was kept wholly or principally by him, and that the pastor is the proper officer to keep such a record. On inspection, it appears to be a regular statement, in proper form for a record, of the admission of members, the choice of officers, and the transaction of the ordinary business of the church. We must take notice of a usage so general as tracts were made by an association of which defendant was president and member of the executive committee which managed its affairs. Plaintiff contended that the contracts proved criminal conspiracy. Held: These facts tended to show that defendant was or is concerned in the commission of the offence and so discovery should not be granted. Kellogg v. Sowerby, 66 N. Y. S. 542. Information for criminal anarchy under statute, charging he sought to overthrow the government. He was member of I. W. W. Written propaganda of that organization, some of it taken from his person, was admitted as declarations of conspirators in furtherance of unlawful purpose. State v. Lowery, 104 Wash. 520, 177 Pac. 355.

§70] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS (Chap. V

that of a church to keep a record. It is also to be considered, that the law recognizes the existence and organization of a church as an aggregate body, takes notice of its acts and doings and annexes thereto various civil rights and powers. It is in virtue of this organization and these proceedings that deacons are elected; and being thus elected, they are empowered and qualified by the law to sue as a corporation. The law therefore does, by necessary implication, authorize and require a church, by a proper officer, to keep some record of its acts. The book was held a church record. Where all but one of the officers of a trade union were non-residents, it was said that the court would not have had jurisdiction to compel them to obey a judgment requiring them to grant an appeal within the order from a decision to expel the plaintiff. A challenge of jurors because they were members of a religious association affiliated with the same denomination as the defendant association was rejected.

§ 71. Governmental Regulation of Non-profit Associations

The increasing interference of governments in affairs that once were deemed private makes it important to consider the extent to which State and federal legislation and regulation can affect non-profit associations. The problem involves the same principles already considered in the sections devoted to governmental control of associations for profit and trusts. It usually arises directly or indirectly from taxation. A membership in the New York Stock Exchange has been held taxable as property at the domicile of the member in Ohio, on the ground that it is personalty whether the association owns real or personal property and that intangible personalty such as a contractual right may be taxed at the domicile of the owner. A statute authorizing

See § 35.
See § 47a.
3 Anderson v. Durr, 100 Ohio 251, 126 N. E. 57. See Citizens Nat. Bank v. Durr, 257 U. S. 99.

<br>

### --- Page 0494 ---

Chap. V] ACTIONS BY AND AGAINST [§ 70

action against an association in its association name makes it an entity and contemplates that it shall have a residence somewhere which must be the general headquarters of its governing officers.

As we have seen many non-profit associations have such intimate connection with business enterprises that in legislative classification it is easy to confuse them with associations for profit. Courts have properly gone far to sustain classifications made by the legislature in enacting police and revenue legislation. The Supreme Court of the United States held that a State tax on memberships in an incorporated Chamber of Commerce is not invalid because "the Associated Press, lodges, fraternal orders, churches etc.," are exempt. "The description itself suggests manifest distinctions which the State is entitled to observe in its taxing policy despite the general allegation that those associations stand in a similar position. The State has a broad discretion as to tax exemptions."* A statute regulating insurance orders, exempting those under a certain membership, was a constitutional classification. Because a benefit society has privileges by statute, it has been said that a trade union probably is included under the term "corporation" which by statute was defined to include "all joint stock companies or associations having any powers or privileges not possessed by individuals or partnerships", to the extent at least that it could sue in its own name. If this doctrine is sound, its vast possibilities in the regulation of trade unions which decline to incorporate are manifest. It is to be noticed, however, that the doctrine related to interpretation of a statute and that no constitutional question was involved.

Perhaps the most important question is whether a classification discriminating between individuals and unincorporated associations is valid. The only case noted which squarely decides it arose out of an effort by the Florida

' Brotherhood v. Cook, 221 S.W. 1049 (Tex. Civ. App.).
® Rogers v. Hennepin County, 240 U.S. 184, 191, 60 L. ed. 594, 36 S. Ct. 265.
* Proctor v. Huffman, 193 Ala. 216, 68 So. 969.
? Wiehtuechter v. Miller, 276 Mo. 322, 208 S.W. 39.

<br>

### --- Page 0495 ---

$70] NON-PROFIT ASSOCIATIONS [ Cur. V

legislature to enforce prohibition by forbidding sales of liquor in so-called "clubs." The court held that the statute was not unconstitutional because it applied to clubs or associations and not individuals, because the legislature has a wide discretion in classifying subjects of police regulation. It was also suggested to be a reasonable classification because people drink more in such places than at home.®

A statute providing for service of process on an agent of an unincorporated association does not deprive its members of their rights without due process of law.® The legislature may provide that a town officer created by the legislature shall be elected by an unincorporated Board of Fire Underwriters some of whom are not citizens of the town or even of the United States.° A statute exempting trade unions, associations of employers or members of them from liability for tortious acts committed by or on behalf of a trade union or association of employers would violate the equality clauses of the Federal and State constitutions."

In declaring invalid a statute forbidding imposing as a condition of employment non-membership in trade unions, Justice Pitney said, "The Act, as the construction given to it by the State Court shows, is intended to deprive employers of a part of their liberty of contract to the corresponding advantage of the employed and the upbuilding of the labor organizations. But no attempt is made or could reasonably be made, to sustain the purpose to strengthen these voluntary organizations, any more than other voluntary associations of persons, as a legitimate object for the exercise of the police power. They are not public institutions, charged by law with public or governmental duties such as would render the maintenance of their membership a matter of direct concern to the general welfare. If they were, a different question would be presented."

* Van Pelt v. Hilliard, 75 Fla. 792, 78 So., 693.

* Appeal of Baylor, 93 S. C., 414, 77 S. E. 59.

w Re Bulger, 45 Cal. 553.

{1 Opinion of the Justices, 211 Mass. 618.
12 Coppage v. Kansas, 236 U. S. 1, 16, 59 L. ed. 441, 358. Ct. 240.

<br>

### --- Page 0496 ---

APPENDIX OF FORMS

Business Trusts With Transferable Shares

EDITORIAL NOTE

The most striking aspect of business in the last century was the growth of co-operative enterprise. With this combination of the capital of many individuals has also come the conception of limitation of individual liability to the amount of capital put at the risk of the enterprise. When this principle was first applied the only agency which the common law afforded for its development was the corporation, a conception of an artificial personality created by grant of the sovereign. This fiction of the law had originated in the corporation sole of the church and in municipal organization. The earliest co-operative business organizations, such as the East India Company, had been overgrown partnerships, applying to the company on a large scale the only rules of law relating to commercial organization with which the courts were then familiar; namely, that law of partnership which had been developed in the eighteenth century from the law merchant of continental Europe. The characteristic of the partnership is unlimited individual liability.

The corporation, which has been adopted as the vehicle for modern co-operative finance, is appropriate for public or quasi public enterprises; that is, enterprises that are public either from the nature of the service, as, for example, a business involving the use of a public franchise, or from the size or scope of a business which results in a virtual monopoly. Such enterprises are appropriately regulated by the States which create or permit their existence, and it was in enterprises of this nature that the corporation was originally employed for other than religious or municipal purposes. The last generation, however, saw the corporation adopted for small and private undertakings. This occurred because of the natural desire of business men to limit the risk of the business to the capital appropriated by them to it. To avoid the evils of special legislation general corporation laws had previously been passed by our legislatures, and these enabled the speedy adoption of the corporate form for purely private and often individual business. In theory, artificial personality was conferred by the sovereign. In fact, limited liability was established.

<br>

### --- Page 0497 ---

APPENDIX OF FORMS

lished by private individuals merely by public declaration of their intention.

The growth of large corporations produced abuses which resulted in corrective legislation. We have just been passing through a period, not yet ended, of strict regulation of corporations. We are now entering upon a period of severe taxation of corporations, due to a change of theory as to the best method of passing on to the public the increasing cost of government. Instead of collecting revenue indirectly through a tariff, we are collecting it indirectly through the corporations. Regulation and taxation must apply to private as well as public corporations, big as well as little, for an arithmetical classification of corporations subject to regulation or taxation would be too easily evaded by those for whom the rules and the taxes are really meant. The result of all this, however, is that private and individual enterprises are subjected to embarrassing restrictions and increasing burdens which are appropriate only for quasi public business concerns.

Business men are naturally casting about for a different method of commercial organization. One alternative is to attain limitation of liability by agreement, express or implied, between the enterprise and its creditors. The time may come when the courts will imply such an agreement from the nature of the dealings. Why should not limitation of liability to capital put at the risk of the business be a normal, rather than an abnormal, theory of business? In fact, through the artificial device of corporations almost universally it has become the usual method of doing business. Business men extending credit have become accustomed to eliminate the idea of individual credit except when the individual name is that under which the business is conducted. "The Boston Company" or "The Boston Trust" at once suggests limited liability. When once this is recognized, the courts will doubtless declare that limitation of liability by agreement is implied in dealings with such an organization. That time has not yet come, or we cannot be sure that it has, which for the purposes of counsel amounts to the same thing.

The multiplication of corporations since 1850 overshadowed a different development from the large partnership. Associations were formed, which, to avoid uncertainties in the application of the law of property, vested their assets in trustees who issued transferable certificates representing the capital contributions of the investors. These trusts became common in Massachusetts for the purpose of real estate development because of restrictions on the real estate holdings of corporations. A crude form of the same type of organization was adopted for certain early attempts at monopoly in Ohio and New York, which soon attached a connotation

<br>

### --- Page 0498 ---

APPENDIX OF FORMS

tion of opprobrium to the name "trust," which is still confusing. This type was then generally abandoned, except in Massachusetts, where its application was broadened from real estate development to holding companies, and even to ordinary manufacturing enterprises. The great desideratum of limited liability was sought by various devices, some of which have proved illusory.

One method, however, is unassailable either in law or in morals. If liability is limited by agreement, express or implied, between the organization and those who extend credit to it, the end is effectively attained and no one can possibly be harmed. In small or closely held concerns this method is often workable. The one danger is that in times of stress a shareholder cannot be sure that the executive of the association will consistently obey his orders to frame his agreements so as to limit liability, and, furthermore, unexpected liabilities are sometimes imposed by force of law to which no limitation by agreement can attach.

There remains one form of organization which eliminates effectively the possibility of personal liability, namely, the trust. At first our judges were not clear just how to distinguish partnership from trust; that is, they were not sure just where to draw the line. Uncertainty for a lawyer advising a client is worse than an adverse decision. Hence lawyers hesitated to adopt the trust form for co-operative enterprise because of this twilight zone between trust and partnership. Later decisions, notably the case of Crocker v. Malley, in the Supreme Court of the United States, have illuminated the subject. It is believed that the State courts will follow it. Rhode Island, Kansas, New York, Wisconsin and Minnesota are already in accord.

An organization can now be formed which will be safely within the line which has been drawn. Although the preparation of the declaration of trust of a modern business trust with transferable shares is a work that should be attempted only by a skilled draftsman, when the investor has assured himself that the papers were prepared under the direction of competent counsel he need consider only a few differences from the corporation with which he is now familiar. In the present state of the decisions it seems essential that the investor or shareholder abandon some of the powers theoretically belonging to the shareholder in a corporation, such as the power of exercising control over the executives of the organization through voting at meetings of stockholders.

Will such a form of organization accomplish the desires of business men, that is, will it be workable? There are two sorts of investors,—those who desire and will exercise supervision over those in executive charge of the enterprise, and small investors who in fact never care to exercise their franchise as stockholders

<br>

### --- Page 0499 ---

APPENDIX OF FORMS

of corporations. The latter will be equally satisfied with a certificate which confers no voting power. The former group are usually personally, or through their intimates, directors of the corporation. It is clear that they will not permanently be content with a form of organization in which they abandon what they have been accustomed to consider the control of the policy of the corporation through their voting power. They will want the power at any time to influence the policy of the organization.

In most instances, however, it will be easy to accomplish this by making them or their representatives trustees of the enterprise. While it has been customary to have a small number of trustees, there is no reason why the number should not be sufficiently large to afford proper representation of all active and large financial interests in the enterprise. If they are thus represented on the board of trustees they will be content with an organization which completely surrenders control from the shareholders to the executives. It is important, however, to make sure that there are genuine beneficiaries who are not also trustees, lest merger result and this makes it impossible for the trust to succeed the "one man" corporation.

A self-perpetuating body of trustees who can be changed only by decree of court for misconduct or neglect, but upon whom the penalties for such misconduct or neglect are at least as great as those imposed upon directors of corporations, should satisfy both classes of investors. The possibility of changing them through a decree of court is practically as good as that which the ordinary minority stockholder has of changing a board of directors of a corporation. The details of the work of the trustees may be delegated to a small executive committee, and under the provisions of the instrument of trust the liability of the trustees may be made several, and not joint.

If the organization in question is clearly a trust, it is equally clear that no individual liability can fall on the beneficiaries for the acts of the trustees. It is easy to provide for the elimination of the liability of individual trustees for their deliberate acts, for self-interest will cause them to stipulate against individual liability and confine liability to the trust estate. Liability imposed involuntarily will be for misconduct, and in that event there is no good reason why the executives themselves should not be individually liable. As directors of corporations they would ordinarily subject themselves to some form of individual liability. They can protect themselves by liability insurance. There is still an element of uncertainty as to the negotiability of bonds and commercial paper issued by business trusts. The subject is very technical and is considered in detail in Section 31. For practical

<br>

### --- Page 0500 ---

APPENDIX OF FORMS

purposes it is believed that the equivalent of negotiability will be held to exist except as between the true owner and a purchaser for value from a thief.

In preparing the forms for a trust with transferable certificates of beneficial interest, counsel will be concerned primarily with the distinction between trust and partnership. Although it is customary to include elaborate stipulations designed to eliminate by agreement all individual liability for debts of the business, investors cannot be certain that these stipulations will be carried out consistently by those in charge of the business. If the organization is a trust and not a partnership, there will be no individual liability of beneficiaries. Since the decision by the Supreme Court of the United States in the case of Crocker v. Malley, following the Massachusetts case of Williams v. Milton, it seems reasonable to expect that the States which have not yet passed upon the question will adopt the distinction between partnership and trust therein set forth. Under these decisions, if the beneficiaries are not “associated” and have not “control” of the trustees, they are not partners as to the business enterprise but merely beneficiaries of the trust. By eliminating all meetings of beneficiaries the element of association is eliminated. By depriving beneficiaries of power to remove or elect trustees or to instruct trustees the element of control is eliminated. Both Williams v. Milton and Crocker v. Malley involved declarations of trust where the power of the trustees to act in certain important contingencies was expressly made conditional on the written consent of all or a specified proportion of the beneficiaries. It was held that this limited right of the beneficiaries did not make them partners. The first essential of the modern declaration of trust, therefore, is the elimination of meetings of beneficiaries and the substitution of separate written consent to certain important acts.

The second essential is to make sure that the trustees and beneficiaries are not identical, otherwise a merger will result. The desire to give representation to all interests on the board of trustees must not be allowed to invade this rule in letter or in spirit. If the courts should sustain recent rulings of the United States Department of Internal Revenue and apply them generally, much greater differentiation between trustees and beneficiaries than is now customary will become necessary. It is believed, however, that these rulings are based on a fundamental misconception. See Sections 14, note 15, 35, note 19, and 37, note 10 supra.

The duration of the trust must be confined within the limits of the rule against perpetuities and of any local statutes invalidating trusts for terms of years.

<br>

### --- Page 0501 ---

APPENDIX OF FORMS

Until these organizations are more familiar and court decisions have defined more fully the laws relating to them, it seems best to avoid the nomenclature of corporations as far as possible. It is better not to have a declared par value for the certificates of beneficial interest. Questions as to the value of assets against which the certificates are issued will thus be avoided. We are already familiar with this principle in corporations with shares without par value. The time and amount of division of income should be left to the discretion of the trustees. There is probably no real objection, however, to preferred shares of various sorts.

The elimination of the personal liability of the trustees is usually required by the trustees. Then arises the problem of an obligation enforceable against the assets of the business but not enforceable against any individual personally. Owing to the doubt as to the validity of such provisions in the absence of an equitable lien on the assets of the trust and the impairment of credit that would result when this situation is fully realized, it is important to make some provision for an equitable lien attaching to all the obligations incurred by the trustees, but so that it shall not interfere with their right to deal with the assets and pass clear title to them in the usual course of business. This requires a provision in substance like that for the floating charge included in the following form. The effect of this elimination of all individual liability on the negotiability of notes and bonds of business trusts has been explained in Section 31.

The power of the trustees must be broadly stated to avoid the hindrance to the despatch of business which would result from a strict application of the rules of law designed for the protection of the beneficiary. Since the trustees are subject personally to liability which no stipulation can avoid, they must protect themselves fully by liability insurance which should be charged as an expense of the business. In order that there may be a clear record of all changes of trustees, there must be some simple and specific way of certifying the resignations and appointments which shall be prima facie evidence of the facts therein recited. In some states it is still necessary for the old trustees to execute a conveyance of title to the new trustees.

Taxes on the issue and transfer of shares must be considered, but it will probably be found that none of these apply to a pure trust as distinguished from an association. The trustees will be taxed like other trustees. Under the Federal Income Tax of 1921 the income tax will be paid by the beneficiaries on their shares of net income actually received. In Massachusetts an agreement may be made with the Tax Commissioner under which the trustees

<br>

### --- Page 0502 ---

APPENDIX OF FORMS

pay an income tax at the rate applicable to business income and the beneficiaries are exempt.

This form of organization is being used in some localities for promotion purposes and the sale of shares to the public is carried on by or on behalf of the trustees. This has elements of danger to the trustees not yet fully appreciated. Since the public is still likely to confuse trust certificates with corporate shares it is believed that trusts are more satisfactory at present for private enterprises which do not need to appeal to the public for funds. If used for promotion purposes, however, it is important to see that the trustees are given express power to do what they intend to do in the way of raising capital. “Blue Sky” laws must also be carefully considered. Most of these are not yet applicable to pure trusts, but the decisions may not always appreciate the distinctions between associations and trusts.

The principles of law involved in the preparation of these instruments are discussed in detail in the appropriate sections of the text of this book. The comprehensive scope of this book has necessarily separated the paragraphs discussing different phases of the subject. It is unwise to attempt a summary here, but for the convenience of counsel who may desire some means of familiarizing themselves quickly with the more important legal problems involved, the annotations to the following form have been prepared. On examining the sections of the book referred to, the reason for including a given provision in the declaration of trust will be apparent.

<br>

### --- Page 0503 ---

THE NORTH COAL MINES

AGREEMENT AND DECLARATION OF TRUST

THIS INDENTURE, made at .........., in the State of ........ on the Twentieth day of ........, in the year 19..,

(hereinafter called the Grantors) of the one part, and .......... (hereinafter called the Trustees) which expression shall extend to and include the Trustees for the time being of these presents, (and the word Trustee shall apply to any one of the said Trustees where the context so admits) of the other part

WHEREAS, certain mineral rights and mineral lands and leases of or options therefor and other property and rights of entry, rights of way, easements and privileges of the Grantors have been respectively transferred and assigned and conveyed to the Trustees by the Grantors, or are intended to be forthwith so transferred with the intent that the same shall be held upon the trusts hereinafter expressed concerning the same to be collectively designated THE NORTH COAL MINES, and

WHEREAS, other property and assets may be hereafter transferred to the Trustees by the Grantors and others with the like intent

NOW THIS INDENTURE WITNESSETH, and it is hereby agreed and declared, that the said Trustees shall hold the said land, leases, options, rights, easements and any other property and assets and the investments for the time being representing the same or any part thereof and the property and effects at any time vested in them for the purposes of these presents (hereinafter called the Trust Estate) in trust to sell and convert into money any real estate at any time comprised in the Trust Estate and to deal with the proceeds thereof and the other property comprised in the trust estate in the manner and with and subject to the powers and provisions hereinafter contained concerning the same for the benefit of the holders for the time being of the certificates hereinafter mentioned (hereinafter called the Beneficiaries) according to the

<br>

### --- Page 0504 ---

THE NORTH COAL MINES

respective interests of the Beneficiaries as evidenced by the said
certificates. But the Trustees shall have power to postpone the
conversion hereinbefore directed of the said real estate or any part
thereof so long as they in their uncontrolled discretion shall think
proper, provided the same shall not be postponed beyond the
time herein limited for the continuance of this trust. And notwithstanding
any postponement of such conversion the said real estate
shall be considered as personal estate for the purposes of enjoyment
and transmission and the rents and income thereof shall be applied
as if they were the income of the proceeds of such conversion and
the real and personal property at any time held by the Trustees
shall constitute one single fund.

The TRUSTEES

1. The Trustees shall have power at any time or times and from
time to time:

(a) To explore, develop, excavate, quarry, mine, refine, manufacture, prepare for market, buy, sell, deal in, transport and ship
coal and other minerals and metals;

the time being subject to any of the trusts hereof and to erect, pull
down, alter, repair and construct buildings and fences thereon and
to make such outlay of money as they shall think proper for the
said purposes and to insure buildings and other improvements or
the rents or rental value thereof or standing timber against loss
or damage by fire or other casualty and to insure against liability
for damage to person or property arising from anything happening
upon the said lands or on account of the condition thereof or on
account of the use, manufacture, sale or other disposal of the

(c) To purchase, take on lease or otherwise acquire any lands
or hereditaments useful in connection with the business of the
trust or which may be taken as security for or in payment of
debts due the trust estate, and to manage and improve all lands
and hereditaments for the time being subject to any of the trusts
hereof, to excavate, quarry, mine and prepare for market the
products thereof, and to erect, pull down, alter, repair and construct buildings and fences thereon and to make such outlay
of money as they shall think proper for the said purposes and

3 See §21, n. 44.

### --- Page 0505 ---

APPENDIX OF FORMS

to insure buildings and other improvements or the rents or rental value thereof, or standing timber against loss or damage by fire or other casualty and to insure against liability for damage to person or property arising from anything happening upon the said lands or on account of the condition thereof or on account of the use, manufacture, sale or other disposal of the minerals upon the said lands, or arising in connection with any process or work of quarrying, mining, manufacturing or transporting the said minerals or any of the products arising from the manufacture thereof;

(d) To grant or create any easement or other right or privilege over or in relation to any lands or hereditaments for the time being subject to any of the trusts hereof and to purchase or acquire any easement or other right or privilege over or respecting other lands to be annexed in enjoyment to any lands or other property at the time subject to any of the trusts hereof and to release any easement or restrictive covenant, agreement or provision over or affecting any other land for such consideration and upon such terms as the Trustees shall think fit;

(e) To grant leases at will or for any term of years in possession or reversion of any lands or other property for the time being subject to the trusts hereof and to make allowances to and arrangements with tenants and others and to accept surrenders of leases and tenancies;

(f) With the consent of beneficiaries owning three-fourths of the beneficial interest in the Trust Estate in the manner provided in paragraph numbered twenty-seven hereinafter, to raise or secure for the purposes of the trusts of these presents such sums of money as they shall think proper and to mortgage the whole or any part or parts of the Trust Estate in such manner and on such terms as they shall think proper for securing the repayment of any moneys so raised or secured and to pay off or enlarge the time for paying off any mortgage for the time being existing or to make any new mortgage for any sum or sums so secured;

(g) To sell the whole or any part of the Trust Estate in whatever state of investment the same may be;

(h) To invest or deal with the moneys forming part of the Trust Estate upon such securities or in such manner as may from time to time be determined by them;

(i) To insure themselves or the Trust Estate against any loss, casualty, liability, or claim, and to insure the lives of any or all of the trustees or of any employees, either for the benefit of the Trust Estate or, with the consent in writing of beneficiaries owning two-thirds in interest in the Trust Estate, for the benefit of the

See § 14, nn. 2, and 14.

<br>

### --- Page 0506 ---

THE NORTH COAL MINES

individuals insured or such beneficiaries as the insured may lawfully designate, and to pay all premiums and charges therefor out of the Trust Estate;

(j) To assume and agree to pay and to pay out of the Trust Estate any taxes assessed against the income received therefrom by the beneficiaries hereof;

(k) To carry on the business of a storekeeper in all its branches and in particular to buy, sell, manufacture and deal in goods, stores, consumable articles, chattels and effects of all kinds, both wholesale and retail, and to transact every kind of agency business and generally to engage in any business or transaction which may seem to the Trustees directly or indirectly conducive to the interests or convenience of the employees of the Trustees, and to make arrangements with persons engaged in any trade, business or profession for concessions to such employees of any special rights, privileges and advantages, and in particular in regard to the supply of goods;

(l) To settle all accounts and to compromise, compound, abandon or adjust by arbitration or otherwise any action, suits, proceedings, disputes, taxes, claims, demands and things relating to the Trust Estate and to transfer to or deposit with any incorporated bank or trust company or other persons any shares or securities forming part of the Trust Estate for the purposes of any arrangement for enforcing or protecting the interests of the Trustees or the owners of such shares or securities and to give time, with or without security, for the payment or delivery of any debts or property and to execute and enter into releases, agreements and other instruments and do all other things proper for any such purpose and to pay or satisfy any debts or claims upon any evidence that they shall think sufficient;

(m) To enter into arrangements expressed in writing for any of the purposes hereinbefore mentioned that the payment of any sum of money or the performance of anything that shall have been agreed upon shall be a floating charge * upon the real and personal property for the time being subject to the trusts of these presents in such manner that the Trustees notwithstanding such charge may continue to deal with the said real and personal property by way of sale, mortgage, charge, lease or otherwise, or paying out to the Beneficiaries hereof any sums or amounts of the profits or other income thereof in accordance with the trusts hereof and that the Trustees shall not be liable for the payment of any such sum of money or for damages for non-performance of anything that shall have been agreed upon except out of the trust estate, and so far as the same shall be sufficient for that

6 See § 31 n. 4 and 11 et seq.

<br>

### --- Page 0507 ---

- APPENDIX OF FORMS

purpose, and that no Trustee shall be in any way liable in respect thereof after he ceases to be a trustee of these presents, and every such floating charge shall rank equally with every other such floating charge unless otherwise specified in the instrument creating the same;

(n) Generally in all matters not hereinbefore specified to deal with the Trust Estate and to manage and conduct the trusts hereof in any manner that they shall deem for the interest of the Beneficiaries as fully as if the Trustees were the absolute owners of the trust premises. And to execute and do all such agreements, deeds, instruments, and things as they shall think proper for executing any of the powers or trusts contained in these presents, and to authorize the execution of any such agreements, deeds or instruments in their names by any of their officers or other persons;

(o) The Trustees may from time to time for the transaction of the business of these trusts or the management, repair or insurance of any part of the Trust Estate, or for seeing to the propriety and sufficiency of any acts of their agents, appoint, remove, reappoint or employ such agents, officers and employees as they may think best and may define their duties and fix their compensation without the Trustees being answerable for the acts and defaults of such agents, officers and employees. The Trustees out of the trust property and estate may pay themselves as compensation not to exceed at any time one per cent. of the gross income arising from the trust property, premises and estate held, and the business transacted and done subject to the trusts of these presents.*

2. The Trustees shall not be obliged to incur any personal liability7 in the execution of any of the trusts or powers herein contained and shall not have any power or authority to borrow money or incur any liability on the credit of or on behalf of the Beneficiaries or to make any contract binding or impose any liability upon the Beneficiaries personally® and shall not be entitled to look to the Beneficiaries personally for indemnity against any liability incurred by the Trustees or any of them. But if the Trustees or any of them shall give their or his personal security for the payment of any money or otherwise incur any personal liability in respect of the premises such Trustees or Trustee shall be entitled to indemnity out of the property for the time

### --- Page 0508 ---

THE NORTH COAL MINES

being comprised in the Trust Estate against any and all liability arising out of the same. In every written order, contract or obligation which the Trustees by themselves or by any officer or agent shall give or enter into, it shall be the duty of the Trustees to refer to this declaration of trust and to stipulate that neither the Trustees nor the Beneficiaries shall be held to any personal liability under or by reason of said order, contract or obligation, but that such obligation shall be enforceable against the trust property as herein provided and every agreement entered into hereunder shall, without any express provision to that effect, operate as an agreement with the Trustees for such a floating charge as is hereinbefore defined. For the purpose of protecting themselves against any personal liability hereunder they are expressly authorized as Trustees to enter into such contracts of insurance as they may deem advisable.

3. All real estate at any time vested in the Trustees for any of the purposes of these presents shall be held by them upon the like trust for sale and conversion and the like powers and provisions in respect of postponement thereof and the application of the rents and income of the same as are herein contained concerning the real estate or rights therein or thereto heretofore granted or assigned or forthwith to be granted or assigned to the Trustees by the Grantors.

4. In making any sale herein authorized the Trustees may sell by public auction or private contract and buy in at any sale by auction or rescind or vary any contract of sale or resell without being answerable for loss and for said purposes the Trustees may do and execute all proper deeds, instruments and things.

5. When any trustee is temporarily incapable or unfit to act in the said trusts the other trustee or trustees for the time being of these presents may exercise the powers and authorities hereby given to the Trustees. And any trustee absent from, or contemplating such absence may by power of attorney or otherwise authorize and empower any other trustee so to act in his behalf for a period not exceeding six months at any one time and to use his name for execution and signature of checks and other negotiable instruments and documents for the purposes of the said trusts without being responsible for loss.

6. Any trustee may resign and be discharged from the said trusts by delivering his resignation thereof in writing signed and acknowledged as required in the case of a deed of conveyance of land to any other trustee for the time being and such resignation shall be effectual and complete only upon the appointment of a new trustee in his place and meanwhile he shall continue to act as such trustee.

7. If any trustee for the time being of these presents shall die or resign or shall be declared by a court of competent jurisdiction to be incapable or unfit to act in the said trusts, the surviving or continuing trustees or trustee shall forthwith mail notice thereof, postage prepaid, to each beneficiary at his registered address and it shall be lawful for the surviving or continuing trustees or trustee for the time being (and for this purpose any retiring trustee shall, if willing to act in the exercise of this power, be considered a continuing trustee) with the consent of Beneficiaries owning three-fourths of the beneficial interest in the trust estate, in the manner provided in paragraph numbered twenty-seven hereinafter, * to appoint by writing a new trustee or trustees in place of the trustee or trustees so dying, resigning, or becoming incapable or unfit to act as aforesaid.

8. Upon any change of the Trustees hereunder such instruments shall be executed, acknowledged, delivered and recorded by the retiring and incoming trustees or by the heirs, or personal representatives of any deceased trustee, as shall be necessary or convenient for vesting the Trust Estate or any part thereof in the Trustees or trustee for the time being or for providing evidence of such vesting."

9. The receipts of the Trustees or either or any of them or their agents in that behalf, for moneys or things paid or delivered to them or him shall be effectual discharges to the person paying or delivering the same therefrom and from all liability to see to the application thereof. No purchaser or person dealing with the Trustees shall be bound to ascertain or inquire whether any assent of the Beneficiaries has been obtained in connection with any act or transaction of the Trustees or as to the existence or occurrence of any event or purpose in or for which a sale, mortgage, pledge or charge is herein authorized or directed or otherwise as to the purpose or regularity of any of the acts of the Trustees purporting to be done in pursuance of any of the powers or provisions herein contained or as to the regularity of the discharge, resignation or appointment of any trustee. And a transfer of the trust estate or any part thereof executed by the Trustee or Trustees.

See § 14 nn. 2 and 14.

37 See § 47 n. 15 et seq.

THE NORTH COAL MINES

Trustees in whom the same shall be vested at the time of any such discharge, resignation or appointment for the purpose of vesting the same in the Trustees for the time being of these presents or providing evidence of such vesting independently of such discharge, resignation or appointment ® shall as to the property comprised in such transfer be conclusive evidence in favor of any such purchaser or other person dealing with the Trustees of the matters therein recited relating to such discharge, resignation or appointment or the occasion thereof or the occasion of such transfer. And no purchaser or person dealing with any Trustee purporting to act during the absence, unfitness or incapacity of any other Trustee or under any delegation of authority from any other Trustee shall be concerned to ascertain or inquire whether an occasion exists in which he is authorized so to act or in which such delegation is permitted or whether such delegated authority is still subsisting.

10. The Trustees shall not be liable for anything done or omitted by them in good faith and shall be answerable and accountable only for their own acts, receipts, neglects and defaults respectively and not for those of each other or any agent employed by them nor for any bank, trust company, broker or other person with whom or into whose hands any moneys or securities may be deposited or come, nor for any defect in title of any property or securities acquired, nor for any loss unless it shall happen through their own default respectively. And no Trustee however appointed shall be obliged to give any bond or surety or other security for the performance of any of his duties in the said trusts.

11. The Trustees or any of them in their individual capacity or in any other fiduciary capacity acting alone or in conjunction with others may be beneficiaries hereunder. 7°

Duration of the Trusts

12. The Trusts contained in these presents shall continue in such manner that the Trustees shall have all the powers and discretions expressed to be given to them respectively by these presents and that no beneficiary shall be entitled to put an end to the same or to require a division of the trust estate or any part thereof except as herein provided until the expiration of seventy-five years from the date hereof or until the death of the last survivor *! of the said and such

8 See § 47, n. 15 et seq.

9 See § 44, n. 18 et seq.

® See § 37, n. 9 et seq. and § 14 nn. 15 and 31.

%1 See § 19. Insert in blank the names of the first trustees.

### --- Page 0511 ---

other persons now living as shall hereafter become trustees of these presents before any person or persons not now living shall have become the only Trustee or Trustees thereof or the offices of Trustees thereof shall have become entirely vacant, whichever of the said periods shall first expire, and at the expiration of the time so limited the said trusts shall terminate.

13. Upon the termination of the said trusts by the said limitation or under the provisions hereinafter contained the Trustees shall sell and convert into money the whole of the Trust Estate not theretofore so sold and converted and shall apportion the net proceeds thereof after the payment of all outstanding obligations among the Beneficiaries ratably according to the respective interests of said Beneficiaries as shown by the certificates held by them.

INCOME

14. The Trustees may from time to time set apart out of the net income of the trust estate as and for reserve or depreciation funds and for working capital, such sums, if any, as they may think proper, and shall provide a sufficient reserve to discharge all taxes and charges which are by law required to be paid out of or measured by the said income and payments and to discharge all property and other taxes levied against and assessed against the trust estate, or the Trustees in their official capacity. The said reserve or depreciation fund and working capital shall be applicable during the continuance of the said trusts to any purpose to which money forming part of the capital or income of the Trust Estate may be applied including the payments to the Beneficiaries hereunder.

15. Out of the residue of the net income of the Trust Estate or, in the discretion of the Trustees, of prior income set apart under the last preceding paragraph, the Trustees may make payments to the Beneficiaries according to the proportionate parts of the whole beneficial interest in the Trust Estate held by the several beneficiaries as cestuis que trust and as set out in their respective certificates, and such payments may be made at fixed or irregular times in the discretion of the Trustees. And the Beneficiaries shall have no right to any such payments except when and as made by the Trustees.

16. The Trustees may determine the time of the commencement of the financial year and from time to time change the same. And until they shall change the same a new financial year shall commence on the first day of January in each year.

22 See §19, n. 5.

2 See § 21, n. 29, et seq.

CERTIFICATES

17. The beneficial interest of the cestuis que trust (herein called the Beneficiaries) in the Trust Estate shall be divided into 15,000 equal shares and shall be evidenced by certificates each of which said certificates shall state the number of shares in whole beneficial interest which is held by the holder thereof and the first certificates for the said shares shall be issued to the persons hereafter named as follows:

To said , in return for the property or rights therein or thereto, heretofore transferred or forthwith to be transferred to the Trustees by the Grantors or intended to be so transferred as aforesaid.

18. A register or registers shall be kept by or under the direction of the Trustees which shall contain the names and addresses of the Beneficiaries and the part of the aggregate beneficial interest of each one of them respectively and the particulars of all future transfers thereof. No beneficiary shall be entitled to have any notice or report mailed to him as herein provided until he has given his address to the Trustees to be entered in the said register.

19. Every beneficiary shall be entitled to receive from the Trustees a certificate signed by the Trustees in substantially the form contained in the First Schedule hereto specifying the part of the aggregate beneficial interest to which such beneficiary is entitled and every certificate shall be registered by the Trustees or by some agent appointed or employed by them for that purpose and shall have on it a certificate to the fact of such registration.

20. If any certificate is worn out or defaced the Trustees may, upon surrender thereof for cancellation, issue a new certificate in place thereof. And on evidence satisfactory to the Trustees that any certificate has been lost or destroyed and on such terms, if any, as to indemnity and otherwise as they may deem proper, the Trustees may issue a new certificate in place thereof.

21. The shares in the beneficial interest shall be transferable as follows. Every transfer of the whole or any part of the interest of any beneficiary (otherwise than by operation of law) shall be in writing, signed by the transferor and upon delivery thereof with the existing certificate of the transferor to the Trustees or their transfer agent, such transfer shall be recorded in the register and a new certificate therefor shall be given to the transferee, and in case of a transfer of only a part of the interest of the holder of the original certificate then a new certificate for the residue

<br>

### --- Page 0513 ---

APPENDIX OF FORMS

thereof shall be given to the transferor. Until the transfer shall be so delivered and recorded the transferor shall be deemed to be the holder of the interest comprised therein for all the purposes hereof and the Trustees shall not be affected by any notice of the transfer.

22. Any person becoming entitled to any share or part of the whole beneficial interest in the Trust Estate in consequence of the death, bankruptcy or insolvency of any beneficiary, or in any way other than by a transfer in accordance with the preceding paragraph, shall be recorded in the register as the holder of the said share or part thereof and receive a new certificate for same upon production of the proper evidence thereof and delivery of the existing certificate to the Trustees or their transfer agent.

23. Two or more persons holding any certificate shall be joint tenants of the entire interest represented thereby and no entry shall be made in the register or in any certificate that any person is entitled to any future, limited or contingent interest therein. But any person registered as the holder of any certificate may, subject to the provisions hereinafter contained, be described therein as a trustee of any kind and any words may be added to the description to identify the trust.

24. The Trustees shall not, nor shall the Beneficiaries or any transfer agent or registrar or other agent of the Trustees, be bound to take notice of or be affected by notice of any trust whether express, constructive or implied or any charge or equity to which any of the said certificates or the interests of any of the Beneficiaries in the trusts of these presents may be subject or to ascertain or inquire whether any sale or transfer of any such certificate or interest by such beneficiary or his personal representatives is authorized by such trust, charge or equity or to recognize any person as having any interest therein except the persons registered as such Beneficiaries. And the receipt of the person in whose name any certificate is registered, or if such certificate is registered in the names of more than one person, the receipt of any one of such persons, shall be a sufficient discharge for all payments of income and other moneys payable in respect of such certificate and from all liability to see to the application thereof.

25. The right or interest of any beneficiary in the whole beneficial interest in the Trust Estate shall be personal property * entitling the holder of said certificate only to the rights and interests in the Trust Estate set forth in these presents. No beneficiary is to have any legal title to the Trust Estate or any part thereof, real or personal, held from time to time subject to the trusts of these presents

% See §21, nn. 6, 29, 53.

<br>

### --- Page 0514 ---

THE NORTH COAL MINES

by the Trustees, and especially the Beneficiaries shall have no right to call for any partition and they shall have no equitable interest or estate in the lands, hereditaments or other property constituting the Trust Estate, but their interest shall consist only of an interest in the money to arise from the sale or other disposition thereof and of the rights herein mentioned previously to such sale.

26. An annual report shall be made by the Trustees as soon as the same can be conveniently prepared after the close of each financial year and such report shall contain an account of the assets and liabilities of the Trust Estate and of the receipts and expenditures and income account of the trusts from the foot of the last previous account, if any, and of the development work since the last report in any mine comprised in the trust estate and of the coal estimated to be then in sight therein, and a copy of such report shall be mailed, postage prepaid, to each beneficiary at his registered address. Special reports shall be mailed postage prepaid by the Trustees to the Beneficiaries whenever they think there is any matter of special interest or importance calling for such report.”

27. The Trustees may, with the consent of Beneficiaries owning three-fourths of the beneficial interest in the trust estate, but after notice mailed, postage prepaid, to each beneficiary at his registered address, alter or add to this declaration of trust,” including the right to increase the number of shares representing the beneficial interest in the Trust Estate, or terminate this trust, or convey, transfer or deliver such part of the Trust Estate as shall not theretofore have been converted into money, to new or other trustees or to a corporation, being first duly indemnified against any outstanding obligation or liability. The consent of the Beneficiaries as above referred to shall be contained in a writing or identical writings setting forth specifically and in detail that to which consent is given, signed by the several beneficiaries.” 

The said writing or one of the said writings shall be signed by at least three of the Trustees and recorded in all of the counties wherein is located any real property then owned by the Trustees and subject to the trusts hereof. Such writing or writings shall be conclusive evidence of the existence of all facts and of compliance with all prerequisites necessary to the validity of such alteration, amendment, termination or conveyance, whether stated in the instrument or not, upon all questions as to title or affecting the rights of third persons. No such alteration or addition shall affect the validity of anything previously done by the Trustees hereunder.

APPENDIX OF FORMS

PROCEEDINGS OF THE TRUSTEES

28. The Trustees may meet together for the transaction of business and regulate their meetings as they think proper and they may prescribe the times and places of regular meetings of the Trustees which may be held without any further notice thereof. The Trustees may make, alter and amend by-laws regulating their conduct and meetings and the management of the undertaking not inconsistent with the terms or provisions of these presents.

29. The quorum necessary for the transaction of business at a meeting of the Trustees shall be three Trustees present personally or by proxy of whom at least two shall be present personally. Such quorum shall have full power to exercise all or any of the powers, authorities and discretions for the time being vested in the Trustees.

30. A trustee may from time to time in writing appoint another Trustee as his proxy to vote at any meeting of the Trustees.

31. Questions arising at any meeting of the Trustees shall be decided by the votes of a majority of the Trustees present personally or by proxy and in case of an equality of votes, the chairman of the meeting shall have a second or casting vote.

32. The Trustees may from time to time elect a president, a secretary and a treasurer, who may or may not be elected from among the Trustees and shall have such authority and perform such duties as may from time to time be determined by the Trustees and any two or more of said offices may be held by the same person.

33. The action of the Trustees in respect of any matter may be evidenced by a resolution passed by them at a meeting or by a writing signed by a majority of the Trustees which writing shall be as valid for all purposes as a resolution passed at a meeting of the Trustees.

34. A certificate signed by the president or secretary shall, in favor of the Trustees and all other persons acting in good faith in reliance thereon, be conclusive evidence of the contents of any resolution or action of the Trustees and of all matters in such certificate contained relating to the same or to the regularity thereof and the passage of such resolution or the taking of such action and no person shall be obliged to make any inquiry as to any of the said matters or as to the election or appointment of any person acting as a trustee or be affected by actual or implied notice of any irregularity therein.

See § 33, n. 6, § 46 nn. 8-10.

31 See § 33, n. 17.

<br>

### --- Page 0516 ---

THE NORTH COAL MINES

35. The Trustees shall cause to be kept in books provided for the purpose minutes of all resolutions and proceedings of the Trustees and of the names of the Trustees present at every meeting, specifying whether they were present in person or by proxy. And such minutes if purporting to be signed by the president or secretary or an assistant secretary shall be evidence of the matters therein stated and of the regularity of the meeting and that proper notice of the meeting was given if any was required in favor of the Trustees and all persons acting thereon in good faith of all such matters and things therein stated.

MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS

36. The trusts of these presents may be collectively designated for all the purposes thereof as The North Coal Mines, and under that name, so far as practicable, all business shall be conducted and transacted by the Trustees.

37. In the construction of these presents words in the singular number include the plural number and vice versa, and words denoting males include females and words denoting persons include firms, associations and corporations and other organizations unless a contrary intention is to be inferred from the subject matter or context. And all the trusts, powers and provisions herein contained shall take effect and be construed according to the laws of .

38. The Trustees may adopt a seal, in such form or design as they may think proper, and may use the same for the better authentication or identification of written instruments executed by them.

2. INCREASE OF NUMBER OF TRUSTEES

At any time and from time to time, the Trustees may increase
the number of Trustees. In the event the said number be thus increased, the persons to fill the places thus created, shall be selected
by a vote of at least seven Trustees, and the successors of the
Trustees thus selected, shall be selected, at all times, by a vote of
at least seven of the original Trustees or of the successors of the
said original Trustees. As soon as any Trustee selected to fill the
place created as herein provided shall have accepted the terms of
this trust by an instrument in writing, which shall be deposited
with the said........ Trust Company or its successors, the Trust
Estate shall forthwith vest in the new Trustee or Trustees, together with the continuing Trustees, without any further act or
conveyance, with all the right, title, interest, privileges, powers,
duties and estate of the continuing Trustees to the same extent
as if such newly selected Trustee or Trustees had been the first
and original Trustee or Trustees under this trust.

3. POWER TO TAKE OVER A BUSINESS

The Trustees are authorized:
(a) To acquire and take over all the property and business of
the........ Company, incorporated under the laws of........ as a
going concern and to pay the debts and obligations of the said....

4. PREFERRED AND COMMON SHARES

During the continuance of this trust the beneficial interests therein and thereunder, shall be solely evidenced by certificates for
shares of beneficial interest for which provision is hereinafter made.

Such beneficial interest shares in the first instance, shall be
divided into Five Thousand (5000) preferred shares of the par
value of One Hundred Dollars ($100.00) each, and Twenty Thousand (20,000) common shares of no par value, all of which shares,
both common and preferred, shall be issued forthwith to.........

The preferred shares shall entitle the holder to receive out of the
net income of the Trust Estate, quarterly, cumulative dividends

<br>

### --- Page 0519 ---

APPENDIX OF FORMS

at the rate of seven (7) per cent. per annum, the said dividends to be cumulative from July 1, 19.., and to be paid or set apart before any dividend shall be paid or set apart for the common shares. In case of liquidation of the Trust Estate, the proceeds of the liquidation shall be first applied to the payment to the holders of preferred shares of the sum of One Hundred Dollars ($100.00) per share, and any accrued and unpaid dividends thereon, and the balance remaining thereafter, shall be divided among the holders of the common shares in proportion to their respective holdings.

5. INCREASE OR REDUCTION OF SHARES

For any of the purposes of this trust, the number of shares may, from time to time, be increased or reduced by the Trustees. In case the number of shares is increased, the additional shares shall be issued and disposed of upon such terms and in such manner as the Trustees may determine. The proceeds from the sale of any new shares thus issued and disposed of, shall constitute part of the Trust Estate.

All future preferred beneficial shares or common beneficial shares which may be issued in addition to the 5,000 preferred beneficial shares and 20,000 common beneficial shares herein provided for, may be of such par value or of no par value, as the Trustees may determine, and the Trustees shall have the right to change the par value of shares issued and outstanding and to require the holders thereof to exchange their shares for new shares with the changed par value.

6. REDEMPTION OF PREFERRED SHARES

The preferred shares shall be redeemable at the option of the Trustees, either in whole or in part, upon any date fixed for the payment of dividends, upon the payment of One Hundred and Ten Dollars ($110.00) per share, and all accrued accumulated dividends to the date of such redemption. In the event of the redemption of less than the entire issue, the shares to be redeemed shall be selected by lot, in the manner provided by the Trustees. The Trustees shall give to the holders of shares to be redeemed, sixty days’ notice by registered mail at their addresses, as they appear on the books of the said Trustees, of such proposed redemption. After the expiration of the time fixed for such redemption, the preferred shares so called for redemption may be declared canceled, whether actually surrendered or not, and thereupon the preference of such shares shall absolutely cease and determine and dividends on such shares cease to accrue.

FORM OF LIMITATION OF LIABILITY IN LEASE

Provided always, and it is hereby declared and agreed, that any claim for debt or damages under the reservations of rent and the covenants on the part of the lessees herein contained or any of them shall be a charge upon and shall be enforceable against the property and effects subject to the trusts of the said indenture at the time of proceedings to enforce the same, but no such claim for debt or damages nor any other claim under the stipulations herein contained shall be enforceable against any trustees or trustee for the time being of said indenture personally, and neither the trustees for the time being nor the shareholders under said indenture of trust shall be held to any personal liability under or by reason of any of the stipulations herein contained. But this provision shall not prevent the lessor from obtaining and enforcing such decree in equity against the trustees for the time being as may be necessary or appropriate to compel the application of the property and effects then in their hands as trustees to the performance of the stipulations of this lease or to prevent the trustees for the time being from violating the stipulations herein contained, and this provision shall not prevent the lessor from maintaining such proceedings as may be necessary or appropriate to obtain possession of the demised premises in case of a violation of the provisions hereof; nor shall this provision otherwise interfere with the force or effect of any of the said covenants or affect any other right or remedy of the lessor in respect of any default in the performance or observance thereof, and this provision shall not exempt any such trustees from personal liability in respect of any trusts herein contained regarding the application of any insurance money or of any moneys paid to them out of damages received for such taking as is hereinbefore mentioned (but so that such personal liability of any trustee shall exist only as to breaches of trust which occur while he is trustee) and, fur

of this bond shall thereupon become due and payable.

This bond is one of a series of bonds of the promisor issued
or to be issued for the aggregate principal sum of not exceeding
$1,000,000 under and in pursuance of and are all equally secured
by a collateral trust indenture dated the 5th day of June 1905
whereby all shares, bonds and other securities of street railway
corporations and other property now owned or hereafter to be
acquired by the........ Electric Companies are pledged to the
Trust Company as Trustee for the security and payment
of the said bonds to which indenture reference is hereby made for
a statement of the property pledged, the nature and extent of
the security, the rights of the holders of the said bonds in the
said security and the terms upon which the said bonds are
secured.

This bond is redeemable at par and accrued interest on any
interest payment day at the option of the undersigned by giving
notice by publication for six successive weeks in a newspaper of
general circulation in the city of Boston which notice shall state
the time and place of such payment. Interest on this bond shall
cease if this bond is not presented for payment in accordance
with such notice.

The contract evidenced by this bond binds only the funds
and property held in trust by the........ Electric Companies
and neither any Trustee or Director of the said companies nor
the holder of any of its certificates for shares shall be held to any
personal liability under or by reason of any of the provisions

<br>

### --- Page 0525 ---

APPENDIX OF FORMS

hereof, such liability if any, being expressly waived by the acceptance of this bond.

This bond shall not become obligatory until it shall have been authenticated by the certificate of the Trustee under the said * indenture hereon endorsed.

Electric Companies has caused these presents to be signed in its behalf by the President and Secretary of its Board of Directors duly authorized and the coupons hereto attached to be authenticated by the facsimile signature of its Treasurer as of the 1st day of June 1905.
